Mastering this classification is a high-weightage KTU Module 1 expected learning outcome (mapped to **CO1** under the KTU 2024 OBE framework). ## 1.2 The Seven Official AM Process Categories | # | Process Family | Feedstock State | Energy Source | Material Type | |---|----------------|-----------------|---------------|---------------| | 1 | Vat Photopolymerization | Liquid (Photopolymer Resin) | UV Light | Polymers, Ceramics | | 2 | Material Extrusion | Filament / Paste | Thermal / Mechanical | Thermoplastics, Food | | 3 | Material Jetting | Liquid Droplets | UV / Thermal | Photopolymers, Waxes | | 4 | Binder Jetting | Powder + Liquid Binder | Thermal (post-cure) | Metals, Sand, Ceramics | | 5 | Powder Bed Fusion | Powder | Laser / Electron Beam | Polymers, Metals, Ceramics | | 6 | Directed Energy Deposition | Powder / Wire | Laser / Plasma / E-Beam | Metals (mostly) | | 7 | Sheet Lamination | Sheets / Foils | Laser / Ultrasonic / Thermal | Paper, Metal, Polymer | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Layer Cake" Model > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: Building a Cake by Horizontal Slicing** > > Imagine you want to make a complex 3D birthday cake. Instead of carving it from a giant block (subtractive), you **slice the cake horizontally into hundreds of thin 2D cross-sections from a CAD model**, and then **deposit each slice one on top of the other**, fusing it before the next one is added. Each AM category simply differs in **what ingredient** (resin, powder, filament, sheet) and **what "torch"** (laser, UV light, extruder, electron beam) is used to fuse that layer. - **Layer thickness** typically ranges from **0.02 mm to 0.5 mm** in most commercial systems. - The **build envelope** (3D space) is constrained by machine architecture, with industrial systems reaching **800 mm × 400 mm × 500 mm** or larger. - Classification matters because it dictates **surface finish, accuracy, material compatibility, build speed, and cost per part**. ## 1.4 Why Classification Matters in Engineering Practice > [!TIP] > Choosing the correct AM process family affects four critical engineering parameters: > 1. **Dimensional Accuracy** — ranges from $\pm 0.05\,\text{mm}$ (SLA) to $\pm 0.3\,\text{mm}$ (DED). > 2. **Surface Roughness ($R_a$)** — varies from **$0.5\,\mu\text{m}$** (DLP) to **$20\,\mu\text{m}$** (FDM). > 3. **Build Rate** — measured in **cm³/hr** or **g/hr**. > 4. **Post-Processing Burden** — from minimal (FDM) to extensive (metal PBF requires stress relief, HIP, and machining). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification Logic The classification of AM processes is a **multi-criteria taxonomy**. A rigorous KTU answer should classify each process across **three independent dimensions**: ### Dimension A: Material State of Feedstock - **Solid State** → Sheet Lamination - **Liquid State** → Vat Photopolymerization, Material Jetting - **Molten State (Extruded)** → Material Extrusion - **Powder State** → Powder Bed Fusion, Binder Jetting, Directed Energy Deposition - **Wire State** → Directed Energy Deposition (DED variant) ### Dimension B: Energy Source Classification - **Photonic Energy (UV Light):** VAT, MJT - **Thermal Energy (Heated Nozzle):** MEX - **High-Energy Beam (Laser):** PBF, DED, SHL - **Charged Particle Beam (Electron Beam):** EBM (a PBF sub-family) - **Chemical Binder:** BJT - **Ultrasonic / Mechanical:** UAM (a SHL sub-family) ### Dimension C: Bonding Mechanism - **Photopolymerization** (UV curing of cross-linkable monomers) - **Thermal Fusion** (melting and solidification) - **Sintering** (sub-melting point diffusion bonding) - **Chemical Bonding** (adhesive activation) - **Welding / Cladding** (localized fusion with wire/powder feed) ## 2.2 Sub-Processes Under Each Family | AM Family | Common Sub-Process Acronyms | Full Form | |-----------|----------------------------|-----------| | Vat Photopolymerization | SLA, DLP, CLIP | Stereolithography, Digital Light Processing, Continuous Liquid Interface Production | | Material Extrusion | FDM, FFF, DIW | Fused Deposition Modeling, Fused Filament Fabrication, Direct Ink Writing | | Material Jetting | PolyJet, MJM, DOD | Photopolymer Jetting, Multi-Jet Modeling, Drop-on-Demand | | Binder Jetting | BJ, 3DP, ExOne | Generic Binder Jetting, Three-Dimensional Printing (MIT) | | Powder Bed Fusion | SLS, SLM, DMLS, EBM | Selective Laser Sintering, Selective Laser Melting, Direct Metal Laser Sintering, Electron Beam Melting | | Directed Energy Deposition | LMD, LENS, DMD, EBAM, PA | Laser Metal Deposition, Laser Engineered Net Shaping, Direct Metal Deposition, Electron Beam Additive Manufacturing, Plasma Arc | | Sheet Lamination | LOM, UAM, CBAM | Laminated Object Manufacturing, Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing, Composite Based Additive Manufacturing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Confused Pairs (High-Yield Distinctions):** > 1. **SLS vs SLM** — SLS sinters (does not fully melt); SLM fully melts the powder → SLM gives denser parts. > 2. **SLA vs DLP** — SLA uses a single-point scanning laser; DLP cures an entire layer at once with a projector → DLP is faster. > 3. **FDM vs PolyJet** — FDM extrudes thermoplastic filament; PolyJet jets photocurable droplets and cures with UV. > 4. **DED vs PBF** — PBF uses a roller to spread powder; DED injects powder/wire into a moving beam. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Critical Equations for AM Classification Problems | Formula / Metric | Expression | Variables & Units | Used In | |------------------|------------|-------------------|---------| | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $P$ = Laser Power (W), $v$ = Scan Speed (mm/s), $h$ = Hatch Spacing (mm), $t$ = Layer Thickness (mm) | PBF, DED | | **Effective Volumetric Energy Density** | $E_v = \dfrac{P \cdot n}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $n$ = Number of scan repetitions | PBF with re-scanning | | **Build Rate (Volumetric)** | $R_v = v \cdot h \cdot t$ | mm³/s | All AM processes | | **Linear Energy Density** | $E_l = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | J/mm | DED, Welding | | **Area Energy Density** | $E_a = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | J/mm² | PBF, DED | | **Specific Energy Consumption** | $E_s = \dfrac{E_{in}}{m_{built}}$ | J/g | All AM for cost analysis | | **Dimensional Scaling Ratio** | $k = \dfrac{L_{built}}{L_{nominal}}$ | dimensionless | Accuracy studies | | **Tolerance Class (ISO 2768-mK)** | $T = \pm \left(a \cdot i^x\right)$ | $a$ = function of class, $i$ = dimension (mm), $x$ = 0.33, 0.5, or 1 | Tolerance mapping | | **Relative Density (Porosity)** | $\rho_{rel} = \dfrac{\rho_{built}}{\rho_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$ | % | PBF, DED quality | | **Hatch Overlap Ratio** | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{h_{actual}}{h_{beam}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | PBF process tuning | | **Aspect Ratio of Build** | $AR = \dfrac{H_{max}}{D_{min}}$ | dimensionless | All AM systems | | **Layer Adhesion Index** | $\sigma_z / \sigma_x$ | ratio (0 to 1) | Anisotropy studies | > [!WARNING] > **Latex Pipe Rule:** Notice that in the formulas above, fractions use $\dfrac{}$ and ratios use $\big/$ — **no vertical pipe symbol `|` is used** to ensure the markdown table renders correctly. ## 2.4 Process Selection Trade-Off Matrix (Engineering Decision Tool) A KTU Part B 14-mark question often asks: *"Select an AM process for [given application]."* The decision is driven by this engineering trade-off matrix: | Selection Criterion | Vat | MEX | MJT | BJT | PBF | DED | SHL | |---------------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----| | **Dimensional Accuracy** | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | | **Surface Finish** | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | | **Material Range** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | | **Multi-Material** | ★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★ | | **Large Parts** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | | **Cost per Part (Low)** | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | | **Lead Time (Low)** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | > **Legend:** ★ = Poor, ★★★★★ = Excellent # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density ($E_v$) for PBF/DED Classification The **volumetric energy density** is the single most cited equation when classifying laser-based AM processes. It determines whether a process operates in the **sintering, melting, or keyhole regime** — which is the basis of sub-classification within Powder Bed Fusion and Directed Energy Deposition. ### Step 1 — Start with the Definition of Energy Energy delivered to a unit volume of material in a single scan pass is defined as: $$ E_v \;=\; \frac{\text{Energy Delivered (J)}}{\text{Volume Affected (mm}^3\text{)}} $$ ### Step 2 — Express Energy Delivered The energy delivered by a laser of power $P$ (in Watts = J/s) scanning at velocity $v$ (in mm/s) is the power multiplied by the interaction (dwell) time. Dwell time for a line of length $L$ is $L/v$, so the energy delivered to a single scan line of length $L$ is: $$ E_{line} \;=\; P \cdot \frac{L}{v} $$ Units check: $(\text{W}) \cdot (\text{mm} / \text{mm/s}) = (\text{J/s}) \cdot (\text{s}) = \text{J}$ ✓ ### Step 3 — Express the Volume Affected per Scan For a single laser scan pass over a powder layer of thickness $t$ (mm) and hatch spacing $h$ (mm), the affected volume per unit length of scan is: $$ V_{unit} \;=\; h \cdot t \cdot 1 \;=\; h \cdot t $$ Units: mm × mm × mm (per unit length) = mm² per mm of scan, which integrates to mm³ over scan length $L$. ### Step 4 — Divide Energy by Volume Dividing the energy per unit scan line by the cross-sectional area affected: $$ E_v \;=\; \frac{P \cdot L / v}{h \cdot t \cdot L} \;=\; \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\,E_v \;=\; \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}\,} $$ **Units check:** $\dfrac{\text{W}}{\text{mm/s} \cdot \text{mm} \cdot \text{mm}} = \dfrac{\text{J/s}}{\text{mm}^3/\text{s}} = \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3}$ ✓ ### Step 5 — Apply Empirical Classification Thresholds Once $E_v$ is known, the resulting AM regime is classified as: | $E_v$ Range (J/mm³) | Resulting Regime | Process Sub-Classification | |---------------------|------------------|----------------------------| | $< 30$ | Insufficient melting → porosity, lack of fusion | Defective SLS / low-energy SLM | | $30 - 100$ | Conduction mode melting, balanced | **SLM / DMLS (Good Quality)** | | $100 - 300$ | Keyhole regime, deep penetration | **DED (LMD / LENS)** | | $> 300$ | Vaporization, keyhole porosity, balling | Defective / excessive energy | ### Step 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style) **Problem:** A Selective Laser Melting (SLM) machine uses a fiber laser with power $P = 200\,\text{W}$, scan speed $v = 800\,\text{mm/s}$, hatch spacing $h = 0.1\,\text{mm}$, and layer thickness $t = 0.03\,\text{mm}$. Determine the volumetric energy density and identify the operating regime. **Solution:** $$ E_v \;=\; \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \;=\; \frac{200}{800 \times 0.1 \times 0.03} $$ Step 1 — Compute denominator: $$ v \cdot h \cdot t \;=\; 800 \times 0.1 \times 0.03 \;=\; 800 \times 0.003 \;=\; 2.4 $$ Step 2 — Final computation: $$ E_v \;=\; \frac{200}{2.4} \;=\; 83.33\;\text{J/mm}^3 $$ Step 3 — Classify using the threshold table: Since $30 < 83.33 < 100$, the process operates in the **conduction-mode melting regime** — ideal for **SLM / DMLS** of metals such as 316L stainless steel or Ti-6Al-4V. This is the **optimal classification window** for high-density parts. ### Step 3.3 Derivation: Build Rate as a Classification Metric Build rate unifies all AM families. For a layer-by-layer process, the build rate $R_v$ (mm³/s) is: $$ R_v \;=\; v_{dep} \cdot A_{layer} $$ where $v_{dep}$ is the deposition speed per layer (mm/s) and $A_{layer}$ is the effective layer area (mm²). For a powder bed system with a recoater speed $v_{rec}$ (mm/s) covering a build area $A$ (mm²) and a layer of thickness $t$ (mm): $$ R_v^{PBF} \;=\; \frac{v_{rec} \cdot A}{t} $$ For Material Extrusion with nozzle diameter $d$ (mm) and print speed $v_{n}$ (mm/s): $$ R_v^{MEX} \;=\; \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v_{n} $$ These expressions allow **direct comparison** between process families — a 14-mark KTU question often asks the student to compare two processes using build rate. ## 3.4 Decision Tree Logic for Process Classification (Textual) A systematic KTU answer should follow this decision tree when classifying a given AM process: 1. **Is the feedstock a liquid photopolymer cured by UV?** → Vat Photopolymerization (sub-classify by laser vs projector). 2. **Is the feedstock a thermoplastic filament pushed through a heated nozzle?** → Material Extrusion (FDM/FFF). 3. **Is the feedstock jetted as discrete droplets from a printhead?** → Material Jetting (PolyJet/DOD). 4. **Is a liquid binder deposited onto a powder bed?** → Binder Jetting. 5. **Is powder fully melted or sintered selectively on a bed?** → Powder Bed Fusion (sub-classify by energy: laser = SLS/SLM/DMLS; e-beam = EBM). 6. **Is energy delivered to a focal point while powder/wire is injected coaxially?** → Directed Energy Deposition (sub-classify by energy + feed). 7. **Are sheets of material bonded and cut layer-by-layer?** → Sheet Lamination (LOM/UAM/CBAM). > [!TIP] > **Exam Strategy:** Always state **three identifiers** (feedstock state + energy source + bonding mechanism) when classifying — examiners award partial marks for each correct dimension. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Tree (ASTM F42 / ISO/ASTM 52900) The following Mermaid block renders the complete hierarchical classification of all seven AM process families as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus. ```mermaid graph TD A["AM PROCESSES
ISO ASTM 52900"]:::root B1["VAT
PHOTOPOLYMERIZATION"]:::vat B2["MATERIAL
EXTRUSION"]:::mex B3["MATERIAL
JETTING"]:::mjt B4["BINDER
JETTING"]:::bjt B5["POWDER BED
FUSION"]:::pbf B6["DIRECTED ENERGY
DEPOSITION"]:::ded B7["SHEET
LAMINATION"]:::shl C1A["SLA - Laser Scan"]:::sub C1B["DLP - Projector"]:::sub C1C["CLIP - Continuous"]:::sub C2A["FDM / FFF"]:::sub C2B["DIW - Direct Ink"]:::sub C3A["PolyJet - UV Jet"]:::sub C3B["MJM - Multi Jet"]:::sub C3C["DOD - Drop on Demand"]:::sub C4A["Binder Jetting - Metal"]:::sub C4B["Binder Jetting - Sand"]:::sub C4C["Color Jet Printing"]:::sub C5A["SLS - Sintering"]:::sub C5B["SLM - Full Melting"]:::sub C5C["DMLS - Metal Laser"]:::sub C5D["EBM - Electron Beam"]:::sub C6A["LMD / LENS"]:::sub C6B["DMD - Wire Feed"]:::sub C6C["EBAM - Electron Beam Wire"]:::sub C6D["Plasma Arc DED"]:::sub C7A["LOM - Paper Foil"]:::sub C7B["UAM - Ultrasonic"]:::sub C7C["CBAM - Composite"]:::sub A --> B1 A --> B2 A --> B3 A --> B4 A --> B5 A --> B6 A --> B7 B1 --> C1A B1 --> C1B B1 --> C1C B2 --> C2A B2 --> C2B B3 --> C3A B3 --> C3B B3 --> C3C B4 --> C4A B4 --> C4B B4 --> C4C B5 --> C5A B5 --> C5B B5 --> C5C B5 --> C5D B6 --> C6A B6 --> C6B B6 --> C6C B6 --> C6D B7 --> C7A B7 --> C7B B7 --> C7C classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px classDef vat fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff classDef mex fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff classDef mjt fill:#ea580c,stroke:#ea580c,color:#ffffff classDef bjt fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff classDef pbf fill:#059669,stroke:#059669,color:#ffffff classDef ded fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff classDef shl fill:#be185d,stroke:#be185d,color:#ffffff classDef sub fill:#f3f4f6,stroke:#6b7280,color:#111827 ``` ## 4.2 Process Selection Architecture (Functional Block Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR IN["CAD MODEL
STL FILE"]:::input CLASSIFY{"AM PROCESS
CLASSIFICATION
DECISION TREE"}:::decision FEED["FEEDSTOCK
SELECTION"]:::stage ENERGY["ENERGY SOURCE
SELECTION"]:::stage BOND["BONDING
MECHANISM"]:::stage POLY["VAT / MJT
Liquid Resin"]:::out THERMO["MEX
Filament"]:::out POWD["PBF / BJT
Powder"]:::out WIRE["DED
Powder or Wire"]:::out FOIL["SHL
Sheet / Foil"]:::out RESULT["3D BUILT PART
LAYER BY LAYER"]:::output IN --> CLASSIFY CLASSIFY --> FEED CLASSIFY --> ENERGY CLASSIFY --> BOND FEED --> POLY FEED --> THERMO FEED --> POWD FEED --> WIRE FEED --> FOIL POLY --> RESULT THERMO --> RESULT POWD --> RESULT WIRE --> RESULT FOIL --> RESULT classDef input fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff classDef decision fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff classDef stage fill:#1e40af,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff classDef out fill:#4b5563,stroke:#4b5563,color:#ffffff classDef output fill:#14532d,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Energy-Source Based Classification Matrix (Block Schematic) ```mermaid graph TB UV["UV LIGHT ENERGY"]:::energy LASER["LASER ENERGY"]:::energy EB["ELECTRON BEAM"]:::energy THERM["THERMAL / MECHANICAL"]:::energy ULTR["ULTRASONIC / CHEMICAL"]:::energy UV --> P1["VAT PHOTOPOLYMERIZATION
SLA DLP CLIP"]:::process UV --> P2["MATERIAL JETTING
PolyJet MJM"]:::process LASER --> P3["POWDER BED FUSION
SLS SLM DMLS"]:::process LASER --> P4["DIRECTED ENERGY DEPOSITION
LMD LENS DMD"]:::process LASER --> P5["SHEET LAMINATION
LOM CBAM"]:::process EB --> P6["POWDER BED FUSION
EBM"]:::process EB --> P7["DIRECTED ENERGY DEPOSITION
EBAM"]:::process THERM --> P8["MATERIAL EXTRUSION
FDM FFF DIW"]:::process THERM --> P9["BINDER JETTING
BJ 3DP"]:::process ULTR --> P10["SHEET LAMINATION
UAM"]:::process classDef energy fill:#1e1b4b,stroke:#1e1b4b,color:#ffffff classDef process fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: List the seven categories of Additive Manufacturing processes as per ASTM/ISO 52900 standard.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** As per the **ASTM F2792 / ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** standard, the seven primary categories of Additive Manufacturing processes are: 1. **Vat Photopolymerization** (e.g., SLA, DLP) 2. **Material Extrusion** (e.g., FDM, FFF) 3. **Material Jetting** (e.g., PolyJet, MJM) 4. **Binder Jetting** (e.g., 3DP, metal binder jetting) 5. **Powder Bed Fusion** (e.g., SLS, SLM, DMLS, EBM) 6. **Directed Energy Deposition** (e.g., LMD, LENS, EBAM) 7. **Sheet Lamination** (e.g., LOM, UAM, CBAM) *(Award 0.5 mark for each correctly named family — full 3 marks for complete list.)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Distinguish between Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) and Selective Laser Melting (SLM).** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | SLS | SLM | |-----------|-----|-----| | Bonding Mechanism | **Sintering** (below melting point) | **Full Melting** (above melting point) | | Density Achieved | 80–95% theoretical | 95–99.9% theoretical | | Material State After Process | Partially fused | Fully molten and re-solidified | | Typical Application | Nylon, PA12 parts | Ti-6Al-4V, 316L SS, Inconel | | Mechanical Strength | Lower (porosity present) | Higher, near-wrought properties | | Energy Required | Lower | Higher | *(Award 1 mark for bonding distinction, 1 mark for density, 1 mark for example materials.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Classify the various Additive Manufacturing processes in detail. Explain Powder Bed Fusion and Directed Energy Deposition processes with neat sketches and their applications.** #### Part (a) — General Classification of AM Processes (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** The Additive Manufacturing processes are classified into **seven major categories** based on the **ASTM/ISO 52900** standard. The classification is based on three criteria: **material feedstock state**, **energy source**, and **bonding mechanism**. **(1) Vat Photopolymerization:** Liquid photopolymer resin is selectively cured layer-by-layer using UV light. Examples: **Stereolithography (SLA), Digital Light Processing (DLP), Continuous Liquid Interface Production (CLIP).** Used for high-accuracy prototypes and dental/medical models. **(2) Material Extrusion:** A thermoplastic filament is heated to a semi-molten state and extruded through a nozzle. Example: **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM).** Used for low-cost functional prototypes, jigs, fixtures. **(3) Material Jetting:** Photopolymer droplets are jetted from multiple nozzles and instantly UV-cured. Example: **PolyJet.** Allows multi-material, full-color parts with high resolution. **(4) Binder Jetting:** A liquid binder is selectively deposited onto a powder bed to bond the particles. Examples: **3D Printing (3DP), ExOne metal binder jetting.** Used for sand casting patterns and large metal parts (requires post-sintering infiltration). **(5) Powder Bed Fusion (PBF):** A thin layer of powder is spread and selectively fused by a thermal energy source. Sub-categories: **SLS (sintering, polymer), SLM / DMLS (full melting, metal), EBM (electron beam melting, metal).** Used for aerospace, medical implants, and tooling. **(6) Directed Energy Deposition (DED):** Focused thermal energy melts material as it is deposited from a coaxial nozzle. Sub-categories: **Laser Metal Deposition (LMD), Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS), Electron Beam Additive Manufacturing (EBAM), Plasma Arc DED.** Used for repair, large structural parts, and hybrid manufacturing. **(7) Sheet Lamination:** Sheets of material are bonded and cut layer-by-layer. Sub-categories: **Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM)**, **Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM)**, and **Composite Based Additive Manufacturing (CBAM).** Used for paper-based prototypes, metal matrix composites, and embedded electronics. > [!TIP] > **Mark Split:** 1 mark per process family for naming, 1 mark for feedstock+energy identification (total ~3 marks for the list). The remaining 4 marks come from **two elaborated families** (PBF and DED are mandatory here as they are requested in part b). #### Part (b) — Detailed Explanation of PBF and DED (7 Marks) **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF):** - **Working Principle:** A roller or blade spreads a thin layer of powder (typically $t = 0.02$ to $0.1\,\text{mm}$) across a build platform. A thermal energy source (laser or electron beam) then scans the cross-section of the part, fusing the powder. The platform descends by one layer thickness, and the process repeats. - **Energy Source Variants:** - **CO₂ or fiber laser** → SLS (polymer), SLM / DMLS (metal) - **Electron beam** → EBM (metal, conducted in vacuum) - **Process Parameters:** Laser power $P$ (W), scan speed $v$ (mm/s), hatch spacing $h$ (mm), layer thickness $t$ (mm). - **Volumetric Energy Density:** $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ — typically $30$ to $100\,\text{J/mm}^3$ for defect-free metal parts. - **Schematic:** Powder bed (top) → Recoater → Build platform (descends) → Laser + galvanometer scanner → Powder feed bin. - **Applications:** Aerospace brackets (GE LEAP engine fuel nozzle), orthopedic implants (TRUMATCH, Ti-6Al-4V acetabular cups), injection mold inserts with conformal cooling. **Directed Energy Deposition (DED):** - **Working Principle:** Powder or wire feedstock is fed coaxially through a deposition head, where it is melted by a focused laser, electron beam, or plasma arc. The head moves in 3D space (often 5-axis), depositing material only where needed. - **Variants:** LMD (laser + powder), LENS (laser + powder), DMD (laser + wire), EBAM (electron beam + wire), PA-DED (plasma + wire). - **Process Parameters:** Laser power, travel speed, powder feed rate (g/min), standoff distance, gas shielding. - **Distinctive Feature:** Multi-axis deposition and ability to **add material to existing parts** (repair and hybrid manufacturing). - **Applications:** Turbine blade repair, large marine propeller fabrication, hybrid CNC + AM manufacturing of large aerospace structures, deposition of functionally graded materials. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. Students often **omit the schematics** — losing 2 marks easily. Even a hand-drawn block diagram showing **powder bed + recoater + laser + platform** is sufficient. > 2. **Do not confuse SLS with SLM** — examiners specifically check if you understand the **sintering vs full melting** difference. > 3. **DED vs PBF** is the highest-weightage distinction in this module — students who do not mention **coaxial powder injection** lose 1 mark. > 4. Always quote the **volumetric energy density formula** when discussing laser-based PBF/DED. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: With a neat flowchart, classify the AM processes based on the state of the starting material. Compare Vat Photopolymerization and Material Jetting in detail. State at least four applications of each.** #### Part (a) — Classification Based on Material State (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** The classification of AM processes based on the **state of the starting feedstock material** is as follows: **1. Solid-State Feedstock (Sheets/Foils):** - Used in **Sheet Lamination** processes such as Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM — paper/ polymer foils) and Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM — metal tapes). - Material: $0.1$ to $0.2\,\text{mm}$ thick sheets. **2. Liquid-State Feedstock (Photopolymers):** - Used in **Vat Photopolymerization** (SLA, DLP, CLIP). - The liquid resin is selectively polymerized by UV light (wavelength $\lambda \approx 355\,\text{nm}$ for SLA, $385$–$405\,\text{nm}$ for DLP). **3. Molten-State Feedstock (Extruded Filament):** - Used in **Material Extrusion** (FDM/FFF), where a thermoplastic filament (e.g., ABS, PLA, Nylon) is heated to $180$–$260\,°\text{C}$ and extruded through a $0.2$–$0.8\,\text{mm}$ nozzle. **4. Powder-State Feedstock (Metals, Polymers, Ceramics):** - Used in **Powder Bed Fusion** (SLS, SLM, DMLS, EBM) and **Binder Jetting** (3DP). - Typical particle size: $15$ to $100\,\mu\text{m}$ for laser-based PBF; $50$ to $200\,\mu\text{m}$ for binder jetting. **5. Wire-State Feedstock:** - Used in **Directed Energy Deposition** (DMD, EBAM, Plasma Arc DED). - Wire diameters: $0.8$ to $3.2\,\text{mm}$ for metallic wire-arc AM. **6. Droplet-State Feedstock (Jetted Photopolymer):** - Used in **Material Jetting** (PolyJet, MJM), where droplets of $1$ to $50\,\text{pL}$ are deposited. > **Flowchart Summary (textual):** > `Raw Material State` → (Solid) → Sheet Lamination > → (Liquid Photopolymer) → Vat Photopolymerization > → (Molten Thermoplastic) → Material Extrusion > → (Powder) → Powder Bed Fusion, Binder Jetting, DED > → (Wire) → DED > → (Droplets) → Material Jetting #### Part (b) — Comparison: Vat Photopolymerization vs Material Jetting (7 Marks) | Comparison Parameter | Vat Photopolymerization | Material Jetting | |----------------------|--------------------------|-------------------| | **Material State** | Liquid photopolymer resin in a vat | Droplets of photopolymer jetted on demand | | **Energy Source** | UV laser (SLA) or UV projector (DLP) | UV lamp (curing) + thermal (jetting) | | **Curing Mechanism** | Photopolymerization (point or area scan) | Photopolymerization (instant per droplet) | | **Layer Thickness** | $0.02$ to $0.15\,\text{mm}$ | $0.014$ to $0.032\,\text{mm}$ (PolyJet) | | **Surface Finish ($R_a$)** | $0.5$ to $5\,\mu\text{m}$ | $0.5$ to $2\,\mu\text{m}$ | | **Accuracy** | $\pm 0.05$ to $\pm 0.1\,\text{mm}$ | $\pm 0.02$ to $\pm 0.05\,\text{mm}$ | | **Multi-Material Capability** | Limited (only resin swap) | Excellent (multiple printheads) | | **Support Structures** | Required (for overhangs) | Easily dissolved wax-like support | | **Build Speed (per layer)** | Slow (SLA) to Moderate (DLP, CLIP) | Fast (multiple jets in parallel) | | **Material Cost** | Moderate | High (proprietary cartridges) | | **Examples** | 3D Systems SLA, Carbon CLIP, EnvisionTec Perfactory | Stratasys PolyJet, 3D Systems MJM | | **Applications (4+ each)** | (1) Dental crowns & aligners
(2) Jewelry casting patterns
(3) Hearing aid shells
(4) Microfluidic devices
(5) Optical lens prototypes
(6) Surgical planning models | (1) Full-color anatomical models
(2) Multi-material prototypes (rigid + rubber)
(3) Transparent + opaque assemblies
(4) Medical visualization (true-tissue models)
(5) Injection-molded-like prototypes
(6) Consumer product mock-ups | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. The phrase **"vat photopolymerization"** must not be confused with **"material jetting"** — both use photopolymers, but the **feedstock location (vat vs printhead)** is the differentiator. > 2. When asked for "at least four applications," write **exactly four or more** — examiners allocate 0.5 mark per application in this section. > 3. The **flowchart** is mandatory (1 mark) — failing to draw one costs 1 mark even if the classification text is correct. > 4. Use **SI units consistently** ($\mu\text{m}$, mm, °C) — examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Classification of AM Processes** - ✅ **Total of 7 process families** as per **ASTM F2792 / ISO/ASTM 52900:2021**: VAT, MEX, MJT, BJT, PBF, DED, SHL. - ✅ **Three classification dimensions** to remember: (1) Material state, (2) Energy source, (3) Bonding mechanism. - ✅ **Liquid photopolymer** → VAT, MJT. **Molten filament** → MEX. **Powder** → PBF, BJT, DED. **Wire** → DED. **Sheet** → SHL. - ✅ **SLS ≠ SLM** — SLS sinters; SLM fully melts. SLM gives $\geq 99\%$ density, SLS gives $80$–$95\%$. - ✅ **DED distinguishing feature:** Coaxial material feed (powder or wire) into a moving energy beam. - ✅ **EBM** is a PBF sub-process that uses an **electron beam** in a **vacuum** — uniquely suited for **conductive metals** like Ti alloys. - ✅ **Volumetric Energy Density** formula: $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ (J/mm³). Optimal range: **$30$–$100\,\text{J/mm}^3$** for defect-free metal PBF. - ✅ **Build rate** of PBF: $R_v = v_{rec} \cdot A \,/\, t$ — for a $250 \times 250\,\text{mm}$ bed, recoater at $100\,\text{mm/s}$, $0.03\,\text{mm}$ layer, this gives $\approx 2 \times 10^5\,\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$. - ✅ **Material Jetting = PolyJet** → **multi-material, full-color, ultra-high resolution** ($0.014\,\text{mm}$ layer). - ✅ **Sheet Lamination (UAM)** uses **ultrasonic welding** of metal tapes — solid-state, no melting, suitable for **embedded sensors and dissimilar metals**. - ✅ **Binder Jetting** requires **post-processing** — debinding + sintering + infiltration → not directly functional. - ✅ **No heat-affected zone** in VAT, MJT, MEX, BJT, and UAM — these are **"cold-classification"** processes with minimal residual stress. - ✅ **Always cite the standard (ASTM/ISO 52900)** in the first line of any classification answer — earns you 0.5 mark of "methodology credit." - ✅ **Process Selection Rule of Thumb:** Prototype + polymer + low cost → **FDM**; Functional metal part + medium volume → **SLM/DMLS**; Repair + large parts → **DED/LMD**; Casting pattern → **Binder Jetting**; Multi-material visual model → **PolyJet**; Embedded electronics → **UAM**. > [!NOTE] > **End of Module 1 — Classification of Additive Manufacturing Processes.** > This topic is foundational for **all subsequent modules** (process physics, materials, design for AM, post-processing, and applications). Mastery of the **seven families**, their **sub-processes**, and the **volumetric energy density** equation is the minimum bar for a KTU 2024 full-mark performance.

topics.title: Materials Used in Additive Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET525 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING AND 3D PRINTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Materials Used in Additive Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 1 — MATERIALS USED IN ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Additive Manufacturing (AM) materials** are the physical feedstock substances — supplied in standardized forms such as **filaments, powders, liquid resins, sheets, and wires** — that are selectively joined, fused, or cured layer-by-layer under computer-controlled energy sources (thermal, photonic, chemical, or kinetic) to construct a three-dimensional solid object directly from a digital CAD model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The material form must be **physically compatible** with the specific AM process. For example, Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) requires a **powder bed**, while Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) mandates a **thermoplastic filament of 1.75 mm or 2.85 mm diameter**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a 3D printer as a highly precise **cake-decorating robot**. Just as a baker can pipe icing (soft paste), lay chocolate sheets (thin layers), or sprinkle sugar crystals (granular powders) to build a multi-tier cake, an AM machine uses different "ingredients" — **plastics, metals, ceramics, resins, or even living cells** — to "print" an object one microscopic layer at a time. The *type of cake* you can make is dictated by *what you put in the piping bag*.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Material Realities Every AM Student Must Memorize:**
> 1. **Feedstock form** (powder / filament / resin / sheet) → determines handling
> 2. **Energy interaction** (heat / light / binder / jet) → determines processing window
> 3. **Final state** (solid / porous / functional) → determines end-use application

### 1.3 Why Material Choice is the Most Critical Design Decision in AM

In conventional (subtractive) manufacturing, the designer typically picks a stock material *after* choosing the process. In **AM, the material and the process are co-dependent** — they are selected together. A change in material often requires a complete re-validation of:
- **Layer adhesion strength** (z-axis vs. xy-axis properties)
- **Anisotropy ratio** (typically **1.0 to 1.5** in well-tuned FDM parts)
- **Build chamber atmosphere** (inert gas, vacuum, or ambient)
- **Post-processing requirements** (support removal, sintering, infiltration)

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Standard Metric:** The **build volume utilization factor** — defined as the ratio of the part volume to the build chamber volume — is most adversely affected by material choice when support structures are mandatory (e.g., metal SLM/DMLS).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analysis Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of AM Materials

AM materials are broadly classified into **six principal families**, each with sub-categories tied to specific ASTM/ISO process families.

### 2.1.1 Polymer-Based Materials (Largest Commercial Segment)

| Sub-Class | Common Examples | AM Process Compatibility | Key Mechanical Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermoplastics (Filament)** | PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU, Nylon (PA12, PA6) | FDM / FFF | Tensile Strength: **30–70 MPa** |
| **Thermoplastics (Powder)** | PA12 (Nylon 12), PA11, TPU powder | SLS, MJF | Tensile Strength: **45–50 MPa** |
| **Photopolymers (Resin)** | Acrylate, Epoxy-acrylate, Vinyl ether | SLA, DLP, MSLA (mSLA) | Tensile Strength: **25–80 MPa** |
| **Elastomers** | TPE, TPU (Shore A 85–95) | FDM, SLS, Polyjet | Elongation at break: **>200 %** |
| **High-Performance Polymers** | PEEK, PEKK, ULTEM (PEI), PPSU | FDM (heated chamber), SLS | Tensile Strength: **90–100 MPa**; HDT up to **260 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **PLA (Polylactic Acid)** is biodegradable, derived from corn-starch or sugarcane, and is the most widely used FDM filament due to its low warpage and printing temperature of **190–220 °C**.

### 2.1.2 Metal-Based Materials (Industrial / Aerospace Segment)

| Sub-Class | Form | AM Process | Typical Particle Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ferrous alloys** | Powder | DMLS, SLM, EBM, DED | **15–45 μm** |
| **Aluminium alloys (AlSi10Mg)** | Powder | SLM, DED | **20–63 μm** |
| **Titanium alloys (Ti-6Al-4V)** | Powder | SLM, EBM, DED | **15–45 μm** |
| **Nickel superalloys (Inconel 718, 625)** | Powder | SLM, EBM, DED | **15–45 μm** |
| **Cobalt-Chrome (CoCrMo)** | Powder | SLM, EBM | **15–45 μm** |
| **Precious metals (Au, Ag, Pt)** | Powder / Wire | SLM, DED, BJ | Varies |
| **Steel (316L, 17-4 PH, H13)** | Powder | DMLS, SLM, BJ | **15–45 μm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Powder Flowability** is measured by the **Hall Flowmeter** standard **ASTM B213** and the **Carney Flow** test **ASTM B964**. A good AM powder exhibits a Hall Flow rate of **≤ 25 s for 50 g**.

### 2.1.3 Ceramic Materials

Ceramics are notoriously difficult to process via AM due to their **high melting points (> 2000 °C)** and **brittle nature**. They are typically handled via indirect routes:

- **Binder Jetting (BJ)** → "Green part" → **debinding** → **sintering** at high temperature
- **Stereolithography (SLA)** with **ceramic-loaded photopolymer resins** (e.g., alumina, zirconia, silica)
- **Robocasting / Direct Ink Writing (DIW)** of ceramic pastes
- **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** of polymer-ceramic composite powders

Common ceramics: **Alumina (Al₂O₃)**, **Zirconia (ZrO₂)**, **Silicon Carbide (SiC)**, **Hydroxyapatite (HA)**.

### 2.1.4 Composite Materials

Composites combine two or more constituent phases to achieve **synergistic properties** (e.g., carbon fibre + polymer matrix).

- **Short Carbon Fibre (CF) reinforced nylon** (Markforged Onyx) — Tensile strength ~ **36 MPa** (neat) to **590 MPa** (with continuous CF)
- **Fibreglass-reinforced thermoplastics**
- **Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)** in DED
- **Graphene / CNT loaded photopolymers** for functional / electrical applications

### 2.1.5 Biomaterials

Used in **medical and tissue engineering** applications.

- **Biodegradable polymers:** PLA, PLGA, PCL, PLLA
- **Bioceramics:** Hydroxyapatite (HA), Tricalcium Phosphate (TCP)
- **Biometals:** Ti-6Al-4V (implants), CoCrMo (dentistry)
- **Bio-inks:** Hydrogels like GelMA, Alginate, Collagen (used in **Bioprinting / Extrusion-based 3D bioprinting**)

### 2.1.6 Smart / Functional Materials

- **Shape Memory Polymers (SMP)** — respond to temperature / light
- **Piezoelectric polymers (PVDF)**
- **Conductive filaments (graphene, copper-loaded PLA)** for printed electronics
- **Thermochromic / photochromic resins** for 4D printing
- **Hydrogels** for soft robotics and biomedical sensors

## 2.2 Material Selection Criteria — The Engineering Decision Framework

When a KTU student is asked to **select an AM material** for a given functional requirement, the decision is governed by the following **seven cardinal criteria** (often appearing in 14-mark questions):

1. **Mechanical performance** — tensile strength, modulus, fatigue, hardness
2. **Thermal performance** — HDT, glass transition (Tg), thermal conductivity
3. **Chemical resistance** — solvent, acid/base, UV stability
4. **Process compatibility** — powder size, flow, melt viscosity
5. **Biocompatibility** — ISO 10993 (medical), FDA approval
6. **Cost-per-kg** — filaments: ~ **₹800–₹4000/kg**; metal powders: ~ **₹8,000–₹30,000/kg**
7. **Post-processing feasibility** — machinability, polishability, sterilization

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick-Reference Matrix

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Powder Sphericity | $\psi$ | $\psi = \dfrac{4 \pi A}{P^{2}}$ where $A$ = projected area, $P$ = perimeter | $\psi \approx 0.85 \text{ to } 1.00$ |
| Powder Tap Density | $\rho_{t}$ | $\rho_{t} = \dfrac{m}{V_{t}}$ after 3000 taps | **3.5–4.5 g/cc** (Ti-6Al-4V) |
| Anisotropy Index (AM) | $AI$ | $AI = \dfrac{\sigma_{z}}{\sigma_{xy}}$ | $0.7 \leq AI \leq 1.0$ |
| Volumetric Energy Density | $E_{v}$ | $E_{v} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ (W/mm³) | **40–120 J/mm³** (SLM) |
| Linear Energy Density | $E_{l}$ | $E_{l} = \dfrac{P}{v}$ (J/mm) | **0.1–1.0 J/mm** (DED) |
| Hatch Spacing | $h$ | Distance between adjacent laser scans | **0.08–0.15 mm** |
| Layer Thickness | $t$ | Z-axis increment per pass | **0.02–0.10 mm** |
| Porosity (AM part) | $\phi$ | $\phi = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{actual}}{\rho_{theoretical}}$ | **< 0.5 %** (well-tuned SLM) |
| Aspect Ratio of Filler | $AR$ | $AR = \dfrac{L}{D}$ of fibre / platelet | **5–20** (short fibre) |
| Melt Flow Index | $MFI$ | Mass extruded in 10 min at specified T, P | **5–20 g/10 min** (ABS) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volumetric Energy Density ($E_v$)** is the single most critical process parameter in powder-bed fusion of metals. Insufficient $E_v$ → lack-of-fusion porosity; excessive $E_v$ → keyhole porosity / vaporization. The optimal "process window" for Ti-6Al-4V in SLM is typically **$E_v$ = 60–80 J/mm³**.

## 2.4 Why This Knowledge Matters in Real Engineering

- **Aerospace (GE Aviation, Airbus):** Ti-6Al-4V bracket weight reduction of **30–50 %** via topology-optimized SLM parts.
- **Medical (Stryker, DePuy):** Patient-specific cranial implants in PEEK or Ti.
- **Automotive (Bugatti, Local Motors):** Functional metal components (brackets, nodes) in 316L.
- **Tooling (conformal cooling inserts):** DED-printed H13 or Maraging 300 tools with internal cooling channels reduce cycle time by **40 %**.
- **Consumer goods:** Adidas Futurecraft, Carbon's lattice midsoles in photopolymer elastomers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density ($E_v$) from First Principles

The **volumetric energy density** is the energy delivered by the heat source (laser/electron beam) per unit volume of material consolidated. It is the most exam-relevant derivation in this module.

### Step 1: Define the melted volume per unit time

Consider a single laser scan of power $P$ (in W) moving at scan speed $v$ (in mm/s). The laser beam has a hatch spacing $h$ (in mm) and a layer thickness $t$ (in mm).

The volume of material melted per unit length of scan is:

$$
V_{unit} = h \cdot t \quad [\text{mm}^{3}/\text{mm}]
$$

### Step 2: Time to scan a unit length

The time taken to traverse one unit length (1 mm) is:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{1}{v} \quad [\text{s}/\text{mm}]
$$

### Step 3: Energy delivered per unit length

The energy delivered to the unit length is simply the power multiplied by the dwell time:

$$
E_{unit} = P \cdot \tau = \dfrac{P}{v} \quad [\text{J/mm}]
$$

> This is also called the **linear energy density**, $E_l = P / v$.

### Step 4: Energy per unit volume

To convert linear energy into volumetric energy, we divide the linear energy by the cross-sectional area being consolidated, $h \times t$:

$$
E_{v} = \dfrac{E_{unit}}{V_{unit}} = \dfrac{P / v}{h \cdot t}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{v} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}} \quad \text{[J/mm}^{3}\text{]}
$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example (KTU-style problem)

> **Given:** Laser power $P = 200$ W; scan speed $v = 800$ mm/s; hatch spacing $h = 0.10$ mm; layer thickness $t = 0.030$ mm.
> **Find:** Volumetric energy density.

**Step 1 — Compute denominator:**

$$
v \cdot h \cdot t = 800 \times 0.10 \times 0.030
$$

$$
v \cdot h \cdot t = 2.4 \quad [\text{mm}^{3}/\text{s}]
$$

**Step 2 — Divide:**

$$
E_{v} = \dfrac{200}{2.4}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{v} = 83.33 \ \text{J/mm}^{3}}
$$

> This value lies in the recommended SLM process window for **316L stainless steel** (typically 60–100 J/mm³), indicating a soundly tuned build.

## 3.2 Derivation: Anisotropy Index ($AI$) in FDM Parts

In FDM, the bond between adjacent roads is weaker than the bulk filament. The **anisotropy index** quantifies this by comparing Z-direction strength to XY-direction strength.

**Step 1 — Define strengths:**

Let $\sigma_{z}$ be the ultimate tensile strength in the Z (build) direction, and $\sigma_{xy}$ the UTS in the XY (in-plane) direction.

**Step 2 — Form the ratio:**

$$
AI = \dfrac{\sigma_{z}}{\sigma_{xy}}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

- $AI = 1.0$ → **isotropic** (ideal, rarely achieved)
- $AI = 0.7$ → **typical FDM** PLA part
- $AI < 0.5$ → **poor inter-layer bonding** (cold extrusion, delamination risk)

**Step 4 — Improving $AI$:** Increase nozzle temperature, decrease layer height, increase infill density, use enclosed heated chamber (for ABS / PC).

## 3.3 Derivation: Powder Packing Efficiency & Porosity Influence

For a given AM powder, the **relative density** of the powder bed before melting governs the upper limit of achievable part density.

Let $\rho_{bulk}$ = bulk density of powder (as-poured), $\rho_{tap}$ = tap density (after vibration), $\rho_{true}$ = skeletal (true) density of the solid material.

**Step 1 — Packing fraction:**

$$
\Phi_{pack} = \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{true}}
$$

Typical values: $\Phi_{pack} = 0.55 - 0.65$ for gas-atomized spherical metal powders.

**Step 2 — Final part porosity:**

$$
\phi_{part} = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{part}}{\rho_{theoretical}}
$$

**Step 3 — Implication for AM:** A powder with poor flowability and low $\Phi_{pack}$ will produce higher **lack-of-fusion defects**, because the laser cannot fully melt the larger effective void space.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process-Material Compatibility

This matrix is **high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions** asking "Suggest a suitable material and process for the following application."

| Application | Recommended Material | Recommended AM Process | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aerospace bracket (lightweight, high strength) | **Ti-6Al-4V** | **SLM (Selective Laser Melting)** | High strength-to-weight, complex topology possible |
| Functional prototype of a gear (office use) | **ABS** or **Nylon (PA12)** | **FDM** or **SLS** | Low cost, adequate mechanicals, no support in SLS |
| Dental crown (biocompatible, aesthetic) | **CoCrMo** or **Zirconia (ZrO₂)** | **SLM** (metal) or **BJ + sintering** (ceramic) | ISO 10993 biocompatibility, high hardness |
| Patient-specific bone scaffold | **PLA / PCL + Hydroxyapatite** | **FDM** or **Bioprinting** | Biodegradable, osteoconductive |
| Conformal cooling insert in injection mould | **H13 tool steel** or **Maraging 300** | **DED (Laser Metal Deposition)** | High hardness, complex internal channels |
| Soft-touch grip / wearable | **TPU (Shore 85A)** | **FDM** or **SLS** | High elongation, elastomeric |
| High-temp aerospace duct | **PEEK** or **ULTEM (PEI)** | **FDM (heated chamber)** or **SLS** | HDT > 200 °C, flame-retardant |
| Investment casting pattern | **Wax / PLA / Castable resin** | **SLA / DLP / FDM** | Burnout clean, low ash residue |
| Functional electronic circuit | **Conductive PLA / Silver ink** | **Material Extrusion / Inkjet** | Electrical conductivity, low resistance |
| Decorative figurine (full colour) | **Plaster-based powder + binder** | **Binder Jetting (colour)** | Multi-colour, fast, low-cost |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Material Selection Algorithm (for KTU Design Problems)

**Step 1 — Identify Functional Requirements**
- Mechanical load? → yield strength, fatigue
- Thermal environment? → HDT, Tg, CTE
- Chemical environment? → chemical resistance chart
- Biological contact? → biocompatibility grade

**Step 2 — Identify Process Constraints**
- Minimum feature size? → resolution of printer
- Build volume? → part envelope
- Surface finish needed? → SLA/DLP for fine finish

**Step 3 — Generate Material Shortlist**
Cross-reference **ASTM/ISO material datasheets** with **process-material compatibility matrix** (see Section 3.4 above).

**Step 4 — Apply Cost and Post-processing Filter**
Eliminate materials whose **per-kg cost** exceeds the budget *or* whose required post-processing (HIP, machining) inflates total cost beyond tolerance.

**Step 5 — Validate with Prototyping**
Print a **test coupon** (e.g., ASTM E8 tensile specimen) and verify mechanicals against datasheet values.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of AM Materials

```mermaid

    A[AM MATERIALS]:::root --> B[POLYMERS]:::poly
    A --> C[METALS]:::metal

    A --> F[BIOMATERIALS]:::bio

    G --> G4[Thermochromic Resins]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef poly fill:#4caf50,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef metal fill:#ff9800,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef ceramic fill:#9c27b0,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    classDef bio fill:#e91e63,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef smart fill:#3f51b5,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Material Form vs. AM Process Compatibility Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    P2[POWDER POLYMER]:::powderp --> S1[SLS MJF]

    P4[METAL POWDER]:::powderm --> M1[SLM DMLS EBM]
    P4 --> M2[BINDER JETTING]
    P4 --> M3[DED]
    P5[METAL WIRE]:::wire --> M3
    P6[SHEET]:::sheet --> L1[SHEET LAMINATION LOM]
    P7[PASTE]:::paste --> R1[ROBOCASTING DIW]
    P8[BIO INK]:::bioink --> BP[BIOPRINTING]

    classDef fil fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef powderp fill:#8bc34a,stroke:#000,color:#000

```

## 4.3 Material Selection Decision Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1[START REQUIREMENT SPEC] --> Q1{LOAD BEARING?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{MAX TEMP EXPOSURE?}
    Q1 -- NO --> S1[NON STRUCTURAL POLYMER PATH]
    Q2 -- LT 100C --> S2[STANDARD POLYMER FDM SLS]
    Q2 -- 100C TO 200C --> S3[HIGH PERF POLYMER PEEK]
    Q2 -- GT 200C --> S4[METAL OR CERAMIC]
    S4 --> Q3{BIO COMPATIBLE?}
    Q3 -- YES --> S5[TITANIUM OR HYDROXYAPATITE]
    Q3 -- NO --> S6[INCONEL OR STEEL]
    S2 --> F1[CHECK COST]
    S3 --> F1
    S5 --> F1
    S6 --> F1
    S1 --> F1
    F1 --> F2[VALIDATE TEST COUPON]
    F2 --> ENDX[FINAL MATERIAL SELECTION]
```

    MET --> M3[MELTING 660 TO 1670 C]
    MET --> M4[COST MEDIUM TO HIGH]

    CER --> C2[FRACTURE TOUGHNESS 3 TO 8 MPA SQRT M]
    CER --> C3[SINTERING 1200 TO 1700 C]
    CER --> C4[COST HIGH]
    COM[COMPOSITE FAMILY] --> CO1[TENSILE UP TO 1000 MPA WITH CF]

    COM --> CO3[COST MEDIUM TO HIGH]
