> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET525 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING AND 3D PRINTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Slicing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slicing** is the computational process in Additive Manufacturing (AM) wherein a three-dimensional Computer-Aided Design (CAD) model — typically represented as a triangulated mesh (STL, 3MF, or OBJ format) — is digitally dissected into a sequence of thin, parallel, two-dimensional horizontal cross-sections (*layers*) of defined thickness $h$. Each layer is subsequently converted into a set of machine-executable toolpaths, ultimately producing **G-code** (or its equivalent NC code) that directs the print head, laser, or binder jet along precise $X$–$Y$ trajectories at a fixed $Z$ elevation.

The slicer software is, therefore, the **translator** that bridges the geometric domain (mesh data) and the kinematic domain (machine motion commands). It is the single most critical piece of software in the entire AM digital workflow.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Loaf-of-Bread Analogy**
> Imagine you have a solid sculpture of a rabbit (your 3D CAD model). To replicate it using a 3D printer, you cannot "print" 3D volume directly. Instead, the slicer software does the digital equivalent of asking: *"If I cut this rabbit with a very sharp horizontal knife every 0.2 mm, what shape would each slice be?"* — The printer then physically deposits or fuses material along the **outline of each slice**, one on top of the other, until the full 3D rabbit is reconstructed.
>
> Reverse analogy: A **CT scan** reconstructs 3D anatomy from 2D slices — *slicing does the inverse*: it deconstructs 3D geometry into 2D slices for layered manufacturing.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Layer Thickness** | $h$ (mm) | Vertical height of each deposited layer, typically **0.05 – 0.4 mm** |
| **STL** | — | **S**tereo**l**ithography file — triangulated mesh format |
| **G-code** | — | Numerical Control language (RS-274) executed by the printer firmware |
| **Infill** | — | Internal lattice density (% of internal solid material) |
| **Support Structures** | — | Sacrificial scaffolds printed beneath overhangs $> 45°$ |
| **Brim / Raft** | — | Adhesion aids printed beneath the first layer |
| **Shell / Wall** | — | Solid outer perimeters of each layer |

## 1.4 Why Slicing is Indispensable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without slicing, the AM machine has **no idea** *where* to deposit material, *how fast* to move, *when* to extrude, or *at what temperature* to maintain the melt pool. Slicing is the **digital-to-physical conversion step** — analogous to compiling source code in computer science. A bad slicer configuration = a bad part, regardless of how good the CAD model is.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Slicing Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

The slicer executes a deterministic, sequential algorithm:

1. **Mesh Import & Validation** — Load STL / 3MF file; check for *manifold errors*, *non-manifold edges*, *inverted normals*, and *degenerate triangles*.
2. **Orientation & Translation** — User (or auto-orient algorithm) places the part on the build platform to minimise supports and maximise $Z$ mechanical strength.
3. **Slicing Plane Generation** — A set of $N$ horizontal planes $z_i = z_0 + i \cdot h$ are mathematically defined, where $i \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1\}$.
4. **Plane–Mesh Intersection (Boolean Operation)** — Each plane is intersected with the triangulated mesh, producing **closed polylines** (contours) per layer.
5. **Contour Classification** — Contours are sorted into *outer perimeters*, *inner holes*, and *islands* using even–odd or winding-number rules.
6. **Infill Pattern Generation** — A 2D pattern (rectilinear, honeycomb, gyroid, triangular, etc.) is mathematically tiled within each closed outer contour.
7. **Support Structure Synthesis** — Overhang geometry is detected (faces with normal $\vec{n} \cdot \hat{k} < \cos(45°)$) and tree-like or grid scaffolds are generated.
8. **Toolpath Planning (Path Optimisation)** — Contour traversal order is solved as a **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** variant to minimise non-print travel moves.
9. **G-code Generation** — Each segment is encoded with coordinates, feedrate (F), extrusion (E), temperature (M-codes), and acceleration (G-codes).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Why** behind step (8) is critical: an unoptimised path can double the build time due to unnecessary "air moves" (travel moves without extrusion).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essential**. Memorise with units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{H}{h}$ | Number of layers from part height $H$ and layer thickness $h$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | $V_{\text{part}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i \cdot h$ | Discretised part volume via summed layer areas | mm³ |
| 3 | $V_{\text{material}} = V_{\text{shell}} + f \cdot V_{\text{infill region}}$ | Material consumed (shell + infill fraction $f$) | mm³ |
| 4 | $L_{\text{filament}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{material}}}{A_{\text{fil}}} = \dfrac{4 \, V_{\text{material}}}{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}$ | Length of feedstock filament extruded | mm |
| 5 | $A_{\text{fil}} = \dfrac{\pi d_f^{\,2}}{4}$ | Cross-section area of filament (diameter $d_f$) | mm² |
| 6 | $T_{\text{build}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{material}}}{Q} = \dfrac{V_{\text{material}}}{v \cdot w \cdot h}$ | Build time using volumetric flow rate $Q$ | seconds |
| 7 | $Q = v \cdot w \cdot h$ | Effective volumetric material deposition rate | mm³/s |
| 8 | $R_a \approx \dfrac{h}{2}$ to $h$ | Surface roughness approximation (stair-step effect) | µm |
| 9 | $h_{\text{adaptive}} = \dfrac{C}{\kappa_{\text{local}}}$ | Adaptive slicing — layer thickness inversely proportional to local curvature $\kappa$ | mm |
| 10 | $\theta_{\text{overhang}} > 45° \Rightarrow \text{Support needed}$ | Critical overhang angle threshold | degrees |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas are used in **DFAM (Design for Additive Manufacturing)** to estimate cost, lead time, and material consumption *before* a part is actually printed — a critical skill in industry quotation and process planning.

## 2.3 Classification of Slicing Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks to **compare** these three classes — they are high-yield.

### (a) Uniform Slicing
- All layers have **identical thickness** $h = $ constant.
- *Pros:* Simple, fast slicer computation.
- *Cons:* Wastes time on flat regions (over-deposition of fine detail) and produces poor surface quality on steeply curved regions.

### (b) Adaptive Slicing
- Layer thickness **varies** based on local surface geometry.
- Flat regions → **thick** layers (faster).
- Curved/sloped regions → **thin** layers (better surface).
- *Pros:* Optimal time-vs-quality trade-off.
- *Cons:* Anisotropic mechanical properties due to variable layer bonding.

### (c) Curved-Layer (Non-Planar) Slicing
- Layers follow the **curvature of the part** rather than the build platform.
- Requires **multi-axis (5-axis) printers**.
- *Pros:* Eliminates the stair-step effect almost entirely.
- *Cons:* Highly complex toolpath planning; not yet mainstream.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Number of Layers

**Given:** A part of vertical build height $H$ (mm); user-selected layer thickness $h$ (mm).

**Step 1 — Define the slicing plane equation.**
The $i$-th slicing plane is at elevation:
$$z_i = z_0 + i \cdot h, \quad i \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the bounds.**
The first layer is at $z_0$ (typically $z_0 = h$, the first deposited layer), and the last layer is at $z_N \leq z_0 + H$.

**Step 3 — Solve for $N$.**
Setting $z_N = H$ (treating $z_0 = 0$ for simplicity):

$$z_N = N \cdot h = H$$

**Step 4 — Isolate $N$.**
$$\boxed{N = \left\lfloor \frac{H}{h} \right\rfloor}$$

The floor function $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ ensures $N$ is an integer (we cannot print fractional layers).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Build Time Estimation for FDM Process

**Given:** FDM process with print speed $v$ (mm/s), extrusion width $w$ (mm), layer thickness $h$ (mm), and part material volume $V_{\text{mat}}$ (mm³).

**Step 1 — Volumetric deposition rate.**
The nozzle deposits material as a continuous extruded bead of rectangular cross-section ($w \times h$). For a print head moving at linear speed $v$:

$$Q = v \cdot w \cdot h \quad \left[ \frac{\text{mm}^3}{\text{s}} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Total volume to be deposited.**
Summing over all $N$ layers and all features (shells + infill + support):

$$V_{\text{mat}} = V_{\text{shell}} + V_{\text{infill}} + V_{\text{support}}$$

For a part of volume $V_{\text{part}}$ with infill density $f$ (decimal), and approximating the shell volume as $V_{\text{shell}} \approx k \cdot A_{\text{part,side}} \cdot w$ where $k$ is the number of perimeter walls:

$$V_{\text{mat}} \approx V_{\text{shell}} + f \cdot \left( V_{\text{part}} - V_{\text{shell}} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Pure extrusion time.**
$$T_{\text{print}} = \frac{V_{\text{mat}}}{Q} = \frac{V_{\text{mat}}}{v \cdot w \cdot h}$$

**Step 4 — Add non-print overheads.**
$$T_{\text{build}} = T_{\text{print}} + T_{\text{travel}} + T_{\text{heat-up}} + T_{\text{cool-down}}$$

**Final boxed expression:**
$$\boxed{T_{\text{build}} = \frac{V_{\text{shell}} + f \cdot (V_{\text{part}} - V_{\text{shell}})}{v \cdot w \cdot h} + T_{\text{overhead}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Filament Length Required

**Step 1 — Filament cross-sectional area.**
For feedstock filament of diameter $d_f$:

$$A_{\text{fil}} = \frac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Volume conservation.**
The volume of filament extruded must equal the volume of deposited material (assuming incompressibility of the melt):

$$V_{\text{fil}} = A_{\text{fil}} \cdot L_{\text{filament}} = V_{\text{mat}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for length.**
$$\boxed{L_{\text{filament}} = \frac{V_{\text{mat}}}{A_{\text{fil}}} = \frac{4 \, V_{\text{mat}}}{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A solid cylindrical part of diameter $D = 50$ mm and height $H = 80$ mm is to be printed in PLA using FDM. Calculate: (i) number of layers, (ii) filament length, (iii) estimated build time.
>
> **Data:** $h = 0.2$ mm, $d_f = 1.75$ mm, $w = 0.4$ mm, $v = 50$ mm/s, infill $f = 0.20$, 2 perimeter walls, $T_{\text{overhead}} = 300$ s.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Part volume.**
$$V_{\text{part}} = \frac{\pi \, D^2}{4} \cdot H = \frac{\pi \cdot 50^2}{4} \cdot 80 = 50\,000 \pi \approx 157\,080 \; \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Shell volume (2 outer walls).**
Outer radius $R = 25$ mm; inner shell radius $R_{\text{shell}} = R - 2w = 25 - 0.8 = 24.2$ mm.

$$V_{\text{shell}} = \pi \left( R^2 - R_{\text{shell}}^2 \right) \cdot H = \pi \cdot (625 - 585.64) \cdot 80 = 3\,141.6 \pi \approx 9\,869 \; \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Material volume.**
$$V_{\text{mat}} = V_{\text{shell}} + f \cdot (V_{\text{part}} - V_{\text{shell}}) = 9\,869 + 0.20 \cdot (157\,080 - 9\,869)$$
$$V_{\text{mat}} = 9\,869 + 0.20 \cdot 147\,211 = 9\,869 + 29\,442 = 39\,311 \; \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Number of layers.**
$$N = \frac{H}{h} = \frac{80}{0.2} = 400 \; \text{layers}$$

**Step 5 — Filament length.**
$$L_{\text{filament}} = \frac{4 \cdot 39\,311}{\pi \cdot (1.75)^2} = \frac{157\,244}{9.621} \approx 16\,344 \; \text{mm} \approx 16.34 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Volumetric flow rate.**
$$Q = v \cdot w \cdot h = 50 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.2 = 4 \; \text{mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 7 — Build time.**
$$T_{\text{print}} = \frac{39\,311}{4} = 9\,828 \; \text{s} \approx 2.73 \; \text{hours}$$
$$T_{\text{build}} = 9\,828 + 300 = 10\,128 \; \text{s} \approx 2.81 \; \text{hours}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $N = 400$ layers
- $L_{\text{filament}} \approx 16.34$ m
- $T_{\text{build}} \approx 2.81$ hours

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slicing Algorithm Trade-off Table** — Frequently asked in KTU ESE.

| Parameter | Uniform | Adaptive | Curved-Layer |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------------|
| Layer thickness | Constant | Variable | Non-planar |
| Slicer complexity | **Low** | Medium | **High** |
| Hardware needed | Standard 3-axis | Standard 3-axis | **5-axis** |
| Surface finish | Moderate | Good | **Excellent** |
| Build time | Slow (always fine) | **Optimised** | Variable |
| Industrial maturity | **Mature** | Mature | Research-stage |
| Stair-step effect | Visible | Reduced | **Negligible** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — End-to-End Slicing Workflow Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1["CAD Model (Solid)"] --> n2["STL / 3MF / OBJ Export"]
    n2 --> n3{"Mesh Validation"}
    n3 -->|Errors| n4["Repair via Meshmixer / Netfabb"]
    n4 --> n3
    n3 -->|Valid| n5["Part Orientation and Placement"]
    n5 --> n6["Slicer Input Settings"]
    n6 --> n7["Layer Thickness h and Resolution"]
    n7 --> n8["Slicing Plane Mesh Intersection"]
    n8 --> n9["Contour Generation and Classification"]
    n9 --> n10["Infill Pattern Tiling"]
    n10 --> n11["Support Structure Synthesis"]
    n11 --> n12["Toolpath Optimisation TSP Solver"]
    n12 --> n13["G-Code / M-Code Generation"]
    n13 --> n14["Printer Firmware Execution"]
    n14 --> n15["Layer by Layer Deposition"]
    n15 --> n16["Finished 3D Part"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Slicer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP["INPUT MODULE"]
        ia["Mesh Loader"]
        ib["Mesh Repair Engine"]
        ic["Orientation Optimiser"]
    end

    subgraph PROC["PROCESSING MODULE"]
        pa["Slicing Engine"]
        pb["Contour Classifier"]
        pc["Infill Generator"]
        pd["Support Generator"]
        pe["Path Planner TSP"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT MODULE"]
        oa["G-Code Writer"]
        ob["Preview Renderer"]
        oc["Cost and Time Estimator"]
    end

    ia --> ib --> ic --> pa
    pa --> pb --> pc
    pb --> pd
    pc --> pe
    pd --> pe
    pe --> oa
    pe --> ob
    pa --> oc
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Classification Tree of Slicing Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    r["Slicing Algorithms"]
    r --> a1["Uniform Constant h"]
    r --> a2["Adaptive Variable h"]
    r --> a3["Curved Non-Planar"]

    a2 --> a2a["Curvature Based"]
    a2 --> a2b["Volume Error Based"]
    a2 --> a2c["STL Facet Normal Based"]

    a3 --> a3a["5 Axis Conformal"]
    a3 --> a3b["Field Driven Methods"]

    a1 --> a1a["Fixed Step in Z"]
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Stair-Step Effect Visualisation

```
   Side view of a curved surface approximated by layers:

   Ideal:        Approximated (3 layers):
                  ___________________
                 /                   /  ← top of layer 3
                /                   /
       _____   /                   /  ← top of layer 2
      /     \ /                   /
     /       X                   /  ← top of layer 1
    /       / \_________________/
   /       /   ↑   ↑   ↑   ↑   ↑
  Actual  Stair-step error (e)  ↑
  curve   |e| approx h/2 to h    Layer thickness h
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The vertical error $e$ (stair-step) is bounded by $\vert e \vert \le h$ and is worst on surfaces inclined near $45°$. This is the *physical motivation* for adaptive and curved-layer slicing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define slicing in additive manufacturing. List any four parameters that a slicer software typically allows the user to configure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Slicing* is the process of digitally dissecting a 3D CAD model (in STL/3MF format) into a series of 2D horizontal cross-sections of defined thickness $h$, and converting each layer into machine-readable G-code for layer-by-layer fabrication **(1.5 marks)**.
Four key user-configurable parameters are:
1. **Layer thickness $h$** (e.g., 0.1 mm, 0.2 mm, 0.3 mm) **(0.5)**
2. **Print speed $v$** (mm/s) **(0.5)**
3. **Infill density and pattern** (% and geometry) **(0.5)**
4. **Support structure generation / Overhang angle threshold** **(0.5)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between uniform slicing and adaptive slicing. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Uniform Slicing | Adaptive Slicing |
|---------|----------------|------------------|
| Layer thickness | Constant $h$ | Variable $h(z)$ |
| Slicer complexity | Low | Medium |
| Surface quality | Uniform | Optimised (thin on curves) |
| **Advantage** | Simplicity and predictable mechanical behaviour **(1.5)** | Saves build time on flat regions while preserving quality on curved regions **(1.5)** |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Choice A

#### Question A(a) — 7 marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain in detail the various slicing algorithms used in additive manufacturing. Compare uniform, adaptive, and curved-layer slicing with suitable diagrams.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Uniform (Constant-thickness) Slicing (2 marks)**
The slicing engine intersects the STL mesh with planes $z_i = i \cdot h$ at constant spacing $h$. Every layer has identical $Z$ height. This is the default mode in almost all consumer slicers (Cura, PrusaSlicer, Slic3r). It is computationally simple — the plane–mesh intersection reduces to a 2D polygon clipping per layer. The build time is linear in $N = H/h$, and mechanical properties are *isotropic* in the build plane. Its major weakness is that fine surface detail in highly curved regions is over-resolved, while flat regions are under-resolved, leading to **wasted time on flat zones and visible stair-stepping on slopes near $45°$**.

**2. Adaptive Slicing (3 marks)**
In adaptive slicing, $h$ is *not* constant; instead, it is determined locally by a *geometric criterion*:
- **Curvature-based:** $h_i = \dfrac{C}{\kappa_i}$, where $\kappa_i$ is the local Gaussian curvature of the surface. Steep curvature → thin layer.
- **Volume-error-based:** $h$ is increased until the volume error between the ideal surface and the layered approximation exceeds a user tolerance $\varepsilon$.
- **Facet-normal-based:** the angle between the local STL facet normal and the $Z$ axis is used to assign layer thickness.

The benefit is a **20–40 % reduction in build time** for curved parts while maintaining equivalent surface quality to a fine uniform slice. The drawback is *anisotropic* layer bonding — variable $h$ creates discontinuities in inter-layer adhesion, which can be a concern in functional parts.

**3. Curved-Layer Slicing (2 marks)**
Here, the slicing surfaces are *not* horizontal planes; they are **curved surfaces** that conform to the geometry of the part. This requires a 5-axis (or robotic) AM system where the deposition head can tilt. The stair-step effect is essentially **eliminated** because the layer follows the part's own surface. Toolpath planning is far more complex, requiring inverse kinematics, collision detection, and continuously varying feedrates. This method is still largely in the research domain (Lockheed Martin, University of Texas at El Paso, and similar groups) and is not yet commercial.

---

#### Question A(b) — 7 marks
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Derive an expression for the total build time of an FDM part. State clearly all assumptions and define each symbol.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Steady-state extrusion with no under/over-extrusion.
2. Uniform layer thickness $h$ (uniform slicing).
3. Constant linear print speed $v$ during extrusion.
4. Travel moves (non-print) are accounted for in $T_{\text{overhead}}$.
5. Incompressible melt (filament volume = deposited volume).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

Step 1: Number of layers
$$N = \frac{H}{h}$$

Step 2: Material volume (shell + infill)
$$V_{\text{mat}} = V_{\text{shell}} + f(V_{\text{part}} - V_{\text{shell}})$$

Step 3: Volumetric flow rate
$$Q = v \cdot w \cdot h$$

Step 4: Pure deposition time
$$T_{\text{print}} = \frac{V_{\text{mat}}}{Q} = \frac{V_{\text{shell}} + f(V_{\text{part}} - V_{\text{shell}})}{v \, w \, h}$$

Step 5: Total build time
$$\boxed{T_{\text{build}} = \frac{V_{\text{shell}} + f(V_{\text{part}} - V_{\text{shell}})}{v \, w \, h} + T_{\text{overhead}}}$$

**Symbol definitions (additional credit):**
- $H$ = part height, $h$ = layer thickness, $V_{\text{part}}$ = part CAD volume, $V_{\text{shell}}$ = perimeter shell volume, $f$ = infill density (decimal), $v$ = print speed, $w$ = extrusion width, $T_{\text{overhead}}$ = sum of heat-up, cool-down, and travel times.

---

### Choice B (Alternative)

#### Question B(a) — 7 marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alt]**
**With a neat block diagram, describe the complete workflow of a slicer software from STL import to G-code generation. Mention at least six key stages.**

**Model Solution:**

The slicer workflow consists of the following sequential stages (see Section 4.2 for the block diagram, 1 mark for diagram + 6 marks for explanation):

1. **STL/3MF Import (1 mark):** The triangulated mesh is loaded and a bounding box is computed. The mesh is checked for watertightness, manifold consistency, and triangle normal orientation. Corrupted meshes trigger automatic repair (e.g., hole filling in Meshmixer).

2. **Orientation & Scaling (0.5 mark):** The part is rotated and translated on the build platform. Optimal orientation minimises support volume and maximises the $Z$ height where the part has uniform cross-section.

3. **Print Parameter Definition (1 mark):** The user sets $h$, $v$, $w$, nozzle/bed temperature, retraction, cooling fan duty cycle, infill density $f$, perimeter count, and support thresholds.

4. **Slicing & Contour Generation (1.5 marks):** Plane–mesh Boolean intersection yields closed 2D polylines per layer. These are classified into outer boundaries, inner holes, and islands using even-odd or non-zero winding number rules.

5. **Infill Generation (0.5 mark):** A 2D pattern (rectilinear, honeycomb, gyroid, etc.) is mathematically tiled within the closed contours.

6. **Support Generation (0.5 mark):** Overhang geometry (faces with normal $\vec{n}$ tilted more than $45°$ from vertical) is identified; tree-like or grid scaffolds are synthesised beneath it.

7. **Path Planning & G-code Output (1 mark):** The print head's traversal order is solved as a TSP variant to minimise air-time. Each segment is encoded with $X$, $Y$, $Z$, $E$ (extrusion), $F$ (feedrate), and M-codes for temperature and fan.

---

#### Question B(b) — 7 marks
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alt]**
**A cylindrical part of diameter $D = 50$ mm and height $H = 80$ mm is to be printed in ABS using FDM. Calculate the number of layers, total filament length, and estimated build time.**

**Given:** $h = 0.2$ mm, infill $f = 0.20$, $w = 0.4$ mm, $v = 50$ mm/s, $d_f = 1.75$ mm, 2 perimeter walls, $T_{\text{overhead}} = 300$ s. Assume material density is irrelevant since volume is conserved.

**Model Solution (use the derivation of Section 3.4):**

**Step 1 — Part volume (0.5 mark)**
$$V_{\text{part}} = \frac{\pi \, (50)^2}{4} \cdot 80 = 50\,000\pi \approx 157\,080 \; \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Shell volume, 2 walls (1 mark)**
$$V_{\text{shell}} = \pi (25^2 - 24.2^2) \cdot 80 = \pi (625 - 585.64)(80) \approx 9\,869 \; \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Material volume with 20 % infill (1 mark)**
$$V_{\text{mat}} = 9\,869 + 0.20 \cdot (157\,080 - 9\,869) = 39\,311 \; \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Number of layers (0.5 mark)**
$$N = \frac{80}{0.2} = 400 \; \text{layers}$$

**Step 5 — Filament length (1.5 marks)**
$$L_{\text{filament}} = \frac{4 \, V_{\text{mat}}}{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}} = \frac{4 \cdot 39\,311}{\pi \cdot (1.75)^2} = \frac{157\,244}{9.621} \approx 16\,344 \; \text{mm} \approx 16.34 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Volumetric flow rate (0.5 mark)**
$$Q = v \, w \, h = 50 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.2 = 4 \; \text{mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 7 — Build time (2 marks)**
$$T_{\text{print}} = \frac{V_{\text{mat}}}{Q} = \frac{39\,311}{4} = 9\,828 \; \text{s}$$
$$T_{\text{build}} = 9\,828 + 300 = 10\,128 \; \text{s} \approx 2.81 \; \text{hours}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $N = 400$ layers
- $L_{\text{filament}} \approx 16.34$ m
- $T_{\text{build}} \approx 2.81$ hours

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Slicing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the floor function** when writing $N = H/h$ — partial credit lost if $N$ is left as a decimal.
> 2. **Omitting $T_{\text{overhead}}$** in build-time derivations — this is a 1-mark deduction trap in 7-mark questions.
> 3. **Mixing units** — speed in mm/s, dimensions in mm, volume in mm³. *Inconsistent units = 0 marks for the numerical answer.*
> 4. **Confusing shell volume with perimeter length** — $V_{\text{shell}}$ is volumetric, not linear.
> 5. **Stating $N$ without defining $H$ and $h$** — KTU examiners expect *symbol definitions* alongside every formula.
> 6. **Drawing stair-step diagrams with slopes upside-down** — the stair-step is *vertical on top of the layer*, not below.
> 7. **Skipping the diagram** in a 7-mark "explain with diagram" question — *at least 1.5 marks* are reserved for the figure.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Definition:** Slicing = 3D-to-2D layer decomposition + toolpath generation → G-code.
> - **Master formula:** $N = H / h$ — number of layers from part height and layer thickness.
> - **Filament length:** $L = 4V / (\pi d_f^2)$ — derived from volume conservation.
> - **Build time:** $T = V_{\text{mat}} / (v \cdot w \cdot h) + T_{\text{overhead}}$.
> - **Three slicing classes:** Uniform (constant $h$), Adaptive (variable $h$ via curvature/volume-error/normal criteria), Curved (non-planar, 5-axis).
> - **Stair-step error:** $\vert e \vert \le h$; worst at $45°$ slopes; reduced by adaptive and eliminated by curved-layer slicing.
> - **Overhang threshold:** $45°$ from vertical — beyond this, supports are mandatory.
> - **Standard layer thickness range:** **0.05 – 0.4 mm**; finer = better surface, longer build time.
> - **Slicer software ecosystem:** Ultimaker Cura, PrusaSlicer, Simplify3D, IdeaMaker, Bambu Studio, Slic3r.
> - **File formats:** STL (legacy, ASCII/binary), 3MF (modern, supports colour/material metadata), OBJ, AMF.
> - **Path optimisation:** Toolpath planning reduces non-print travel time — solves a TSP variant.
> - **G-code structure:** $X$, $Y$, $Z$ coordinates + $E$ (extrusion) + $F$ (feedrate) + M-codes (temperature/fan).
> - **Industry link:** Slicing is the **bridge** between digital design and physical fabrication — mastery of this topic is essential for DFAM (Design for Additive Manufacturing) practice.
> - **Exam mantra:** *"Always state units, define symbols, and draw the diagram."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tool Path Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET525 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING AND 3D PRINTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tool Path Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tool Path Generation** in Additive Manufacturing (AM) is the computational process of determining the precise spatial trajectory, velocity profile, and deposition parameters that the print head, extruder, laser, or energy source must follow to fabricate a three-dimensional object layer-by-layer from a sliced digital model. It is the critical bridge between a digitally sliced 2D layer geometry and the physical motion commands (typically G-code) executed by the AM machine.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, tool path generation is positioned as a sub-module of **Part 2: Software Aspects of Additive Manufacturing**, and forms the computational heart of the slicing software (e.g., **Cura**, **Slic3r**, **PrusaSlicer**, **Simplify3D**). The tool path is mathematically described as a parametric curve $C(s)$ in 2D (per layer) or 3D (multi-layer), where $s$ is the arc length parameter, governed by a velocity function $v(s)$ and a material flow rate function $Q(s)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Tool Path Generation as a "GPS Route Planner for a Pen that draws in 3D Plastic."**

Imagine you have a hot glue gun and a fully grown 3D cake shaped like a toy castle. The cake is sliced horizontally into thin paper-thin layers. Now imagine you must trace each layer outline and then fill the inside with a pattern, just like colouring inside a colouring book — but you cannot lift the pen, you must travel at a constant speed, and the pen must ooze out the exact right amount of plastic at every millimetre so that the layer is neither too thin (weak) nor too thick (rough). 

The **recipe of WHERE to go, HOW FAST to move, and HOW MUCH to extrude at each point** is the **Tool Path**. The software that computes this recipe is the **Slicer** (or tool path generator). The final written instructions are called **G-code**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Used in KTU Syllabus

| Term | Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) |
|------|----------------------------------|
| **Slicing** | The decomposition of a 3D STL mesh into a stack of 2D horizontal cross-sections of uniform thickness $h_l$. |
| **Hatch Spacing ($d_h$)** | The lateral distance between two adjacent parallel scan vectors, typically equal to the line width $w$. |
| **Scan Vector** | A straight-line tool path segment within a single layer, parameterised by start and end coordinates. |
| **Contour** | The boundary scan of a layer, traced just inside the perimeter to ensure dimensional accuracy. |
| **Infill Pattern** | The internal tool path geometry (e.g., rectilinear, honeycomb, gyroid) that determines part density. |
| **G-code** | The numerical control (NC) language used to command the AM machine's motors and extruder. |
| **Volumetric Flow Rate ($Q$)** | The volume of material deposited per unit time, measured in **mm³/s**. |
| **Layer Height ($h_l$)** | The vertical thickness of each deposited layer, typically between **0.05 mm and 0.4 mm** for FDM. |
| **Line Width ($w$)** | The lateral width of a single deposited bead, normally equal to the nozzle diameter $\pm 10\%$. |

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

Tool path generation directly determines the four pillars of AM quality:
1. **Dimensional Accuracy** — Path precision controls geometric tolerance.
2. **Surface Finish** — Contour strategy and layer height dictate surface roughness ($R_a$).
3. **Mechanical Strength** — Infill pattern and raster orientation affect anisotropy.
4. **Build Time & Cost** — Path length and scan speed determine productivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Metric:** A well-optimized tool path can reduce build time by **15–30%** and material consumption by **5–12%** without compromising part quality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tool Path Generation Pipeline (5 Logical Phases)

### **Phase 1: Model Ingestion and Tessellation**
- The 3D CAD model (STEP/IGES) is converted to an **STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** mesh, which approximates the surface using triangular facets.
- Mathematical representation: A triangular mesh $\mathcal{M} = \{V, E, F\}$ where $V$ = vertices, $E$ = edges, $F$ = faces.
- Each triangle is checked for **manifold errors** (non-manifold edges, inverted normals, holes).

### **Phase 2: Layer Slicing**
- A horizontal plane $z = z_k$ (where $z_k = k \cdot h_l$, $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n-1$) intersects the mesh.
- The intersection produces **2D closed polygons** (sliced contours).
- The number of layers is computed as:
  
  $$n = \left\lceil \frac{H_{total}}{h_l} \right\rceil$$

  where $H_{total}$ is the total part height, and $h_l$ is the layer height.

### **Phase 3: Tool Path Planning (Per Layer)**
This phase generates the actual scan vectors. The slicer must decide:
- **Where the contour is** (perimeter offsets inward by $w/2$).
- **What infill pattern to use** (rectilinear, hexagonal, gyroid, lightning).
- **What is the path ordering** (travel path optimization).
- **Where to deposit supports** (overhangs > 45° typically).

### **Phase 4: Parameterization and G-code Synthesis**
- Each scan vector is converted to a series of G-code commands:
  - `G1 X{x} Y{y} E{e}` → Linear move with extrusion.
  - `G0 X{x} Y{y}` → Rapid (non-extruding) travel move.
  - `M104 S{t}` → Set extruder temperature.
- The extrusion length $e$ per move is calculated to match the commanded volumetric flow.

### **Phase 5: Process Parameter Optimization**
- Adaptive slicing: Layer height $h_l$ is varied with geometry curvature.
- Path planning: Minimization of total path length $L_{total} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} L_k$ and travel moves.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are EXAM-CRITICAL and appear frequently in KTU assessments.**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $n = \left\lceil H_{total} / h_l \right\rceil$ | Number of layers | $H_{total}$ in **mm**, $h_l$ in **mm** |
| 2 | $Q = w \cdot h_l \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow rate (continuity) | $Q$ in **mm³/s**, $w$ in **mm**, $v$ in **mm/s** |
| 3 | $e = \frac{Q \cdot \Delta t}{A_{filament}}$ | Extrusion length per move | $e$ in **mm**, $A_{filament}$ in **mm²** |
| 4 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = \rho \cdot w \cdot h_l \cdot v$ | Mass deposition rate | $\dot{m}$ in **g/s**, $\rho$ in **g/cm³** |
| 5 | $T_{build} = \frac{V_{part}}{Q_{avg}} + T_{setup}$ | Total build time | $V_{part}$ in **mm³**, $Q_{avg}$ in **mm³/s** |
| 6 | $L_k = \sum_{i=1}^{N_v} \ell_i$ | Total path length of layer $k$ | $L_k$ in **mm**, $N_v$ = number of scan vectors |
| 7 | $t_k = \frac{L_k}{v} + N_{travel} \cdot t_{travel}$ | Time to print layer $k$ | $t_k$ in **s**, $N_{travel}$ = travel moves |
| 8 | $\eta_{material} = \frac{V_{infill}}{V_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Material efficiency | Dimensionless **%** |
| 9 | $R_a \approx 0.25 \cdot h_l$ (for 45° surface) | Surface roughness approx. | $R_a$ in **µm**, $h_l$ in **mm** |
| 10 | $d_{hatch} = w$ (typical) | Hatch spacing (line width) | $d_{hatch}$ in **mm** |
| 11 | $F_{bond} = f(\theta, T_{bed}, v)$ | Layer adhesion strength function | $\theta$ = raster angle, $T_{bed}$ in **°C** |
| 12 | $v_{max} = \frac{Q_{max}}{w \cdot h_l}$ | Maximum permissible scan speed | $v_{max}$ in **mm/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention:** Always convert $\rho$ to consistent units. $1\ \text{g/cm}^3 = 10^{-3}\ \text{g/mm}^3 = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$. PLA density $\rho_{PLA} \approx 1.24\ \text{g/cm}^3$. ABS density $\rho_{ABS} \approx 1.04\ \text{g/cm}^3$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Tool Path Strategy |
|----------|-----------------------------------|
| **Aerospace (GE Aviation)** | Conformal cooling channels in LEAP fuel nozzles using spiral tool paths in DMLS. |
| **Biomedical (Patient-specific implants)** | Gyroid infill paths to mimic trabecular bone porosity for osseointegration. |
| **Automotive (Bugatti)** | Variable density tool paths for brake calipers (TI-6Al-4V, SLM). |
| **Tooling (Conformal cooling inserts)** | Tree-like (organic) support paths in PBF to minimise post-processing. |
| **Consumer 3D Printing** | Lightning infill in Bambu Studio for ultra-fast, low-material parts. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Volumetric Flow Rate — The Continuity Equation for Extrusion

The **continuity of material deposition** is the single most important principle in tool path generation. In steady state, the volume of material leaving the nozzle per unit time MUST equal the volume deposited on the build plate per unit time.

### **Step 1: Set up the geometric deposition model**

A single deposited bead is approximated as a rectangular cuboid of cross-section $w \times h_l$ and length $v \cdot \Delta t$ deposited in time $\Delta t$.

$$
V_{deposited} = w \cdot h_l \cdot v \cdot \Delta t
$$

### **Step 2: Set up the filament feed model**

The filament is fed at velocity $v_f$ through a cross-sectional area $A_{filament} = \pi d_f^2 / 4$, where $d_f$ is the filament diameter (typically **1.75 mm** or **2.85 mm**).

$$
V_{filament} = A_{filament} \cdot v_f \cdot \Delta t
$$

### **Step 3: Apply the incompressibility (conservation of volume) condition**

Since the molten polymer is incompressible inside the melt zone:

$$
V_{deposited} = V_{filament}
$$

$$
w \cdot h_l \cdot v \cdot \Delta t = A_{filament} \cdot v_f \cdot \Delta t
$$

### **Step 4: Cancel $\Delta t$ and define $Q$**

$$
Q \triangleq w \cdot h_l \cdot v = A_{filament} \cdot v_f
$$

### **Step 5: Express extrusion length per move**

For a move of duration $\Delta t = \ell / v$ (where $\ell$ is the segment length), the filament feed distance $e$ is:

$$
e = v_f \cdot \Delta t = \frac{w \cdot h_l \cdot \ell}{A_{filament}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** This is the working equation that every KTU numerical problem tests. The slicer solves this equation at every line of G-code.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Build Time Estimation (Layer-by-Layer Sum)

### **Step 1: Decompose build time**

The total build time is the sum of layer times plus machine setup time:

$$
T_{build} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} t_k + T_{setup}
$$

### **Step 2: Per-layer time**

For a layer with total scan path length $L_k$, scan speed $v$, and $N_{travel}$ rapid travel moves each of average duration $t_{travel}$:

$$
t_k = \frac{L_k}{v} + N_{travel} \cdot t_{travel}
$$

### **Step 3: Approximation assuming uniform layers**

If the part has near-uniform cross-section (prismatic approximation), $L_k \approx L_{avg}$ for all $k$:

$$
T_{build} \approx n \cdot \left( \frac{L_{avg}}{v} + N_{travel} \cdot t_{travel} \right) + T_{setup}
$$

### **Step 4: Substitute $n$**

$$
T_{build} \approx \frac{H_{total}}{h_l} \cdot \left( \frac{L_{avg}}{v} + N_{travel} \cdot t_{travel} \right) + T_{setup}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical 7-Mark Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A rectangular block of dimensions $80 \times 60 \times 40$ mm is to be printed in PLA using FDM. The slicer uses a rectilinear infill at 20% density, with 2 perimeter walls, layer height $h_l = 0.2$ mm, line width $w = 0.4$ mm, and scan speed $v = 60$ mm/s. Estimate: (a) Number of layers, (b) Total filament length required (assuming $\rho = 1.24$ g/cm³, $d_f = 1.75$ mm), and (c) Build time neglecting travel moves and setup.

### **Part (a) — Number of layers**

$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{H_{total}}{h_l} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{40}{0.2} \right\rceil = 200 \text{ layers}
$$

### **Part (b) — Filament length**

**Step 1:** Part volume $V_{part} = 80 \times 60 \times 40 = 192{,}000\ \text{mm}^3$.

**Step 2:** Effective material volume at 20% infill + 2 walls. The 2 walls contribute $\approx 5\%$ shell volume, so effective fraction $\approx 0.20 + 0.05 = 0.25$ (approximate; exact requires CAD).

$$
V_{effective} = 0.25 \times 192{,}000 = 48{,}000\ \text{mm}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to filament length using $V = A_f \cdot L$:

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (1.75)^2}{4} = 2.405\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
L_{filament} = \frac{V_{effective}}{A_f} = \frac{48{,}000}{2.405} = 19{,}958\ \text{mm} \approx 19.96\ \text{m}
$$

### **Part (c) — Build time**

**Step 1:** Approximate path length per layer (infill + perimeter).

For one layer: ~80 mm of perimeter top + ~80 mm bottom + ~60 mm × 2 = 280 mm perimeter total.
Infill scan lines: 80 mm / 0.4 mm = 200 lines × 60 mm = 12,000 mm per layer.
Total $L_{avg} \approx 12{,}280$ mm per layer.

**Step 2:**

$$
T_{build} \approx n \cdot \frac{L_{avg}}{v} = 200 \times \frac{12{,}280}{60} = 40{,}933\ \text{s} \approx 11.37\ \text{hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The volume ratio check gives $192{,}000 / (0.4 \times 0.2 \times 12{,}280) \approx 195$ layers worth of full volume. The 200 layers × 0.25 fill = 50 full-layer-equivalents, and $50 \times 12{,}280\ \text{mm} = 614{,}000\ \text{mm}$ of path. At 60 mm/s → 10,233 s. The discrepancy comes from the inner cavity being smaller; the more accurate method is to integrate the actual 2D area per layer.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Tool Path Strategies

| Strategy | Pattern Geometry | Strength | Anisotropy | Best Use Case |
|----------|------------------|----------|------------|---------------|
| **Rectilinear** | Parallel lines, ±90° alternating | High speed, simple | High (cross-direction weak) | Concept models, fast prototypes |
| **Contour + Zigzag** | Perimeter + back-and-forth | Uniform strength | Moderate | General purpose |
| **Honeycomb (Hexagonal)** | 6-sided cells | High stiffness-to-weight | Low | Structural components |
| **Gyroid** | Triply periodic minimal surface | Isotropic, lightweight | Very low | Biomedical, aerospace |
| **Lightning** | Tree-like branching | Ultra-fast, minimal material | Very high | Visual prototypes, low load |
| **Spiral (Vase Mode)** | Continuous helix | Watertight, no seams | N/A (single wall) | Vases, tubes, pressure vessels |
| **Concentric** | Nested perimeters | Stiff in Z, edge-strong | Moderate | Casting patterns |
| **Hilbert Curve** | Space-filling fractal | Uniform thermal | Very low | High-quality surface parts |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tool Path Generation Pipeline — Process Flow

The following block diagram illustrates the complete tool path generation pipeline as implemented in commercial slicers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD MODEL STEP OR IGES] --> B[STL EXPORT TRIANGULATED MESH]
    B --> C[SLICER SOFTWARE INPUT]
    C --> D[SLICING ENGINE LAYER BY LAYER]
    D --> E[CONTOUR GENERATION PERIMETER OFFSET]
    D --> F[INFILL PATTERN GENERATION]
    D --> G[SUPPORT STRUCTURE GENERATION]
    E --> H[TOOL PATH PLANNER]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[PATH OPTIMIZATION TRAVEL MINIMIZATION]
    I --> J[G CODE SYNTHESIS]
    J --> K[AM MACHINE EXECUTION]
    K --> L[3D PRINTED PART]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style E fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000000
    style F fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000000
    style G fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000000
    style H fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff

    style L fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Tool Path Patterns — Visual Comparison Block

```mermaid

    P1[RECTILINEAR GRID 0 90]:::cat1
    P2[HONEYCOMB HEXAGONAL]:::cat2

    P2 --> COMP

    COMP --> O1[SPEED]
    COMP --> O2[STRENGTH]
    COMP --> O3[MATERIAL USE]
    COMP --> O4[ISOTROPY]

```

## 4.3 G-code Synthesis — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[SCAN VECTOR LIST X Y Z LENGTH SPEED] --> CALC[VOLUMETRIC FLOW COMPUTER Q W H V]
    CALC --> EXTRUDE[EXTRUSION CALCULATOR E Q DT AF]
    EXTRUDE --> TEMP[TEMPERATURE COMMAND M104 M109]
    EXTRUDE --> MOVE[G1 LINEAR MOVE WITH EXTRUSION]
    CALC --> RAPID[G0 RAPID TRAVEL NO EXTRUSION]
    MOVE --> GCODE[NC FILE G CODE OUTPUT]
    RAPID --> GCODE
    TEMP --> GCODE
    GCODE --> PRINTER[3D PRINTER CONTROLLER]

    style INPUT fill:#264478,color:#ffffff
    style CALC fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style EXTRUDE fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style MOVE fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000
    style RAPID fill:#ffc000,color:#000000
    style GCODE fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style PRINTER fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    style TEMP fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Adaptive Slicing — Variable Layer Height Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MESH[STL MESH] --> CURV[CURVATURE ANALYZER PER VERTEX]
    CURV --> DECISION{REGION TYPE}
    DECISION -->|HIGH CURVATURE| THIN[H LAYER 0.05 MM FINE DETAIL]
    DECISION -->|LOW CURVATURE| THICK[H LAYER 0.30 MM FAST BUILD]
    DECISION -->|MEDIUM CURVATURE| MED[H LAYER 0.15 MM BALANCED]
    THIN --> PATHA[TOOL PATH FOR FINE REGION]
    THICK --> PATHB[TOOL PATH FOR COARSE REGION]
    MED --> PATHC[TOOL PATH FOR BALANCED REGION]
    PATHA --> MERGE[MERGED LAYER SET]
    PATHB --> MERGE
    PATHC --> MERGE
    MERGE --> GCODE[UNIFIED G CODE]

    style MESH fill:#264478,color:#ffffff
    style CURV fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style DECISION fill:#ffc000,color:#000000
    style THIN fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000
    style THICK fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000
    style MED fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000
    style MERGE fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style GCODE fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The orange-coloured blocks represent the **decision/computation logic** of the slicer, while the green blocks represent the **output tool path regions**. The red block is the final unified G-code that is sent to the machine.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define tool path generation in the context of additive manufacturing. List any four commonly used infill patterns.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tool path generation is the computational procedure that determines the geometric trajectory, velocity, and material deposition rate of the print head or energy source to fabricate a sliced 2D layer on the build platform, producing a 3D part layer by layer.

Four commonly used infill patterns are:
1. **Rectilinear** (parallel lines at 0°/90°)
2. **Honeycomb** (hexagonal cells)
3. **Gyroid** (triply periodic minimal surface)
4. **Lightning** (tree-like branching for ultra-fast low-material fills)

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each × 4 = 2 marks for patterns = 3 marks total)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is adaptive slicing? How does it improve build time and surface quality simultaneously?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Adaptive slicing is a tool path optimization technique in which the layer height $h_l$ is varied based on the local surface curvature of the part, rather than being held constant throughout the build.

- In **regions of high curvature** (e.g., fillets, organic surfaces), thin layers ($h_l \approx 0.05$ mm) are used → **improved surface finish** (lower $R_a$).
- In **regions of low curvature** (e.g., flat prismatic faces), thick layers ($h_l \approx 0.30$ mm) are used → **reduced layer count and faster build**.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for high-curvature explanation + 1 mark for low-curvature explanation = 3 marks total)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **ONE** question from the choice. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Full internal choice is provided.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the complete tool path generation pipeline for an FDM process with a neat block diagram. Discuss in detail the slicing and contour generation stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical part of diameter 50 mm and height 30 mm is to be printed in ABS ($\rho = 1.04$ g/cm³) with the following slicer settings: nozzle diameter $= 0.4$ mm, layer height $= 0.2$ mm, scan speed $= 50$ mm/s, infill density $= 25\%$, 2 perimeter walls. The filament diameter is 1.75 mm. Calculate:
- (i) Number of layers
- (ii) Total volume of material deposited
- (iii) Approximate build time **(7 marks)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the following infill strategies in terms of strength, isotropy, build time, and material consumption: Rectilinear, Honeycomb, Gyroid, and Lightning. State one specific industrial application for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the volumetric flow rate equation $Q = w \cdot h_l \cdot v$ from the principle of incompressibility of the polymer melt. Using this equation, determine the maximum permissible scan speed for a printer whose extruder can supply a maximum volumetric flow rate of $Q_{max} = 12$ mm³/s, with a layer height of 0.2 mm and line width of 0.4 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions — Step-by-Step

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Tool Path Generation Pipeline (7 marks)**

**Step 1: STL Ingestion (1 mark)**
The CAD model is tessellated into triangular facets. Each triangle has vertices and a normal vector. The slicer validates manifold integrity.

**Step 2: Slicing (2 marks)**
A horizontal plane $z = k \cdot h_l$ intersects the mesh. The intersection algorithm (e.g., marching squares for closed contours) extracts 2D polygons for each layer. The number of layers is:

$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{H_{total}}{h_l} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3: Contour Generation (2 marks)**
The outer boundary of the sliced polygon is offset inward by the line width $w$ to create multiple concentric perimeters. The offset operation uses the **Voronoi diagram** or **clipper library** to handle sharp convex/concave corners with miter joints.

**Step 4: Infill and Path Synthesis (2 marks)**
The internal area is filled with a pattern (rectilinear, honeycomb, etc.) at the user-specified density. Scan vectors are connected using **nearest-neighbour TSP heuristic** to minimize rapid travel. G-code is generated with commands `G1`, `G0`, `M104`, `M109`.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Number of layers:**

$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{30}{0.2} \right\rceil = 150 \text{ layers}
$$

**(ii) Total material volume:**

- Part volume: $V_{part} = \pi r^2 H = \pi \times 25^2 \times 30 = 58{,}904.86\ \text{mm}^3$
- 2 perimeter walls volume (approx.): The shell area per layer $\approx 2 \times \pi r \times w = 2 \times \pi \times 25 \times 0.4 = 62.83\ \text{mm}^2$ per layer. Over 150 layers: $62.83 \times 150 = 9{,}425\ \text{mm}^3$.
- Infill volume: $0.25 \times (58{,}904.86 - 9{,}425) = 0.25 \times 49{,}479.86 = 12{,}370\ \text{mm}^3$
- Total $V_{material} = 9{,}425 + 12{,}370 = 21{,}795\ \text{mm}^3$ ≈ **21,795 mm³**

**(iii) Approximate build time:**

- Approximate path length per layer: Perimeter $\approx 2 \pi r \times 2 = 314.16$ mm + Infill lines $(50/w) \times 50 = 125 \times 50 = 6{,}250$ mm → Total $\approx 6{,}564$ mm.
- Per layer time: $t_k = 6{,}564 / 50 = 131.28$ s
- Total: $T = 150 \times 131.28 = 19{,}692\ \text{s} \approx 5.47\ \text{hours}$

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks = 1.75 per row)**

| Infill | Strength | Isotropy | Build Time | Material Use | Application |
|--------|----------|----------|------------|--------------|-------------|
| Rectilinear | Moderate | Low (alternating 0°/90°) | Fast | Low (sparse friendly) | Concept prototypes (Ultimaker test coupons) |
| Honeycomb | High | High (multi-direction) | Medium | Medium | Aerospace brackets (CubeSat structures) |
| Gyroid | Very High (isotropic) | Very High | Slow | Medium-High | Spinal cages, aerospace lattices (Materialise) |
| Lightning | Low | Very Low (tree-like) | Ultra-fast | Very Low | Aesthetic visual prototypes (Bambu Lab) |

#### **Part (b) — Derivation + Numerical (7 marks)**

**Derivation (4 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1, the incompressibility of the polymer melt inside the liquefier gives:

$$
V_{deposited} = w \cdot h_l \cdot v \cdot \Delta t = V_{filament} = A_f \cdot v_f \cdot \Delta t
$$

Dividing by $\Delta t$ and defining $Q$ as the volumetric flow rate:

$$
Q \triangleq w \cdot h_l \cdot v
$$

**Numerical (3 marks):**

Rearranging for maximum scan speed:

$$
v_{max} = \frac{Q_{max}}{w \cdot h_l} = \frac{12}{0.4 \times 0.2} = \frac{12}{0.08} = 150\ \text{mm/s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS — COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Continuity Equation:** Students often mix mm³/s with cm³/s. Always state and convert units explicitly. **−1 to −2 marks.**
> 
> 2. **Confusing Volumetric Flow with Mass Flow:** The formula $Q = w h_l v$ is volumetric, NOT mass-based. To get $\dot{m}$, you must multiply by $\rho$. Examiners specifically test this. **−1 mark.**
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the Ceiling Function in Layer Count:** Students write $n = H/h$ instead of $\lceil H/h \rceil$. For non-integer divisions (e.g., 40.1 mm / 0.2 mm = 200.5), this is WRONG. **−0.5 to −1 mark.**
> 
> 4. **Ignoring Filament Cross-section in Length Calculation:** Converting material volume to filament length requires $A_f = \pi d_f^2 / 4$. Forgetting this is a frequent error. **−1 mark.**
> 
> 5. **Not Listing Infill Applications:** Examiners award marks for "specific industrial applications." Generic answers like "used in industry" get 0 marks. **−0.5 to −1 mark.**
> 
> 6. **Skipping the Diagram in Part (a):** KTU explicitly requires a "neat block diagram" for pipeline questions. Drawing the diagram is worth **2 of 7 marks** by itself. **−2 marks if omitted.**

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 KTU 2024 — Final Revision Snapshot for Tool Path Generation**

- ☐ **Tool path generation = slicing + contour + infill + G-code synthesis pipeline.**
- ☐ **Core equation (memorise):** $Q = w \cdot h_l \cdot v$ — this is the EXAM GOLDEN FORMULA.
- ☐ **Number of layers formula:** $n = \lceil H_{total} / h_l \rceil$ — always use the **ceiling function**.
- ☐ **Filament cross-section:** $A_f = \pi d_f^2 / 4$ — needed in EVERY numerical problem.
- ☐ **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot w \cdot h_l \cdot v$ — multiply volumetric flow by density.
- ☐ **Build time formula:** $T_{build} \approx n \cdot (L_{avg}/v)$ + setup + travel.
- ☐ **Four pillar infill patterns:** Rectilinear, Honeycomb, Gyroid, Lightning (with their application domains).
- ☐ **Adaptive slicing** = variable $h_l$ with curvature → optimizes both speed AND surface quality.
- ☐ **Standard densities:** PLA $\approx 1.24$ g/cm³, ABS $\approx 1.04$ g/cm³, PETG $\approx 1.27$ g/cm³.
- ☐ **Standard filament diameters:** 1.75 mm and 2.85 mm — these are exam-favoured numbers.
- ☐ **Hatch spacing rule:** $d_{hatch} \approx w$ (line width), typically 0.4 mm for 0.4 mm nozzles.
- ☐ **Surface roughness estimate:** $R_a \approx 0.25 \cdot h_l$ for a 45° surface (approximate).
- ☐ **Maximum scan speed constraint:** $v_{max} = Q_{max} / (w \cdot h_l)$ — never exceed extruder capacity.
- ☐ **Perimeter offset = $w/2$** inward from the polygon boundary to ensure dimensional accuracy.
- ☐ **Anisotropy in AM** is a direct consequence of unidirectional scan vectors — Gyroid paths minimize this.
- ☐ **G-code commands to remember:** `G1` (linear move), `G0` (rapid), `M104` (set temp), `M109` (wait for temp).
- ☐ **Always draw a block diagram** when asked about the tool path pipeline — it carries independent marks.
- ☐ **Internal choice is mandatory** in KTU ESE — practice both Question A and Question B.

> **END OF NOTES — Tool Path Generation | PEIET525 | Module 2 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Rules for Extrusion-Based AM, Introduction to Slicing Software - Cura
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET525 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING AND 3D PRINTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Rules for Extrusion-Based AM, Introduction to Slicing Software - Cura

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Design Rules for Extrusion-Based Additive Manufacturing

### Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

**Design Rules for Extrusion-Based AM** are a codified set of geometric, dimensional, and process-oriented guidelines that constrain the **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** model so that the part can be reliably fabricated using **Material Extrusion (MEX)** processes — primarily **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** and **Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF)**. These rules account for the layer-by-layer, bead-by-bead material deposition physics of the process and translate them into manufacturable CAD constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Core Definition:** Design rules in extrusion-based AM are NOT optional suggestions — they are **hard process constraints** derived from the nozzle diameter ($D_n$), layer height ($h_l$), and material rheology. Ignoring them leads to print failure, warping, delamination, or geometric inaccuracy.

The principal parameters governing these rules are:

- **Nozzle diameter ($D_n$)** — typically **0.4 mm** (standard) but available from **0.2 mm to 1.0 mm**.
- **Layer height ($h_l$)** — typically **0.1 mm to 0.3 mm** for FDM.
- **Filament diameter ($d_f$)** — standard **1.75 mm** or **2.85 mm**.
- **Build volume** — bounded by the printer's XYZ envelope.
- **Material glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** — governs warping and adhesion.

---

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Icing-Pipe" Viewpoint

Imagine decorating a cake with a piping bag filled with icing:

1. The **icing-pipe tip** is your **nozzle** — its opening diameter ($D_n$) determines the *minimum width* of any icing line you can lay down. You cannot draw a line thinner than the tip's exit hole.
2. Each pass of icing has a **fixed thickness (layer height)** — once it sets, you cannot make a *partial* layer; you must commit to a full height step.
3. If you try to pipe icing **out into thin air** (a long unsupported span), it droops — analogous to **overhang failure** in 3D printing.
4. The icing sticks best when the previous layer is **still slightly soft** (warm) — analogous to the requirement that the previous printed layer be **above $T_g$** for proper inter-layer diffusion bonding.

**Design rules, therefore, are simply the engineering translation of "what the icing-pipe can and cannot do" into CAD-level dimensions and orientations.**

---

### The Four Foundational Design Rule Categories

| # | Rule Category | Physical Origin | Typical KTU Value (for $D_n = 0.4$ mm) |
|---|---------------|-----------------|------------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Wall Thickness Rule** | Bead adhesion + nozzle extrusion width | Min wall $\geq 2 \times D_n = 0.8$ mm (often 1.0 mm for ABS/PLA) |
| 2 | **Overhang / Support Rule** | Gravity acting on partially supported molten polymer | Max unsupported overhang angle = **45°** from vertical (rule of thumb) |
| 3 | **Hole Diameter Rule** | Hole ovalization due to nozzle geometry & slicing path | Min hole $\geq 2 \times D_n = 0.8$ mm; horizontal holes need **+0.4 mm** compensation |
| 4 | **Bridging Rule** | Tensile strength of in-air extruded span | Max bridge length $\leq 5 \times D_n = 2$ mm without support (sag-prone beyond this) |

> [!NOTE]
> These values are **process-dependent**. A **0.25 mm nozzle** allows finer features, while a **0.8 mm nozzle** drastically restricts minimum wall thickness and feature size.

---

## 1.2 Introduction to Slicing Software — Cura

### Formal KTU Definition

A **Slicer** is a software utility that converts a triangulated **STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** mesh — the standard output of CAD systems — into **G-code**, the low-level machine instructions that command the printer's motors, heaters, and extruder step-by-step. **Cura** (developed by **Ultimaker B.V.**, first released 2011, currently open-source under the LGPLv3 license) is the **de facto industry-standard slicer** for extrusion-based AM.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Slicer as a "Translator Chef"

Think of Cura as a **translator** sitting between three parties:

- **The Chef's Recipe Book (STL file):** Contains the *shape* of the dish, but only the *shape* — not how to cook it.
- **The Head Translator (Slicer/Cura):** Reads the recipe, decides *which ingredients (filament)* to use, *how thickly to slice the layers*, *how hot the oven must be*, and *which parts need support scaffolding*.
- **The Kitchen Robot (3D Printer):** Executes the translated G-code instructions blindly, one motor step at a time.

Without this translation, the printer would receive an STL mesh and have no idea how to physically produce the part.

---

### Core Functional Modules of Cura

Cura's architecture is organized into modular engines:

1. **Slice Engine** — performs the planar slicing of the mesh at discrete $z$-heights.
2. **Infill Engine** — generates the internal lattice structure (gyroid, cubic, lines, etc.).
3. **Support Engine** — computes tree/linear support structures for overhangs.
4. **Adhesion Engine** — generates brim, raft, or skirt to prevent warping at first-layer interfaces.
5. **G-code Writer (Post-Processor)** — emits machine-specific G-code via a configurable *printer definition* (machine.json / .curaprofile).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The slicer is where the **CAD design intent** is finalized. A brilliant CAD model can still fail if the slicer is misconfigured (wrong temperature, no supports, wrong orientation). Conversely, clever slicer settings can rescue a marginal design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Basis of Extrusion-Based Design Rules

### A. The Bead Geometry and the Nozzle Constraint

In FDM/FFF, the extruded filament leaving the nozzle is a **viscoelastic polymer melt** that is deposited as a roughly elliptical or "pancake-shaped" bead. The cross-section of this bead is set by:

- **Nozzle diameter** $D_n$
- **Layer height** $h_l$
- **Volumetric flow rate** $Q_v$

The fundamental constraint is the **volumetric continuity equation**:

$$Q_v = A_{bead} \cdot v_{print}$$

where $A_{bead}$ is the cross-sectional area of the extruded bead and $v_{print}$ is the print head travel speed. Since $A_{bead} \approx D_n \cdot h_l$ (first-order rectangular approximation) or $A_{bead} \approx \pi \cdot D_n^2 / 4$ (elliptical), the relationship between extrusion rate and print speed is fixed by geometry.

**Why this matters for design rules:**
- If a CAD wall is *thinner* than $D_n$, the slicer cannot deposit a continuous bead — it produces a **gappy, weak wall**.
- If the layer height exceeds approximately **75% of $D_n$**, the bead loses adhesion to the previous layer (insufficient compression).
- If the layer height is below approximately **25% of $D_n$**, the extruder stalls (insufficient material throughput per unit time).

This gives the **process window**:

$$0.25 \cdot D_n \;\leq\; h_l \;\leq\; 0.75 \cdot D_n$$

For the standard **$D_n = 0.4$ mm** nozzle, this translates to:

$$0.10\;\text{mm} \;\leq\; h_l \;\leq\; 0.30\;\text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **WHY** behind all minimum-feature-size rules — they are direct consequences of the bead geometry that the slicer is forced to produce.

---

### B. Overhang Physics — The 45° Rule

An overhang is a printed feature whose **top surface** is unsupported from below in the build direction. The maximum self-supporting angle is determined by the balance of two forces:

1. **Gravity-induced sag** of the molten bead
2. **Inter-layer adhesion** (van der Waals + partial diffusion bonding)

The empirical "**45-degree rule**" states that any overhang whose face makes an angle of **less than 45° from vertical** (i.e., a slope steeper than 45° from the horizontal) can usually be printed without support. Beyond this angle, supports are required.

Mathematically, the overhang angle $\theta_{oh}$ is defined as:

$$\theta_{oh} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta z}\right)$$

where $\Delta x$ is the horizontal extent of the overhang over a vertical rise $\Delta z$. Support is required when:

$$\theta_{oh} < 45° \quad \text{(steep overhangs — safe)}$$
$$\theta_{oh} \geq 45° \quad \text{(gentle overhangs — typically need supports)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students often confuse the convention: **steeper = safer**. A vertical wall ($\theta = 0°$ from vertical) is fine. A horizontal ceiling ($\theta = 90°$ from vertical) absolutely needs support.

---

### C. Wall Thickness Rule (Multi-Wall Constraint)

Modern slicers (Cura included) print walls as **concentric perimeters** (shells). If the slicer is set to print **$N$ perimeter walls**, the total wall thickness $W$ is:

$$W = N \cdot w_{bead} \approx N \cdot D_n$$

For a part to be **structurally printable**, the minimum wall thickness is:

$$W_{min} = 2 \cdot D_n$$

This is because:
- **1 perimeter** is too thin and tends to delaminate.
- **2 perimeters** give a structurally sound wall that resists peel forces.
- **3+ perimeters** are used for load-bearing or watertight parts.

For $D_n = 0.4$ mm: $W_{min} \approx 0.8$ mm. Most slicers default to $W = 1.2$ mm (3 perimeters) for PLA and **1.6 mm** (4 perimeters) for ABS to control warping.

---

### D. Minimum Hole Diameter and Horizontal Hole Compensation

Holes in FDM suffer from two systematic errors:
1. **Hole shrinkage** (vertical-axis holes print slightly smaller due to inward-curling bead tension).
2. **Hole elongation** (horizontal-axis holes print oblong because the nozzle cannot cut a sharp internal corner).

The compensation rules are:

$$\text{Vertical hole (axis along } z\text{): } D_{design} = D_{target} + 0.2\;\text{mm}$$

$$\text{Horizontal hole (axis along } x\text{ or } y\text{): } D_{design} = D_{target} + 0.4\;\text{mm}$$

Minimum printable hole diameter:

$$D_{hole,min} = 2 \cdot D_n$$

For $D_n = 0.4$ mm: $D_{hole,min} = 0.8$ mm (in practice, **2.0 mm** is recommended to avoid clogging/nozzle deflection).

---

### E. Bridging Limit

A **bridge** is a horizontal span of filament deposited in mid-air between two support pillars. The maximum bridgeable length $L_{bridge}$ is empirically:

$$L_{bridge,max} \approx 5 \cdot D_n \quad \text{(for standard FDM, sagging threshold)}$$

Beyond this length, the bridge sags visibly and may collapse.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value or pipe-symbol notations are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

| # | Rule / Concept | Formula | Typical Value ($D_n = 0.4$ mm) | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------------|---------|--------------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Process window for layer height | $0.25 \cdot D_n \le h_l \le 0.75 \cdot D_n$ | $0.10 \le h_l \le 0.30$ mm | Bead must compress into previous layer without starving |
| 2 | Bead width ≈ nozzle width | $w_{bead} \approx D_n$ | $0.4$ mm | Bead is the smallest feature unit |
| 3 | Minimum wall thickness | $W_{min} = N \cdot D_n$, $N \ge 2$ | $0.8$ mm | Two-perimeter wall is the printable minimum |
| 4 | Minimum feature size | $f_{min} = D_n$ | $0.4$ mm | Anything smaller is unrepresentable by a single bead |
| 5 | Minimum hole diameter | $D_{hole,min} = 2 \cdot D_n$ | $0.8$ mm | Allows full circular toolpath |
| 6 | Vertical hole compensation | $D_{design} = D_{target} + 0.2$ mm | $+0.2$ mm | Compensates bead inward curling |
| 7 | Horizontal hole compensation | $D_{design} = D_{target} + 0.4$ mm | $+0.4$ mm | Compensates nozzle corner radius |
| 8 | Maximum overhang angle | $\theta_{oh} \ge 45°$ from vertical → no support | $45°$ | Steeper features self-support |
| 9 | Maximum bridge length | $L_{bridge,max} = 5 \cdot D_n$ | $2$ mm | Beyond this, supports mandatory |
| 10 | Volumetric flow continuity | $Q_v = A_{bead} \cdot v_{print}$ | varies | Links extrusion rate to print speed |
| 11 | Inter-layer bond strength (qualitative) | $\propto (T_{extrusion} - T_g)$ | $T_{extrusion} \approx 200\text{–}230°C$ for PLA | Hotter extruder = stronger bond |
| 12 | Build volume constraint | $\vert x \vert \le X_{max}, \vert y \vert \le Y_{max}, \vert z \vert \le Z_{max}$ | e.g. $220 \times 220 \times 250$ mm | Part must fit printer envelope |

---

## 2.3 Cura Slicer — Theoretical Function Pipeline

Cura's workflow is a deterministic, sequential pipeline:

1. **Mesh Ingestion** — Loads the STL/OBJ/3MF file, verifies manifold (watertight) geometry.
2. **Mesh Repair** — Heals common defects: non-manifold edges, inverted normals, holes in the surface.
3. **Slicing** — Intersects the mesh with horizontal planes at $z = k \cdot h_l$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N_{layers}$.
4. **Path Generation** — For each layer, generates the outer perimeter, inner perimeters, infill, top/bottom skin, and supports.
5. **G-code Emission** — Translates the path into machine-executable instructions (G0/G1 moves, M104/M140 temperature commands, G28 homing, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **The slicing step itself is a computational geometry operation:** a plane-mesh intersection. The result is a set of 2D polygons per layer, which the slicer then offsets inward/outward to generate walls and infill.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Flow Continuity for Bead Geometry

We now derive the relationship that links extruder feed rate, nozzle geometry, and print speed.

### Step 1 — Statement of the Problem

A polymer filament of diameter $d_f$ is fed into a heated nozzle at linear feed rate $v_f$ (mm/s). It exits the nozzle of diameter $D_n$ as a molten bead deposited at print head travel speed $v_p$ (mm/s). Establish the relationship between these quantities.

### Step 2 — Solid Filament Cross-Sectional Area

The cross-sectional area of the incoming solid filament is:

$$A_f = \frac{\pi \cdot d_f^2}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Volumetric Flow Rate of Incoming Filament

The volumetric flow rate of polymer arriving at the melt zone is the cross-section times the linear feed rate:

$$Q_v = A_f \cdot v_f = \frac{\pi \cdot d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_f$$

This is the fundamental **continuity equation** of the extruder.

### Step 4 — Bead Cross-Sectional Area (Rectangular Approximation)

Assume the deposited bead has a rectangular cross-section of width $D_n$ (nozzle diameter) and height $h_l$ (layer height). The bead area is therefore:

$$A_{bead} = D_n \cdot h_l$$

This is a **first-order approximation**; in reality, the bead is slightly oval due to polymer die-swell and surface tension, but the rectangular model is the slicer-engineering standard used by Cura.

### Step 5 — Conservation of Mass (Incompressible Melt)

The polymer melt is incompressible, so the volumetric flow rate of the deposited bead must equal the volumetric flow rate of the fed filament:

$$A_{bead} \cdot v_p = A_f \cdot v_f$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 2 and 4:

$$D_n \cdot h_l \cdot v_p = \frac{\pi \cdot d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_f$$

### Step 6 — Solve for Filament Feed Rate

Rearranging for $v_f$:

$$v_f = \frac{4 \cdot D_n \cdot h_l \cdot v_p}{\pi \cdot d_f^2}$$

This is the **master formula** that every FDM slicer (including Cura) uses internally to command the extruder stepper motor.

### Step 7 — Numerical Example (Cura Default)

Take the typical Cura defaults:
- $D_n = 0.4$ mm
- $h_l = 0.2$ mm
- $v_p = 60$ mm/s
- $d_f = 1.75$ mm (standard PLA filament)

Compute the numerator and denominator step by step.

The numerator evaluates to:

$$4 \cdot D_n \cdot h_l \cdot v_p = 4 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 60$$

$$= 4 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 12.0 = 1.6 \cdot 12.0 = 19.2\;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$$

The denominator evaluates to:

$$\pi \cdot d_f^2 = \pi \cdot (1.75)^2 = \pi \cdot 3.0625 \approx 9.621\;\text{mm}^2$$

Dividing, the filament feed rate is:

$$v_f = \frac{19.2}{9.621} \approx 1.995\;\text{mm/s}$$

This means the extruder must push filament at approximately **2.0 mm/s** to maintain a 60 mm/s print head speed with the default Cura parameters — a value that is correctly computed by the slicer at print start.

### Step 8 — Engineering Insight

If the slicer were to command $v_f = 2$ mm/s but the actual extruder stepper under-extrudes (e.g., due to a partially clogged nozzle), the **printed part would be under-extruded** — visible as gaps between adjacent beads and weak layer bonding. This is why **flow calibration** is a critical pre-print step in professional FDM workflows.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Overhang Critical Angle

### Step 1 — Physical Setup

Consider a layer of length $L$ printed at a height $z$ that is supported only at one end. The free end sags by a distance $\delta$ under its own weight $W_{bead}$. The bead is modeled as a cantilever beam of rectangular cross-section.

### Step 2 — Bending Beam Approximation

Treating the unsupported bead as a beam of length $L$ loaded uniformly by gravity, the deflection $\delta$ is:

$$\delta = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot L^4}{8 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of the molten polymer (~1.24 g/cm³ for PLA)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $E$ = Young's modulus of the *molten* bead (very low, ~10⁴ Pa)
- $I$ = second moment of area of the bead cross-section

For a rectangular bead of width $D_n$ and height $h_l$:

$$I = \frac{D_n \cdot h_l^3}{12}$$

### Step 3 — Failure Criterion

The overhang fails when the sag $\delta$ exceeds the layer height $h_l$ (i.e., the bead droops onto the previous layer irregularly):

$$\delta \ge h_l$$

### Step 4 — Geometric Translation to Overhang Angle

For a sloped overhang making angle $\theta_{oh}$ with the vertical, the unsupported horizontal projection is:

$$L = \frac{h_l}{\cos(\theta_{oh})}$$

Substituting this into the failure criterion (after canceling $h_l$ from both sides) yields, after manipulation, the approximate empirical rule:

$$\theta_{oh,critical} \approx 45°$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is intentionally **approximate** because the molten-bead Young's modulus is highly temperature-dependent and the simple cantilever beam model neglects thermal cooling. The **45° rule** is therefore an empirical engineering rule of thumb, not a first-principles result. KTU exam answers should reflect this nuance.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping CAD Feature → Design Rule → Cura Setting

| CAD Feature | Design Rule Applied | Consequence if Violated | Corresponding Cura Setting |
|-------------|---------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| Thin wall < 0.8 mm | Minimum wall = $2 D_n$ | Wall prints as single weak perimeter; may collapse | "Wall Line Count" $\ge 2$; set minimum feature size |
| Horizontal hole < 2 mm | $D_{hole,min} = 2 D_n$ | Hole closes up or prints as irregular blob | "Hole Horizontal Expansion" = +0.4 mm |
| Steep overhang > 45° | Overhang angle rule | Drooping, ugly underside | "Support Structure" → enabled, "Support Overhang Angle" = 45° |
| Long horizontal span > 2 mm | Bridge rule | Sag, stringing | Enable supports or reorient the part |
| Tall thin pillar | Cross-section shrinkage | Pillar warps or detaches | Enable "Brim" or "Raft" for build-plate adhesion |
| Large flat base | First-layer warping | Corners lift (ABS) | "Build Plate Adhesion" → Brim with 8–10 mm offset |
| Small embossed text | Minimum feature size | Text vanishes/blurs | Emboss text height $\ge 1.0$ mm and width $\ge 0.6$ mm |
| Threads / snap fits | Tolerance stack-up | Snap fits too tight/loose | Design with **0.15–0.25 mm** tolerance for FDM |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Extrusion Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Filament Spool<br/>d_f = 1.75 mm] --> B[Drive Gear<br/>v_f = filament feed rate]
    B --> C[Heated Nozzle<br/>T_nozzle = 200 to 230 C]
    C --> D[Melt Zone<br/>Q_v = A_f v_f]
    D --> E[Layer Deposition<br/>h_l = layer height]
    E --> F[Build Platform<br/>T_bed = 60 to 80 C]
    F --> G[Solidified Bead<br/>width = D_n]
    G --> H{Within Design Rules?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Successful Print]
    H -- No --> J[Failure Mode<br/>Warping or Sag or Gap]
    I --> K[Finished Part]
    J --> L[Reorient or Add Supports]
```

## 4.2 Cura Slicer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputStage["Input Stage"]
        A1[STL or OBJ or 3MF Mesh]
        A2[Mesh Repair Module<br/>Heals non-manifold edges]
        A1 --> A2
    end

    subgraph SliceStage["Slicing Stage"]
        B1[Plane Mesh Intersection<br/>at z = k h_l]
        B2[Perimeter Generator<br/>Outer and Inner Walls]
        B3[Infill Pattern Engine<br/>Lines or Grid or Gyroid]
        B4[Support Engine<br/>Tree or Linear]
        B5[Adhesion Engine<br/>Brim or Raft or Skirt]
        A2 --> B1
        B1 --> B2
        B1 --> B3
        B1 --> B4
        B1 --> B5
    end

    subgraph PathStage["Path Planning Stage"]
        C1[Travel Optimization]
        C2[Retraction Control<br/>Avoid Stringing]
        C3[Flow Compensation]
        B2 --> C1
        B3 --> C1
        C1 --> C2
        C2 --> C3
    end

    subgraph OutputStage["Output Stage"]
        D1[G-code Writer<br/>Machine Specific Post Processor]
        D2[Printer<br/>Executes G0 G1 M104 M140]
        C3 --> D1
        D1 --> D2
    end
```

## 4.3 Design Rule Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([New CAD Model]) --> Q1{Is wall thickness<br/>greater than 0.8 mm?}
    Q1 -- No --> R1[Modify CAD<br/>Add Material to Thin Wall]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Are there overhangs<br/>steeper than 45 degrees?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Enable Support in Cura<br/>Set Overhang Angle 45]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Any horizontal holes<br/>smaller than 2 mm?}
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 -- Yes --> R2[Apply Hole Horizontal Expansion<br/>0.4 mm in Cura]
    R2 --> Q5
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Does the part fit<br/>the build volume?}
    Q5 -- No --> R3[Scale Down or Split Model]
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6{Does the base need<br/>adhesion help?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> R4[Add Brim or Raft]
    Q6 -- No --> Slice[Proceed to Slicing]
    R1 --> Q2
    R3 --> Q6
    R4 --> Slice
    Slice([Ready for Print])
```

## 4.4 Overhang Angle Visualization (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SteepSafe["Steep Angle: SAFE (No Support Needed)"]
        direction LR
        S1[Vertical Wall<br/>theta equals 0 deg] --> S2[Near Vertical<br/>theta less than 30 deg]
    end

    subgraph Critical["Critical Angle: Borderline"]
        C1[Slope at 45 deg<br/>Add Supports for Safety] --> C2[Some sagging observed]
    end

    subgraph GentleFail["Gentle Angle: SUPPORT MANDATORY"]
        F1[Shallow Slope 60 deg] --> F2[Horizontal Ceiling 90 deg]
    end

    SteepSafe --> Critical --> GentleFail
```
