# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEIET525]**

**State any three design rules that must be considered while designing a part for Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) process. Justify the basis of each rule.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Minimum Wall Thickness Rule:** The minimum wall thickness should be at least **$2 \times D_n$** (i.e., 0.8 mm for a 0.4 mm nozzle). *Justification:* A single perimeter wall is too thin to resist peel forces during printing and tends to delaminate; two perimeters provide a structurally sound wall.

2. **Overhang Angle Rule:** Overhangs should not exceed **45° from the vertical** without supports. *Justification:* Beyond this angle, the molten bead cannot support its own weight against gravity and will sag.

3. **Minimum Hole Diameter Rule:** Holes should have a diameter of at least **$2 \times D_n = 0.8$ mm** (practically ≥ 2 mm). *Justification:* The slicer needs a minimum circular path for the nozzle to traverse without the perimeter collapsing inward.

*(Any other valid rule such as bridge length, hole compensation, or layer height window can earn full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEIET525]**

**What is a slicer in additive manufacturing? List any four functions performed by the slicer software Cura.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **slicer** is a software utility that converts a 3D mesh (STL/3MF) into machine-readable G-code by virtually slicing the model into horizontal layers.

Four functions of Cura:

1. **Mesh Slicing** — Intersects the mesh with horizontal planes at specified layer height $h_l$.
2. **Infill Generation** — Creates internal lattice structure (lines, grid, gyroid) at user-defined density.
3. **Support Structure Generation** — Computes support pillars/tree structures for overhangs.
4. **Build Plate Adhesion Setup** — Generates brim, raft, or skirt to prevent first-layer warping.

*(A 5th valid function: G-code emission via post-processor for the specific printer.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEIET525 Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **design rules for extrusion-based additive manufacturing**. For each rule, state the physical basis and the typical numerical value (assume nozzle diameter $D_n = 0.4$ mm). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **architecture and workflow of the Cura slicer software**, with a block diagram. List the main categories of print settings available in Cura and explain any five of them. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

The design rules for extrusion-based AM (FDM/FFF) ensure that the CAD model is **physically realizable** by the layer-by-layer extrusion process. The principal rules are:

**1. Minimum Wall Thickness (1 mark):**
The minimum wall thickness is governed by the number of perimeter walls and the bead width. For a slicer configured to print $N$ perimeters, the wall thickness is $W = N \cdot D_n$. The minimum printable value (with $N = 2$) is $W_{min} = 0.8$ mm for a 0.4 mm nozzle. *Physical basis:* A single-bead wall lacks the cross-sectional area to resist peel forces and the warping-induced bending moments during cooling, leading to delamination.

**2. Minimum Feature Size (1 mark):**
Any feature (rib, boss, embossed text) must be at least $D_n = 0.4$ mm in one dimension. *Physical basis:* The slicer discretizes the model with a finite-width bead and cannot deposit a sub-bead-width feature.

**3. Overhang Angle Rule (1.5 marks):**
Overhangs with an angle $\theta_{oh}$ from the vertical less than 45° (i.e., steeper than 45° from horizontal) can be self-supported. Beyond this, supports are mandatory. *Physical basis:* The molten polymer bead has very low stiffness in the molten state; under gravity, it sags if the unsupported span exceeds the bead's effective bending capacity.

**4. Minimum Hole Diameter and Hole Compensation (1.5 marks):**
Holes smaller than $2 \cdot D_n = 0.8$ mm cannot be printed. For holes oriented horizontally, design them **0.4 mm larger** than the target diameter; for vertical holes, add **0.2 mm**. *Physical basis:* The slicer's toolpath has finite corner radius; the deposited bead's surface tension pulls inward, shrinking the hole.

**5. Bridging Rule (1 mark):**
Maximum unsupported horizontal bridge length is $L_{bridge,max} = 5 \cdot D_n = 2$ mm. *Physical basis:* The bead's tensile strength in the molten state limits how far a horizontal span can be deposited in mid-air before sagging.

**6. Layer Height Process Window (1 mark):**
The layer height $h_l$ must lie between $0.25 \cdot D_n$ and $0.75 \cdot D_n$, i.e., **0.10 to 0.30 mm** for a 0.4 mm nozzle. *Physical basis:* Below the lower bound, the extruder starves; above the upper bound, the bead does not compress adequately into the previous layer, producing poor adhesion.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Cura Architecture (3.5 marks):**

Cura follows a modular pipeline architecture:

- **Input Module:** Accepts STL/OBJ/3MF mesh files. Performs mesh repair (heals non-manifold edges, fixes normals).
- **Slicing Engine:** Performs plane-mesh intersection at $z = k \cdot h_l$ to produce 2D layer contours.
- **Path Generation Engine:** For each layer, generates (i) outer and inner perimeter walls, (ii) infill pattern, (iii) top/bottom skin, (iv) support structures, (v) adhesion aids.
- **Path Planning Engine:** Optimizes travel moves, applies retraction to prevent stringing, computes flow compensation.
- **Post-Processor (G-code Writer):** Emits machine-specific G-code using the active printer's `.json` definition.

**Five Main Print Settings in Cura (3.5 marks — 0.7 marks each):**

1. **Layer Height** — Vertical resolution of slicing. Lower = higher quality, longer print time.
2. **Print Speed** — Travel speed of the print head. Affects bead quality and surface finish.
3. **Nozzle Temperature** — Must exceed $T_g$ and stay below polymer degradation. PLA: 200–230°C.
4. **Infill Density** — Percentage of internal volume filled (0% hollow to 100% solid). Affects strength vs material usage.
5. **Support Structure** — Enable/disable, set overhang threshold angle, choose support pattern (lines, grid, tree).
6. **Build Plate Adhesion** — Brim (extra outline rings), Raft (thick lattice under part), or Skirt (outline only, no contact).
7. **Retraction Settings** — Distance and speed at which filament is withdrawn during travel moves to prevent stringing.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **step-by-step workflow of the Cura slicer** from STL import to G-code export. State the role of the post-processor in this workflow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 0.4 mm nozzle printing PLA filament of diameter 1.75 mm, with layer height 0.2 mm and print head speed 60 mm/s, calculate the **filament feed rate** required at the extruder. If the layer height is changed to 0.1 mm, what is the new feed rate? Comment on the effect on print time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Workflow of Cura (5 marks):**

1. **Import the 3D Model:** The user loads an STL/OBJ/3MF file via the "Open File" dialog. The mesh is displayed in the build-plate preview.
2. **Mesh Analysis and Repair:** Cura automatically analyzes the mesh for errors (non-manifold edges, holes, inverted normals) and offers to repair them. *Significance:* A non-manifold mesh will produce incomplete or failed slices.
3. **Print Configuration:** The user selects the printer profile (e.g., "Creality Ender 3") and the material (e.g., "Generic PLA"). The slicer loads the corresponding material presets (temperatures, speeds).
4. **Orientation and Placement:** The part is positioned on the build plate. Orientation affects layer count (and hence print time), surface quality, and support requirements.
5. **Setting Custom Print Parameters:** The user adjusts print quality (layer height), strength (infill density, wall count), speed, supports, and adhesion aids.
6. **Slicing:** Clicking "Slice" triggers the slicing engine, which generates the per-layer 2D contours and the complete G-code. A preview of each layer, estimated print time, and filament usage is displayed.
7. **G-code Export/Save:** The G-code is either saved to a file (e.g., on an SD card) or sent directly to the printer via USB.

**Role of the Post-Processor (2 marks):**
The post-processor (or G-code writer) is the final module that translates the slicer's internal path representation into the **machine-specific G-code dialect** of the target printer. It handles printer-specific commands such as homing sequences (G28), bed leveling (G29), end-of-program routines (M104/M140 for temperature setting, M84 for motor disable), and firmware-specific features (e.g., linear advance, arc commands G2/G3). Without the post-processor, the G-code would not match the printer's firmware expectations.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $D_n = 0.4$ mm
- $d_f = 1.75$ mm
- $h_l = 0.2$ mm (Case 1) and $0.1$ mm (Case 2)
- $v_p = 60$ mm/s

**Formula Derived (from Section 3.1):**

$$v_f = \frac{4 \cdot D_n \cdot h_l \cdot v_p}{\pi \cdot d_f^2}$$

**Case 1: $h_l = 0.2$ mm (2 marks)**

$$v_{f,1} = \frac{4 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 60}{\pi \cdot (1.75)^2}$$

Numerator: $4 \times 0.4 \times 0.2 \times 60 = 19.2\;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$

Denominator: $\pi \times 3.0625 \approx 9.621\;\text{mm}^2$

$$v_{f,1} = \frac{19.2}{9.621} \approx 1.995\;\text{mm/s} \approx 2.0\;\text{mm/s}$$

**Case 2: $h_l = 0.1$ mm (2 marks)**

$$v_{f,2} = \frac{4 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 60}{\pi \cdot (1.75)^2}$$

Numerator: $4 \times 0.4 \times 0.1 \times 60 = 9.6\;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$

Denominator: unchanged at $9.621\;\text{mm}^2$

$$v_{f,2} = \frac{9.6}{9.621} \approx 0.998\;\text{mm/s} \approx 1.0\;\text{mm/s}$$

**Comment on Print Time (3 marks):**
The feed rate is **halved** (from 2.0 mm/s to 1.0 mm/s) when the layer height is halved. However, the print time **doubles** because the number of layers required to build the same part height is doubled (each layer is now half as thick). The total volume of plastic deposited is unchanged, but the print head must traverse twice as many layers. The wall quality and surface finish improve because each layer is thinner, but the trade-off is doubled print time and increased wear on the printer's mechanical components.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention for overhangs:** Students frequently state "overhangs > 45° need support" without specifying whether the angle is measured from vertical or horizontal. **Always state explicitly** that the angle is measured from the *vertical*. This single phrase is worth 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing minimum wall with minimum feature size:** Minimum wall is $2D_n$ (two perimeters), but minimum feature size is $D_n$ (single bead). Markers deduct for conflating these.
> 3. **Forgetting the units in flow rate calculations:** The filament feed rate is in **mm/s**, NOT mm³/s. Many students confuse the two.
> 4. **Slicer is not the same as the printer:** Cura does NOT print — it only generates G-code. The printer is the executor.
> 5. **Mesh repair is automatic but not always sufficient:** Severely corrupt meshes may need external tools (Meshmixer, Blender) before slicing will succeed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Design Rules and Cura**

### A. Design Rules (Memorize the Numbers)

- [ ] **Nozzle diameter $D_n$ is the master parameter** — all design rules scale with it.
- [ ] **Minimum wall thickness = $2 \cdot D_n$** (typically 0.8 mm; slicer default 1.2 mm).
- [ ] **Minimum feature size = $D_n$** (0.4 mm).
- [ ] **Minimum hole diameter = $2 \cdot D_n$** (0.8 mm; recommended 2 mm).
- [ ] **Overhang angle limit = 45° from vertical** (steeper is safe).
- [ ] **Bridge length limit = $5 \cdot D_n$** (2 mm).
- [ ] **Layer height window = $0.25 D_n$ to $0.75 D_n$** (0.10–0.30 mm).
- [ ] **Hole compensation: +0.2 mm vertical, +0.4 mm horizontal.**
- [ ] **Holes, slots, and threads need tolerance:** 0.15–0.25 mm for press/snap fits.

### B. Cura Slicer (Memorize the Pipeline)

- [ ] **Cura is developed by Ultimaker** and is the **industry-standard open-source slicer**.
- [ ] **Cura does NOT print** — it only generates G-code.
- [ ] **Input formats:** STL, OBJ, 3MF (3MF is the modern, preferred format).
- [ ] **Slicing = plane-mesh intersection at $z = k \cdot h_l$.**
- [ ] **Five functional modules:** Input → Slicing → Path Generation → Path Planning → G-code Output (Post-Processor).
- [ ] **G-code commands to know:** G0 (rapid move), G1 (linear move), G28 (home), M104 (set extruder temp), M140 (set bed temp), M84 (disable motors).
- [ ] **Adhesion aids:** Brim (extra outline rings), Raft (thick lattice under the part), Skirt (outline only — does not touch part).
- [ ] **Infill patterns:** Lines, Grid, Triangles, Cubic, Gyroid, Lightning.
- [ ] **Support types:** Normal (linear grid) vs Tree (organic, easier to remove, less material).

### C. Formulae (Memorize Exactly)

- [ ] $v_f = \dfrac{4 \cdot D_n \cdot h_l \cdot v_p}{\pi \cdot d_f^2}$
- [ ] $0.25 D_n \le h_l \le 0.75 D_n$
- [ ] $W_{min} = 2 \cdot D_n$
- [ ] $L_{bridge,max} = 5 \cdot D_n$

### D. Common Viva/Practical Questions

- [ ] "Why does FDM need design rules and not just print anything?" → *Answer:* Bead geometry, gravity, surface tension all impose hard physical constraints.
- [ ] "What is the difference between brim and raft?" → *Answer:* Brim is a single-layer outline ring (good for warping); raft is a multi-layer sacrificial base (good for poor bed adhesion).
- [ ] "Why does a horizontal hole print bigger than designed?" → *Answer:* Nozzle corner radius; compensated by +0.4 mm in Cura.
- [ ] "If I halve the layer height, what happens to print time and quality?" → *Answer:* Print time doubles; surface quality improves; feed rate halves.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creality Print
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET525 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING AND 3D PRINTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Creality Print

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Creality Print** is a proprietary, open-source-based slicing software (slicer) developed by **Creality 3D Technology Co., Ltd.**, designed primarily to convert 3D digital models (typically in **STL, OBJ, or 3MF** file formats) into machine-executable **G-code (numerical control instructions)** for Creality's Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) and Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF) 3D printers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEIET525, Module 2):**
> Creality Print falls under the broader topic of *Slicing Software for Additive Manufacturing*. It is a **vendor-specific slicer** (closed ecosystem optimization for Creality hardware) derived from the **CuraEngine** open-source slicing kernel, augmented with Creality's proprietary print-profiles, print-time estimation algorithms, and Cloud-based print management features.

Formally stated, the slicing pipeline inside Creality Print is mathematically expressed as:

$$
f_{\text{slice}} : \mathcal{M}_{3D} \longrightarrow \mathcal{I}_{G\text{-code}}
$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{M}_{3D}$ = the triangulated 3D mesh model space
- $\mathcal{I}_{G\text{-code}}$ = the set of instruction lines (G0, G1, G28, M104, M140, etc.) readable by the printer's firmware (e.g., Marlin, Klipper).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **3D printer as a very obedient, very literal "chef"** who only understands a single language — a list of precise commands telling it *"move 0.1 mm to the right, extrude 0.005 mm³ of plastic, heat the nozzle to 210 °C"*. The 3D model you download or design is like a **photograph of the finished dish**: the chef cannot eat a photograph.

**Creality Print is the "translator-recipe-writer"** sitting between you and the chef.

| Real-World Role | Technical Equivalent in 3D Printing |
|---|---|
| Customer ordering a cake | User loading a `.stl` / `.obj` / `.3mf` file |
| Chef | 3D Printer hardware (nozzle, motors, bed) |
| Recipe book in a foreign language | 3D model file (mesh of triangles) |
| Translator writing step-by-step instructions | **Creality Print slicer** |
| Final written recipe | G-code file (`.gcode`) |
| The customer reading the recipe | 3D printer firmware executing G-code line-by-line |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** If Cura is the "Swiss Army Knife" of slicing (generic, works for almost any printer), then **Creality Print is a "factory-tuned scalpel"** — pre-calibrated for Creality's K1, K1 Max, Ender-3, CR-10, Halot (resin), and Kobra series, with print profiles optimized for Creality's specific kinematics, accelerations, and direct-drive extruder behavior.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Creality Print

The slicer computes and displays several **standard metrics** in bold, all of which frequently appear in KTU numerical questions:

- **Layer Height (h):** typically **0.08 mm to 0.30 mm** (Creality K1 series supports down to 0.08 mm).
- **Print Speed (V_p):** **up to 600 mm/s** (K1 Max); default **150–250 mm/s**.
- **Nozzle Temperature (T_n):** **190–260 °C** for PLA; **220–260 °C** for PETG; **230–280 °C** for ABS.
- **Bed Temperature (T_b):** **50–60 °C** (PLA); **70–85 °C** (PETG); **95–110 °C** (ABS).
- **Infill Density (ρ_i):** **0% (hollow) to 100% (solid)**, default **15–20%**.
- **Estimated Print Time (t_p)** and **Filament Length (L_f)** in meters/grams.
- **Acceleration (a):** **up to 20 000 mm/s²** (K1 Max with Input Shaping).

## 1.4 Software Architecture Stack

Creality Print is built on a multi-layered architecture:

> [!NOTE]
> **Layered Architecture (top → bottom):**
> 1. **Graphical User Interface (GUI)** — built on **Qt Framework (C++/QML)** for cross-platform support (Windows, macOS, Linux).
> 2. **Slicing Engine** — derived from **CuraEngine** (open-source C++ kernel from UltiMaker) with Creality's proprietary modifications.
> 3. **Geometry Processing Module** — handles **mesh repair, manifold checking, and boolean operations**.
> 4. **G-code Post-processor** — emits Creality-compatible G-code with vendor-specific start/end macros.
> 5. **Cloud Module** — optional **Creality Cloud** integration for remote print job submission, slicing in the cloud, and model library access.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Slicing Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation of Creality Print can be decomposed into **seven sequential stages**, each of which is examinable in KTU Part A (3-mark) or Part B (14-mark) questions.

### Stage 1 — **Model Import & Validation**
- User imports a 3D mesh.
- The software performs **manifold validation** (every edge must be shared by exactly 2 triangles).
- Non-manifold edges are auto-repaired using algorithms like **Hole Filling** and **Normal Recalculation**.

### Stage 2 — **Model Orientation & Support Generation**
- User rotates, scales, and translates the model on the virtual build plate.
- If the model has overhangs exceeding the **support overhang angle (θ_s)**, default **45°**, Creality Print automatically generates **tree supports** or **grid supports**.

### Stage 3 — **Layer Slicing (The Core Operation)**
- The slicer intersects the 3D mesh with horizontal planes at intervals equal to the **layer height (h)**.
- Each intersection produces a 2D contour polygon per layer.
- Mathematical representation:

$$
z_k = z_{\min} + k \cdot h, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N_{\text{layers}} - 1
$$

Where:

$$
N_{\text{layers}} = \left\lceil \frac{z_{\max} - z_{\min}}{h} \right\rceil
$$

### Stage 4 — **2D Path Planning (Infill + Walls)**
- For each 2D layer contour, Creality Print generates:
  - **Outer perimeter (wall)**
  - **Inner perimeters** (number = wall line count, $W$, default 3)
  - **Infill pattern** — selectable from **Lines, Grid, Triangles, Gyroid, Cubic, Lightning, Honeycomb**
  - **Top and bottom solid layers** (typically 4–5)

### Stage 5 — **Speed & Acceleration Profile Assignment**
- Each move type is assigned a distinct speed: outer wall < inner wall < infill < travel.
- **Jerk** and **acceleration** are limited per-axis to prevent **ringing/ghosting** artifacts.

### Stage 6 — **G-code Emission**
- G-code is written with mandatory Creality-specific headers (e.g., `M140 S60` for bed heating, `M104 S210` for nozzle heating).
- The output is a `.gcode` file (or `.cxgcode` for Creality's encrypted format).

### Stage 7 — **Cloud Transfer / Local Transfer / MicroSD Export**
- File is sent to printer via **USB, Wi-Fi, Creality Cloud, or MicroSD card**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following table — these are the most-tested formulas in PEIET525 numerical/analytical questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of layers | $N_{\text{layers}} = \lceil \dfrac{H_{\text{model}}}{h} \rceil$ | $H_{\text{model}}$ = model height (mm), $h$ = layer height (mm) | Computes total slicing steps |
| 2 | Print time estimate (per layer) | $t_{\text{layer}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{path}}}{V_p}$ | $L_{\text{path}}$ = path length (mm), $V_p$ = print speed (mm/s) | Total print time prediction |
| 3 | Total filament volume | $V_{\text{filament}} = L_f \cdot \pi \cdot \left(\dfrac{d_f}{2}\right)^2$ | $L_f$ = filament length (mm), $d_f$ = filament diameter (1.75 mm typical) | Material cost estimation |
| 4 | Filament mass | $m_f = V_{\text{filament}} \cdot \rho_{\text{mat}}$ | $\rho_{\text{mat}}$ = material density (g/cm³) | Weight check for shipping/structural |
| 5 | Material densities | PLA: **1.24 g/cm³**, PETG: **1.27 g/cm³**, ABS: **1.04 g/cm³**, TPU: **1.21 g/cm³** | — | Mass calculation |
| 6 | Extrusion flow rate | $Q = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_{\text{ext}}$ | $v_{\text{ext}}$ = extruder feed speed (mm/s) | Volumetric extrusion control |
| 7 | Volumetric flow rate | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{h \cdot w \cdot V_p}{1000}$ | $w$ = line width (mm) | Max flow limit (e.g., 10–25 mm³/s) |
| 8 | Wall count & shell thickness | $t_{\text{shell}} = W \cdot w$ | $W$ = wall line count, $w$ = line width | Mechanical strength of print |
| 9 | Effective infill density (effective mass) | $m_{\text{eff}} = m_{\text{solid}} \cdot \rho_i + m_{\text{shell}}$ | $\rho_i$ as a fraction (0–1) | Weight optimization |
| 10 | Input Shaping frequency (K1) | $f_{IS} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $k$ = stiffness, $m$ = moving mass | Vibration compensation |
| 11 | Z-hop during travel | $z_{\text{hop}} = h_{\text{hop}}$ (typ. 0.1–0.2 mm) | — | Prevents nozzle collision with part |
| 12 | Overhang angle threshold | $\theta_s = 45°$ default | — | Support generation trigger |
| 13 | Pressure Advance (Linear) | $E_{\text{adv}} = k_p \cdot \Delta v$ | $k_p$ = pressure advance coeff. (s), $\Delta v$ = velocity change | Reduces corner blob |
| 14 | Material cost | $C_m = m_f \cdot C_{\text{per kg}}$ | $C_{\text{per kg}}$ in ₹/kg | Economic analysis |
| 15 | Total print time (approximate) | $t_p \approx N_{\text{layers}} \cdot t_{\text{layer, avg}} + t_{\text{heatup}}$ | $t_{\text{heatup}}$ ≈ 60–120 s | Time planning |

## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Concept

- **Why slicing into layers?** Because FDM/FFF printers **physically cannot deposit material in 3D space continuously** — they build layer-by-layer from the bottom up. Slicing is the *bridge* between a 3D CAD model and a 1D deposition path.
- **Why wall count matters?** Walls determine the **structural integrity and surface finish**. More walls = more strength but longer print time.
- **Why infill?** Infill reduces **material usage and print time** while providing internal rigidity. A **gyroid infill** is excellent for isotropic strength; **lightning infill** is for ultra-fast prints where strength is not critical.
- **Why Input Shaping?** At high speeds (≥300 mm/s), mechanical vibrations cause **ghosting/ringing**. Input Shaping cancels these vibrations using feed-forward control.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rapid Prototyping:** Industrial designers use Creality Print's **Tree Supports** to print complex geometries (lattice structures, organic shapes) that would be impossible to machine.
- **Dental & Medical Models:** Creality's **Halot resin printers** use a parallel slicing engine for high-resolution (<50 µm XY) prints.
- **Architecture:** Convert architectural CAD to physical models at **1:100 or 1:50 scale**.
- **Education (KTU Labs):** KTU-affiliated colleges use Ender-3 and K1 series with Creality Print in B.Tech Mechanical/Mechatronics labs for ME481/ME490 Mini-Projects.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Print Time, Filament Mass & Cost Estimation

This is the **most frequently asked KTU numerical** on slicer topics. We will solve it exhaustively.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup (Typical KTU Numerical)

> A KTU student is 3D printing a **calibration cube of dimensions 20 mm × 20 mm × 20 mm** on a Creality K1 using Creality Print with the following parameters:
> - Layer height $h = 0.2$ mm
> - Line width $w = 0.4$ mm
> - Print speed $V_p = 200$ mm/s
> - 3 perimeter walls, **15% infill (gyroid)**
> - Top layers = 4, bottom layers = 4
> - Material: **PLA**, density $\rho = 1.24$ g/cm³, filament diameter $d_f = 1.75$ mm
> - Filament cost: ₹1 800 per kg
> - **Find: (a) Total print time, (b) Filament mass used, (c) Cost of material.**

---

### 3.1.2 Step A — Number of Layers

The model height is $H = 20$ mm. The first layer is typically 0.2 mm (matches layer height), so:

$$
N_{\text{layers}} = \left\lceil \frac{H}{h} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{20}{0.2} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 100 \right\rceil = 100 \text{ layers}
$$

**Interpretation:** The slicer will create **100 horizontal cross-sections**.

---

### 3.1.3 Step B — Shell vs. Infill Cross-Section

For each **non-top, non-bottom layer** (i.e., the inner "shell+infill" layers):

- Shell thickness on each side = $W \cdot w = 3 \cdot 0.4 = 1.2$ mm
- Effective cross-section that is solid shell (perimeter, including both sides): $2 \cdot 1.2 = 2.4$ mm from each side
- Effective **infill cross-section** (length × width):

$$
L_{\text{infill}} = 20 - 2 \times 1.2 = 20 - 2.4 = 17.6 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
A_{\text{infill}} = L_{\text{infill}} \times L_{\text{infill}} = 17.6 \times 17.6 = 309.76 \text{ mm}^2
$$

For **top and bottom layers** (4 + 4 = 8 layers), the entire layer is **100% solid**:

$$
A_{\text{solid}} = 20 \times 20 = 400 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Number of solid layers = 8; number of partial layers = $100 - 8 = 92$.

---

### 3.1.4 Step C — Volume Computation

**Volume of solid top + bottom layers:**

$$
V_{\text{solid}} = 8 \cdot 400 \cdot h = 8 \cdot 400 \cdot 0.2 = 640 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Volume of shell in 92 partial layers:**
The shell area (per layer) = Total area − infill area = $400 - 309.76 = 90.24$ mm²

$$
V_{\text{shell}} = 92 \cdot 90.24 \cdot 0.2 = 1660.416 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Volume of infill in 92 partial layers:**

$$
V_{\text{infill,full}} = 92 \cdot 309.76 \cdot 0.2 = 5699.584 \text{ mm}^3
$$

But infill is only **15% dense**:

$$
V_{\text{infill,actual}} = 0.15 \cdot 5699.584 = 854.9376 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Total Volume:**

$$
V_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{solid}} + V_{\text{shell}} + V_{\text{infill,actual}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 640 + 1660.416 + 854.9376 = 3155.35 \text{ mm}^3
$$

---

### 3.1.5 Step D — Convert to cm³ and Find Mass

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 3155.35 \text{ mm}^3 \times \frac{1 \text{ cm}^3}{1000 \text{ mm}^3} = 3.155 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
m_f = V_{\text{total}} \cdot \rho = 3.155 \cdot 1.24 = 3.913 \text{ g}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check via filament length:**
> $$
> V_{\text{fil}} = L_f \cdot \pi \cdot \left(\frac{d_f}{2}\right)^2
> $$
> $$
> L_f = \frac{3.155 \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot (1.75/2)^2} = \frac{3155}{2.405} = 1312.0 \text{ mm} \approx 1.31 \text{ m}
> $$

---

### 3.1.6 Step E — Material Cost

$$
C_m = \frac{m_f}{1000} \cdot C_{\text{per kg}} = \frac{3.913}{1000} \cdot 1800 = ₹7.04
$$

---

### 3.1.7 Step F — Approximate Print Time

Average path length per layer ≈ perimeter (mm) + infill lines. For simplicity, a typical estimate is:

$$
t_{\text{avg layer}} \approx 30 \text{ seconds (for a 20×20 mm cube at 200 mm/s)}
$$

$$
t_p \approx 100 \cdot 30 + 90 \text{ (heatup)} \approx 3090 \text{ s} \approx 51.5 \text{ minutes}
$$

---

### 3.1.8 Final Consolidated Answer

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Total layers | $N$ | **100** |
| Total volume | $V_{\text{total}}$ | **3 155.35 mm³** |
| Filament mass | $m_f$ | **≈ 3.91 g** |
| Filament length | $L_f$ | **≈ 1.31 m** |
| Material cost | $C_m$ | **≈ ₹7.04** |
| Print time | $t_p$ | **≈ 51.5 minutes** |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Feature-to-Use-Case Mapping

| Creality Print Feature | Technical Function | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Tree Supports** | Branching organic supports | Complex organic shapes, easy removal |
| **Grid Supports** | Lattice support grid | Standard overhangs ≥ 45° |
| **Lightning Infill** | Ultra-sparse internal structure | Visual prototypes (fast) |
| **Gyroid Infill** | Triply periodic minimal surface | Functional parts (isotropic strength) |
| **Spiralize Outer Contour (Vase Mode)** | Continuous Z-lift with single wall | Vases, lamps, decorative items |
| **Ironing** | Re-melt top surface | Smooth glossy top layers |
| **Pressure Advance** | Compensate extruder lag | High-speed printing corners |
| **Input Shaping** | Vibration cancellation | K1 series at >300 mm/s |
| **Z-hop** | Lift nozzle on travel | Avoid bumping into tall parts |
| **Flow Rate Calibration** | Adjusts extrusion multiplier | First-time material use |
| **Creality Cloud** | Remote slicing & printing | Multiple printers, lab management |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Creality Print Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    G --> H[2D Path Planner: Walls, Infill, Top, Bottom]
    H --> I[Speed and Acceleration Profile Assignment]
    I --> J[Pressure Advance and Input Shaping]
    J --> K[G-code Post-Processor]
    K --> L{Output Destination}
    L -- Local --> M[MicroSD or USB Transfer]
    L -- Wireless --> N[Creality Cloud Server]

    O --> P
```
