## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Flow Equivalence & Filament Feed Velocity

This is the **single most important derivation** in FDM, and it has appeared in multiple KTU past papers. The principle: *the volume of solid filament pushed into the liquefier per second must equal the volume of molten polymer leaving the nozzle per second* (conservation of mass / volume, assuming negligible thermal expansion and no voids).

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow rate of the solid filament entering the liquefier:**

The filament is a cylinder of diameter $d_f$, fed at velocity $v_f$. In one second, the cylinder swept out has length $v_f$ and cross-section $\frac{\pi d_f^2}{4}$. Therefore the volume rate is:

$$Q_{in} = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_f$$

*Logic:* Volume = Area × Length = $\frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \times v_f \times (1\text{ s})$.

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate of the extruded bead leaving the nozzle:**

The extruder deposits a bead of width $w_r$ and height $h_L$ (which equals the layer thickness) at a print-head velocity $v_p$. In one second, the bead laid down is a rectangular slab of volume $w_r \cdot h_L \cdot v_p \cdot 1$.

$$Q_{out} = w_r \cdot h_L \cdot v_p$$

*Logic:* The bead is approximately a rectangular cuboid; the cross-section is $w_r \times h_L$, and length per second is $v_p$.

**Step 3 — Apply conservation of volume (steady state, incompressible):**

At every instant in the **steady state**, $Q_{in} = Q_{out}$ (the molten polymer is incompressible and the flow is steady; we ignore thermal expansion which is a small second-order correction).

$$\dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_f = w_r \cdot h_L \cdot v_p$$

*Logic:* Volume in = Volume out. Any mismatch causes under-extrusion (gaps) or over-extrusion (blobs).

**Step 4 — Solve for the filament feed velocity $v_f$:**

Multiply both sides by $\frac{4}{\pi d_f^2}$:

$$v_f = \dfrac{4 \cdot w_r \cdot h_L \cdot v_p}{\pi d_f^2}$$

*Logic:* Algebraic rearrangement — isolate $v_f$.

**Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example (typical KTU problem):**

Given: $d_f = 1.75 \text{ mm} = 1.75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $w_r = 0.4 \text{ mm} = 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $h_L = 0.2 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $v_p = 60 \text{ mm/s} = 0.06 \text{ m/s}$.

Substitute:

$$v_f = \dfrac{4 \times (0.4 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.06}{\pi \times (1.75 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

Numerator:

$$4 \times 0.4 \times 0.2 \times 0.06 = 0.0192 \text{ (in units of } 10^{-6}\text{)}$$

Denominator:

$$\pi \times (1.75)^2 \approx 3.1416 \times 3.0625 \approx 9.6211 \text{ (in units of } 10^{-6}\text{)}$$

Therefore:

$$v_f \approx \dfrac{0.0192}{9.6211} \approx 1.996 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} \approx 2.0 \text{ mm/s}$$

*Logic:* The filament is fed at ~2 mm/s, which corresponds to an extruder step rate of (2.0 / 1.75) ≈ 1.14 mm filament per mm of extrusion × the printer's steps-per-mm calibration.

### 3.2 Derivation: Build Time Estimation

**Step 1 — Define the part volume and the effective volumetric deposition rate:**

The extruder deposits material at $Q_{out} = w_r \cdot h_L \cdot v_p$ (m³/s). The minimum time to lay down a solid part of volume $V_{part}$ is:

$$t_{min} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{Q_{out}} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{w_r \cdot h_L \cdot v_p}$$

*Logic:* Time = Volume ÷ Rate.

**Step 2 — Include the overhead factor for non-extruding moves:**

The print head spends time *travelling* between features, *retracting* for lifts, *acceleration/decelerating* at corners, and performing *first-layer homing*. Empirically, this overhead multiplies the time by a factor $f_o$ between **1.5 and 3.0**.

$$t_{actual} = f_o \cdot \dfrac{V_{part}}{w_r \cdot h_L \cdot v_p}$$

*Logic:* $f_o = 1$ means no overhead; $f_o = 2$ means the head spends half the total time extruding and half travelling.

**Step 3 — Worked example:**

A bracket has $V_{part} = 25 \text{ cm}^3 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$. With $w_r = 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $h_L = 0.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $v_p = 0.06$ m/s, $f_o = 2.5$:

$$t_{actual} = 2.5 \times \dfrac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{(0.4 \times 10^{-3})(0.2 \times 10^{-3})(0.06)}$$

$$t_{actual} = 2.5 \times \dfrac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{4.8 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.5 \times 5208 \text{ s} \approx 13020 \text{ s} \approx 3.6 \text{ hours}$$

*Logic:* Substituting and converting seconds to hours (÷3600).

### 3.3 Derivation: Staircase Effect on Inclined Surface

For a part surface inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal build platform, the staircase error (the gap between the ideal surface and the layered surface) is derived from simple trigonometry.

**Step 1 — Visualisation:** Each layer has thickness $h_L$. For an inclined surface, the horizontal distance between successive layer boundaries is $h_L / \tan(\alpha)$.

**Step 2 — Vertical deviation from the ideal surface:**

The maximum vertical deviation $e_s$ at the midpoint of a layer occurs at the centre of the layer and is:

$$e_s = \dfrac{h_L}{2} \cos(\alpha)$$

*Logic:* Geometric projection — the height of the triangle's apex from its base.

A simpler and often-quoted form for the **peak-to-valley error** (max-to-min within one layer):

$$e_s = h_L \cos(\alpha)$$

*Logic:* This is the full peak-to-valley height of the triangular staircase step. The cosine reflects that as $\alpha \to 90°$ (vertical wall), the error vanishes; as $\alpha \to 0°$ (horizontal), the error equals the full layer thickness.

**Step 3 — Numerical example:**

For $\alpha = 30°$ and $h_L = 0.2$ mm:

$$e_s = 0.2 \times \cos(30°) = 0.2 \times 0.866 = 0.173 \text{ mm}$$

*Logic:* The 30° surface will have a peak-to-valley deviation of 0.173 mm.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use FDM vs. Other AM

| Criterion | FDM is Best When… | FDM is Poor When… |
|---|---|---|
| **Material** | Thermoplastics (PLA, ABS, PETG, Nylon, PC, TPU) | Metals, ceramics, photopolymers, very high-temp polymers (PEEK possible but expensive) |
| **Tolerance / Accuracy** | ±0.2 mm typical; ±0.1 mm industrial | Tight tolerances (<±0.05 mm) — use SLA/DMLS |
| **Surface finish** | Functional prototypes; post-processing acceptable | Optical / aesthetic surfaces — use SLA/PJ |
| **Build size** | Large parts up to 1 m³ industrial | Micro-features <100 µm — use PolyJet / DLP |
| **Mechanical strength** | Anisotropic is acceptable | Truly isotropic parts required — use SLS (powder bed) |
| **Cost / accessibility** | Low capital (₹20K to ₹50L), low running cost | High throughput at scale — consider SLA / SLS |
| **Material waste** | Bio / compostable (PLA) required | High reuse rate (SLS powder ~80% reuse) preferred |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]** *Define Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM). List any four thermoplastic materials commonly used in FDM.*

**Model Answer (Examiner-Key Pattern):**

Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) is a material extrusion–based additive manufacturing process in which a thermoplastic filament is fed into a heated liquefier, melted, and extruded through a nozzle to deposit material layer-by-layer to form a 3D part.

Four common FDM materials:
1. **PLA (Polylactic Acid)** — biodegradable, easy to print, low warp.
2. **ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene)** — tough, heat resistant, requires enclosure.
3. **PETG (Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol)** — chemically resistant, food-safe grades.
4. **TPU (Thermoplastic Polyurethane)** — flexible elastomer (shore hardness 85A–95A).

*(Examiner's tip: Always mention the category "material extrusion" and at least one material property for full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** *Explain the role of (a) the liquefier, and (b) the build platform in an FDM system.*

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Liquefier (Hot-End):** The liquefier heats the thermoplastic filament to a temperature above its glass transition ($T_g$) and near its melting point ($T_m$), typically 180 °C – 300 °C. It provides the thermal energy required to reduce the polymer's viscosity so it can be extruded through the nozzle as a continuous bead. It contains a heater cartridge, thermistor, and nozzle in thermal contact via a metal heater block.

(b) **Build Platform:** The build platform supports the part during fabrication. In most FDM machines, it is **heated (50 °C – 110 °C)** to reduce thermal gradients and minimise **warping**, especially in ABS and PC. The surface (PEI, glass + adhesive, or BuildTak) is chosen to maximise **first-layer adhesion** and allow part removal after cooling.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) with Internal Choice

> **Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern:** Per KTU 2024 Scheme, candidates answer EITHER Q(A) OR Q(B) — both carry 14 marks and are sub-divided as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Pattern]**

**Q(A)(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of the Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) process. (7 Marks)

**Q(A)(b)** Discuss any seven process parameters that influence the mechanical properties and surface finish of FDM-printed parts. (7 Marks)

#### Full Model Solution for Q(A)

**Part (a) — Working Principle of FDM:**

The FDM process consists of **five sequential subsystems** working in synchrony:

1. **Filament Feed:** A thermoplastic filament (typically 1.75 mm diameter PLA, ABS, or PETG) is unspooled and pushed by a pinch-roller drive into the liquefier.
2. **Melting in the Liquefier:** Inside the liquefier, a heater block raises the filament temperature above its glass transition and melting points. A thermistor provides closed-loop temperature feedback to the controller.
3. **Extrusion Through the Nozzle:** The molten polymer is forced through a fine nozzle (typically 0.4 mm) under pressure from the incoming solid filament. The volumetric flow rate equals the feed rate of the solid filament.
4. **Deposition in XY:** The print head moves in the X-Y plane along a programmed path, depositing a bead of width $w_r$ and height $h_L$ (layer thickness) on the build platform.
5. **Z-Increment:** After completing one layer, the build platform (or print head) moves down (or up) by one layer thickness, and the next layer is deposited on top. Each new layer bonds to the previous via polymer chain inter-diffusion at the elevated interface temperature.

The cycle repeats until the part is complete, after which **support structures** (printed in a dissolvable or break-away material) are removed, and the part is post-processed as required.

**Part (b) — Seven Process Parameters Affecting Quality:**

| # | Parameter | Effect on Mechanical Properties | Effect on Surface Finish |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Layer Thickness ($h_L$)** | Smaller $h_L$ = more inter-layer bonds, higher strength. | Smaller $h_L$ = smoother surface. |
| 2 | **Raster Width ($w_r$)** | Wider rasters = fewer interfaces = higher XY strength. | Marginal effect. |
| 3 | **Raster Angle ($\theta$)** | ±45° alternating layers = more isotropic; 0°/90° = highly anisotropic. | Marginal effect. |
| 4 | **Air Gap ($g_a$)** | Negative = better bonding; positive = porous = weak. | Negative = glossy; positive = rough. |
| 5 | **Build Orientation** | Z-orientation = weak Z-strength; XY-orientation = stronger. | Vertical surfaces (90° to bed) have staircase; 45° surfaces are roughest. |
| 6 | **Extrusion Temperature** | Higher = better inter-layer adhesion. | Higher = risk of stringing, oozing. |
| 7 | **Infill Density / Pattern** | Strength ∝ infill density. Gyroid/honeycomb more isotropic. | Internal — no surface effect. |

**Marks Allocation Insight (7 marks):**
- 1 mark for the schematic
- 2 marks for the 5 sequential sub-steps
- 2 marks for the bond/cooling mechanism
- 1 mark for support structures mention
- 1 mark for the table (b)

---

#### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) **[Alternative Internal Choice]**

**Q(B)(a)** Derive the relationship between filament feed velocity, print speed, raster width, layer thickness, and filament diameter in FDM. A 1.75 mm filament is fed at 2.5 mm/s with a 0.4 mm nozzle, layer thickness 0.2 mm. Find the print speed. (7 Marks)

**Q(B)(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of FDM compared to other AM processes. State four typical industrial applications of FDM. (7 Marks)

#### Full Model Solution for Q(B)

**Part (a) — Derivation + Numerical:**

From conservation of volumetric flow rate (steady state, incompressible melt):

$$Q_{in} = Q_{out} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_f = w_r \cdot h_L \cdot v_p$$

Solving for the print speed:

$$v_p = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2 v_f}{4 w_r h_L}$$

**Substituting** the given values (with $d_f = 1.75 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $v_f = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s, $w_r = 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $h_L = 0.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m):

$$v_p = \dfrac{\pi \times (1.75 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times (0.4 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.2 \times 10^{-3})}$$

Numerator:
$$\pi \times 3.0625 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \approx 24.05 \times 10^{-9}$$

Denominator:
$$4 \times 0.4 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-6} = 0.32 \times 10^{-6} = 320 \times 10^{-9}$$

Therefore:

$$v_p = \dfrac{24.05 \times 10^{-9}}{320 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 0.0751 \text{ m/s} \approx 75 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations, Applications:**

**Advantages of FDM:**
1. Low capital cost and low material cost.
2. Wide range of thermoplastics including engineering grades (PC, ULTEM, Nylon).
3. Office-safe (no toxic resins, no powders, no lasers).
4. Good for large parts (up to 1 m industrial build volumes).
5. Simple workflow; minimal post-processing for functional parts.

**Limitations of FDM:**
1. **Anisotropy** — Z-direction strength is much lower than XY.
2. **Staircase effect** on inclined surfaces limits dimensional accuracy.
3. **Layer adhesion** depends on operator-settable parameters.
4. **Nozzle clogging**, **warping**, and **stringing** are common defects.
5. Slow build time compared to powder-bed processes for equivalent volume.
6. Limited resolution (≥100 µm minimum feature) compared to vat photopolymerisation.

**Four Industrial Applications:**
1. **Functional prototypes** in product design (jigs, fixtures).
2. **End-use parts** in low-volume manufacturing (e.g., aerospace ducting, automotive interior trim).
3. **Biomedical models** for surgical planning and custom prosthetics (e.g., cranial implants in PEEK).
4. **Tooling** — low-cost injection mould inserts, drill guides, and casting patterns.
5. *(Optional 5th)* Architectural scale models and education.

**Marks Allocation Insight (7 marks):**
- 2 marks: correct derivation
- 2 marks: substitution and arithmetic
- 1 mark: units and final answer
- 1 mark: advantages (2–3 listed)
- 1 mark: applications (4 listed)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **"FDM = FFF" confusion:** Examiners accept both, but you must **define** whichever term you use. Blindly using "FDM" without saying it is a Stratasys trademark loses 0.5 mark in definition questions.
> 2. **Staircase error formula:** Students frequently write $e_s = h_L \sin(\alpha)$ or $e_s = h_L \tan(\alpha)$ — both **wrong**. The correct form is $e_s = h_L \cos(\alpha)$ (peak-to-valley) or $h_L/2 \cos(\alpha)$ (midpoint).
> 3. **Volumetric flow numerical:** Do **not** forget to convert all dimensions to **metres** before substitution. A common error is mixing mm and m, leading to answers off by a factor of $10^3$ to $10^6$.
> 4. **Anisotropy direction:** When asked "which direction is weakest?", answer is **Z-direction (build direction, inter-layer)** — not "vertical," because orientation can reorient the build axis.
> 5. **Process parameters table:** Listing parameters without stating their **effect** earns only 1 of 2 marks per pair. Always pair the parameter with its mechanical/visual consequence.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this before the exam!):**

- ✅ FDM = **Material Extrusion** (ASTM 52900); FFF is the open-source equivalent.
- ✅ Filament diameter standard: **1.75 mm** (or 2.85 mm); nozzle standard: **0.4 mm**.
- ✅ Five subsystems: **Feed, Liquefier, Motion, Platform, Chamber**.
- ✅ Four sub-processes: **Feed → Melt → Extrude → Solidify**.
- ✅ **Conservation of volume** is the basis of the $v_f = \dfrac{4 w_r h_L v_p}{\pi d_f^2}$ derivation — most important equation.
- ✅ **Layer thickness** reduces strength anisotropy and improves surface finish; **raster angle** ±45° alternation gives near-isotropic XY behaviour.
- ✅ **Z-direction is the weakest** mechanical axis due to limited polymer chain inter-diffusion between cooled layers.
- ✅ **Build orientation** affects strength, surface finish (staircase), and support requirements.
- ✅ **Air gap**: negative = strong & dense; positive = porous & weak; zero = balanced.
- ✅ Staircase error: $e_s = h_L \cos(\alpha)$ — minimum at $\alpha = 90°$ (vertical wall).
- ✅ Industrial FDM materials: **PEEK, ULTEM (PEI), PC, Nylon, Carbon-fibre reinforced** variants.
- ✅ Defects to remember: **Warping, Stringing, Layer Delamination, Under-extrusion, Z-wobble, Elephant's foot, Pillowing**.
- ✅ Process invented by **S. Scott Crump, 1989**; FDM patent expired **2009** → RepRap revolution.
- ✅ Build time ≈ $\dfrac{V_{part}}{w_r h_L v_p} \times f_o$ with overhead factor $f_o \in [1.5, 3]$.
- ✅ Chamber heating (60 °C – 90 °C) is **critical** for engineering thermoplastics (PC, ULTEM) to avoid warping and to improve Z-strength.
- ✅ Part cooling fan: **ON** for PLA (rapid solidification); **OFF** for ABS (to avoid thermal shock and cracking).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET525 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING AND 3D PRINTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** is a powder bed fusion (PBF) based additive manufacturing process defined under the ASTM/ISO 52900 standard (Process Category: **Material Extrusion / Powder Bed Fusion — Polymer**), in which a high-power laser (typically a **CO₂ laser** of wavelength **10.6 μm** for polymers, or a fiber/Yb-fiber laser for metals) selectively scans and sinters (heats to a temperature just below the melting point, fusing particles via diffusion bonding) successive layers of powdered material spread over a build platform. It is a non-extrusion-based 7-axis-class freeform fabrication technique predominantly used for producing functional prototypes and end-use parts from thermoplastic polymers, metals, ceramics, and composites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEIET525 – Module 3):**
> SLS is classified under *Powder Bed Fusion Systems* and forms the conceptual backbone for understanding advanced AM processes like SLM, DMLS, and EBM. KTU examiners frequently ask comparative questions between SLS and other PBF variants.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive View)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Snow Sandcastle & Sunlight Builder":**
> Imagine you are building a sandcastle on a beach, layer by layer. Each layer is a thin sprinkle of damp sand. Instead of using your hands to pack it, you use a magnifying glass to focus bright sunlight *only* on the exact outline of the castle. The sand under the focused sunlight fuses and hardens, while the rest of the sand remains loose. Once that layer is done, you sprinkle the next thin layer of sand on top, and repeat. The loose, unfused sand acts as a natural **support** that holds up overhanging features (arches, bridges, the castle's roof) — so you never have to build temporary scaffolding.
>
> - The **magnifying glass + sunlight** = the **laser** (heat source).
> - The **damp sand** = the **powdered material** (PA12 nylon, for example).
> - The **loose sand** around the fused outline = the **unsintered powder bed** (natural support).
> - **Sprinkling each new layer** = the **recoater / roller mechanism**.

This is exactly how SLS works — the unfused powder surrounding the part is the genius feature that **eliminates the need for dedicated support structures**, a major advantage over FDM/FFF or SLA.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Standard Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value (Polymer SLS) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Laser Type | CO₂ laser | — |
| Laser Wavelength (λ) | **10.6** | μm |
| Laser Power (P) | **5 – 50** | W |
| Scan Speed (v) | **1000 – 5000** | mm/s |
| Hatch Spacing (h) | **0.1 – 0.5** | mm |
| Layer Thickness (t) | **0.05 – 0.15** | mm |
| Build Chamber Temperature | **Just below melting point** (~170 °C for PA12) | °C |
| Oxygen Content | Inert N₂ atmosphere (< 2% O₂) | % |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why & How"

The SLS process follows a precisely choreographed sequence. Here is the structured logic with the underlying physics:

### Step 1: Pre-heating of the Build Chamber
- **Why?** Polymer powders have poor thermal conductivity. Pre-heating the entire powder bed to a temperature **T_pre ≈ T_melt − 5 to 10 °C** (just below the melting point) reduces the thermal gradient the laser must impose.
- **How?** Heated radiators (IR lamps / resistive heaters) maintain the powder bed near the crystallization temperature of the polymer. This minimizes part warping (thermal residual stresses) and reduces the laser energy required per unit volume.

### Step 2: Powder Deposition (Recoating)
- **Why?** A fresh, uniform powder layer is required to build the next cross-section.
- **How?** A **recoater blade or counter-rotating roller** spreads powder from the feed chamber to the build chamber. Layer thickness is controlled by the Z-axis piston lowering by a calibrated increment (typically **100 μm**).

### Step 3: Laser Scanning & Sintering
- **Why?** Sintering is a **diffusion-driven solid-state bonding** process. The laser energy raises the local particle temperature just enough to enable polymer chain interdiffusion across particle boundaries (and neck formation in metals).
- **How?** A **galvanometer scanner (galvo)** with two rotating mirrors directs the laser beam across the X-Y plane following the sliced 2D geometry (the cross-section from the STL file). At each point, the polymer fuses at molecular level.

### Step 4: Platform Lowering
- The build platform descends by one layer thickness. The process repeats until the part is complete.

### Step 5: Post-Processing
- The build chamber is cooled (cool-down cycle: 6–12 hours for large parts).
- Parts are **depowdered** (brushed/vacuumed) to remove unsintered powder.
- Optional: bead blasting, dyeing, infiltration (e.g., with epoxy for water-tightness), machining.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Equation: Volumetric Energy Density (VED)**
> The single most important equation in SLS process control, frequently asked in KTU exams (2-marks and 14-marks).

$$
E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3} \right]
$$

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_v$ | Volumetric Energy Density | **J/mm³** | Energy delivered per unit volume of powder |
| $P$ | Laser Power | **W** (= J/s) | Total power of the laser beam |
| $v$ | Scan Speed | **mm/s** | Speed of laser spot travel |
| $h$ | Hatch Spacing | **mm** | Distance between adjacent scan lines |
| $t$ | Layer Thickness | **mm** | Depth of each powder layer |

### Secondary KTU-Relevant Equations

| # | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_s = P / v$ | **Linear Energy Density** (J/mm) — energy per unit scan length |
| 2 | $E_a = P / (v \cdot h)$ | **Areal Energy Density** (J/mm²) — energy per unit scan area |
| 3 | $\eta_{abs} = 1 - R$ | **Absorptivity** of the powder bed (R = reflectance) |
| 4 | $D_p \propto t^{1/3}$ | Part density scales with cube root of energy (empirical) |
| 5 | $T_{pre} \approx T_m - (5 \text{ to } 10) \, °C$ | Optimal pre-heat temperature window |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Windows (Examiner's Favourite):**
> - **Too low $E_v$** → insufficient fusion, **porous, weak, "orange-peel" parts**.
> - **Too high $E_v$** → material degradation, **"growth" effect (crystallization)**, polymer burn, dimensional inaccuracy.
> - **Sweet spot for PA12:** $E_v \approx 0.05 - 0.10$ J/mm³.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | SLS Application | Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Lightweight lattice structures, ducting | PA12, PA11, TPU |
| **Medical** | Custom orthotics, prosthetics, surgical guides | PA12 (biocompatible grades) |
| **Automotive** | Functional prototypes, jigs & fixtures | PA12-GF (glass-filled) |
| **Consumer Goods** | End-use production of small batches | PA12, TPU |
| **Foundry** | Investment casting patterns (lost-wax replacement) | Polystyrene (PS), PMMA |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Volumetric Energy Density from First Principles

### Given:
A laser of power $P$ (in Watts = J/s) scans a single line of length $L$ at scan speed $v$.

### Step 1: Time of laser interaction over a single scan line
The time the laser spends over a length $L$ at scan speed $v$ is:
$$
\tau = \frac{L}{v}
$$
where $\tau$ is in seconds.

### Step 2: Total energy deposited per scan line
Energy is power multiplied by time. So the energy delivered along one scan line of length $L$ is:
$$
E_{line} = P \cdot \tau = P \cdot \frac{L}{v} \quad [\text{J}]
$$

### Step 3: Define hatch spacing $h$ and effective area
Adjacent scan lines are separated by hatch spacing $h$ (in mm). The effective area swept by a single scan line of length $L$ is:
$$
A = L \cdot h \quad [\text{mm}^2]
$$

### Step 4: Areal Energy Density
The energy per unit area of the sintered cross-section is:
$$
E_a = \frac{E_{line}}{A} = \frac{P \cdot L / v}{L \cdot h} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^2} \right]
$$

### Step 5: Volumetric Energy Density
The sintered volume is the area multiplied by layer thickness $t$. The energy per unit volume of the powder is therefore:
$$
E_v = \frac{E_a}{t} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3} \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU exam equation** for SLS. Memorize both the derivation and the units — examiners award full marks only when both are stated.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Time Required to Build a Part

### Given:
A solid cuboid part of dimensions $L_x \times L_y \times L_z$ mm, with layer thickness $t$ and hatch spacing $h$.

### Step 1: Number of layers
$$
N_{layers} = \frac{L_z}{t}
$$

### Step 2: Number of scan lines per layer (in X direction)
$$
N_{lines} = \frac{L_y}{h}
$$

### Step 3: Total scan length over the entire build
$$
L_{total} = N_{lines} \cdot N_{layers} \cdot L_x = \frac{L_y}{h} \cdot \frac{L_z}{t} \cdot L_x
$$
$$
L_{total} = \frac{L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z}{h \cdot t}
$$

### Step 4: Total laser on-time
$$
t_{laser} = \frac{L_{total}}{v} = \frac{L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{s}]
$$

### Step 5: Total energy consumed
$$
E_{total} = P \cdot t_{laser} = \frac{P \cdot L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = E_v \cdot L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This proves a beautiful result: **Total Energy = Energy Density × Part Volume.** This is a direct consequence of the volumetric energy density definition.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 50 mm × 30 mm × 20 mm PA12 part is built in an SLS machine with laser power P = 30 W, scan speed v = 2500 mm/s, hatch spacing h = 0.25 mm, and layer thickness t = 0.10 mm. Calculate:
> (a) Volumetric Energy Density
> (b) Total energy required to build the part
> (c) Total laser on-time

### Part (a): Volumetric Energy Density
$$
E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{30}{2500 \times 0.25 \times 0.10}
$$
$$
E_v = \frac{30}{62.5} = 0.48 \;\text{J/mm}^3
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students compute $E_v = 0.048$ J/mm³ by mistakenly dividing by 10. Always double-check the denominator.

### Part (b): Total Energy
$$
E_{total} = E_v \cdot V = 0.48 \times (50 \times 30 \times 20) = 0.48 \times 30000
$$
$$
E_{total} = 14400 \;\text{J} = 14.4 \;\text{kJ}
$$

### Part (c): Total Laser On-Time
$$
t_{laser} = \frac{L_{total}}{v} = \frac{L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{30000}{2500 \times 0.25 \times 0.10} = \frac{30000}{62.5}
$$
$$
t_{laser} = 480 \;\text{s} = 8 \;\text{minutes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: 8 minutes is the *laser on-time only*. Actual build time will be longer (typically hours to a full day) due to recoater travel, chamber heating/cooling, and the slow Z-axis stepping.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: SLS vs. Other PBF Processes

| Parameter | SLS (Polymer) | SLM (Metal) | EBM (Metal) | DMLS (Metal) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heat Source | CO₂ Laser | Fiber Laser | Electron Beam | Fiber Laser |
| Sintering/Melting | **Sintering** (sub-melt) | **Full Melting** | Full Melting | Full Melting |
| Atmosphere | Inert N₂ | Inert Ar | Vacuum | Inert N₂/Ar |
| Pre-heat | Required | Optional | Required | Required |
| Support Structures | **Not needed** (powder supports) | Required | Required | Required |
| Typical Part Density | 90–95% | 99.5%+ | 99.5%+ | 99.5%+ |
| Surface Finish (Ra) | 8–12 μm | 5–20 μm | 20–40 μm | 5–20 μm |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SLS Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of an SLS system, mapping the interaction between the optical, thermal, mechanical, and control subsystems. This is a high-yield visual for KTU descriptive questions ("Explain the working principle of SLS with a neat sketch").

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[STL File Input] --> B[Slicer Software]
    B --> C[2D Layer Cross-Sections]
    C --> D[Control Computer]
    D --> E[Galvanometer Scanner X-Y]
    D --> F[Z-Axis Build Platform]
    D --> G[Recoater Roller Motor]
    D --> H[IR Heater Controller]

    H --> I[Powder Bed - Pre-heated to T_pre]
    I --> J[Build Chamber]
    G --> J
    F --> J

    K[CO2 Laser Source 10.6 um] --> L[Beam Shaper and F-Theta Lens]
    L --> E
    E --> M[Laser Spot on Powder Bed]
    M --> N[Sintered Cross-Section]
    N --> O[Layer N+1 Powder Deposition]
    O --> P[Platform Descends by Layer Thickness t]

    P --> Q{All Layers Done?}
    Q -->|No| M
    Q -->|Yes| R[Cool-Down Cycle 6 to 12 hours]
    R --> S[Depowdering and Post-Processing]
    S --> T[Finished SLS Part]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Stepwise Sequential Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Preheat Chamber to T_melt - 5 to 10 C] --> S2[Step 2: Lower Build Platform by t]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Recoater Spreads Fresh Powder Layer]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Laser Scans 2D Cross-Section]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Powder Sinters via Diffusion Bonding]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Platform Descends Again]
    S6 --> S7{All Layers Complete?}
    S7 -->|No| S3
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Step 7: Cool-Down and Depowder]
    S8 --> S9[Final Part Extraction]
```

## 4.3 Key Subsystem Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OpticalSubsystem
        O1[CO2 Laser] --> O2[Beam Expander]
        O2 --> O3[Galvo Mirrors X and Y]
        O3 --> O4[F-Theta Lens]
        O4 --> O5[Focused Spot on Powder]
    end

    subgraph ThermalSubsystem
        T1[IR Radiant Heaters] --> T2[Powder Bed]
        T2 --> T3[Thermocouples]
        T3 --> T4[PID Controller]
        T4 --> T1
    end

    subgraph MechanicalSubsystem
        M1[Recoater Roller] --> M2[Feed Chamber]
        M2 --> M3[Build Chamber]
        M3 --> M4[Z-Axis Piston]
    end

    subgraph AtmoControl
        A1[N2 Inlet] --> A2[Chamber]
        A2 --> A3[O2 Sensor]
        A3 --> A4[Safety Valve lt 2 percent O2]
    end

    O5 -.interacts with.-> T2
    M1 -.deposits powder to.-> T2
    A2 -.encloses.-> T2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** When drawing SLS in your exam, **always label**:
> 1. The CO₂ laser source
> 2. The recoater direction
> 3. The build platform and feed platform
> 4. The IR heaters
> 5. The N₂ inlet for inert atmosphere
> 6. The galvo scanner path
> A labeled diagram with these 6 elements typically fetches **3–4 out of 7 marks** in a KTU 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEIET525]**
*Define Selective Laser Sintering (SLS). Mention any two advantages of SLS over FDM.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) is a powder bed fusion based additive manufacturing process in which a high-power laser (typically a CO₂ laser) selectively sinters powdered material (polymers, metals, or ceramics) layer by layer to form a 3D solid part, as per the sliced geometry of a CAD model.

**Two Advantages over FDM (1.5 marks):**
1. **No support structures required** — the surrounding unsintered powder acts as a natural support, enabling fabrication of complex overhangs and internal features.
2. **Superior mechanical properties** — SLS parts are isotropic and exhibit higher strength and fatigue resistance compared to FDM parts, which suffer from layer-line weakness along the Z-axis.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEIET525]**
*State the Volumetric Energy Density equation for SLS and explain the effect of increasing scan speed on part density.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Equation (1.5 marks):**
$$
E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$
where $P$ = laser power, $v$ = scan speed, $h$ = hatch spacing, $t$ = layer thickness.

**Effect of Increasing Scan Speed (1.5 marks):**
As scan speed $v$ **increases**, the volumetric energy density $E_v$ **decreases inversely**. This means less energy is deposited per unit volume, leading to **insufficient sintering**, **reduced neck formation** between particles, **lower part density**, and **higher porosity**. If $v$ is increased too much, the part becomes mechanically weak and may exhibit "orange-peel" surface texture.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEIET525 Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of the Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) process. Label all major components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 60 mm × 40 mm × 25 mm cuboid part is to be built in an SLS machine using PA12 powder. The process parameters are: laser power $P = 24$ W, scan speed $v = 2000$ mm/s, hatch spacing $h = 0.20$ mm, layer thickness $t = 0.10$ mm. Calculate:
  (i) Volumetric Energy Density
  (ii) Total energy consumed
  (iii) Total laser on-time
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Working Principle (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
A labeled diagram showing:
- CO₂ laser source
- Galvanometer scanner (X-Y mirrors)
- F-theta lens
- Powder feed chamber (with rising piston)
- Recoater roller/blade
- Build chamber (with descending piston)
- IR heaters (above powder bed)
- N₂ inlet for inert atmosphere
- Control computer (slicer output)

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
1. The CAD model is sliced into 2D cross-sections by the slicer software. **(0.5)**
2. The build chamber is pre-heated by IR heaters to a temperature just below the melting point of PA12 (~170 °C), to reduce thermal gradients and warping. **(0.5)**
3. The recoater roller spreads a thin, uniform layer of PA12 powder (thickness = $t$) from the feed chamber to the build chamber. **(0.5)**
4. The CO₂ laser (10.6 μm) is directed by the galvo scanner to sinter the powder at the locations specified by the current cross-section. Sintering occurs via diffusion bonding at the particle interfaces (neck formation). **(1.0)**
5. The build platform descends by $t$, the feed platform rises, and a fresh powder layer is deposited. **(0.5)**
6. Steps 3–5 repeat until all layers are built. **(0.5)**
7. The chamber undergoes a controlled cool-down (6–12 hours), after which the part is removed, depowdered, and post-processed. **(0.5)**

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $L_x = 60$ mm, $L_y = 40$ mm, $L_z = 25$ mm, $P = 24$ W, $v = 2000$ mm/s, $h = 0.20$ mm, $t = 0.10$ mm.

**Part (i): Volumetric Energy Density (2 marks)**
$$
E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{24}{2000 \times 0.20 \times 0.10} = \frac{24}{40} = 0.60 \;\text{J/mm}^3
$$

**Part (ii): Total Energy Consumed (2.5 marks)**
Volume of part: $V = 60 \times 40 \times 25 = 60000$ mm³
$$
E_{total} = E_v \times V = 0.60 \times 60000 = 36000 \;\text{J} = 36 \;\text{kJ}
$$

**Part (iii): Total Laser On-Time (2.5 marks)**
$$
t_{laser} = \frac{L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{60000}{2000 \times 0.20 \times 0.10} = \frac{60000}{40} = 1500 \;\text{s} = 25 \;\text{minutes}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, PEIET525 Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the role of each of the following in SLS:
  (i) Pre-heating of the powder bed
  (ii) Inert atmosphere (N₂)
  (iii) Recoater blade/roller
  (iv) Hatch spacing
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare SLS with FDM and SLA on the following parameters: layer thickness range, support structure requirement, surface finish, material range, and part isotropy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Role of Components (7 marks)

**(i) Pre-heating (1.75 marks):**
The powder bed is pre-heated to a temperature $T_{pre} \approx T_m - (5 \text{ to } 10)°C$, typically ~170 °C for PA12. This:
- Reduces the thermal gradient imposed by the laser → minimizes **warping and residual stress**.
- Reduces laser energy required per layer → **faster sintering**.
- Maintains the powder near its crystallization temperature → enables uniform sintering.

**(ii) Inert Atmosphere (1.75 marks):**
N₂ gas is purged into the build chamber to maintain oxygen content < 2%. This:
- **Prevents oxidation** of metal powders (in metal SLS).
- **Prevents polymer degradation / charring** at the high laser temperatures.
- Eliminates fire risk with fine polymer powders (which are combustible).

**(iii) Recoater Blade/Roller (1.75 marks):**
The recoater deposits a uniform, controlled-thickness layer of fresh powder on the build platform before each laser pass. It:
- Ensures **uniform layer thickness** (critical for dimensional accuracy).
- Compacts the powder slightly → improves **powder bed density** → better part density.
- Distributes powder from the feed chamber to the build chamber.

**(iv) Hatch Spacing (1.75 marks):**
Hatch spacing $h$ is the distance between adjacent laser scan lines. It:
- Determines the **overlap** between consecutive scan lines → affects inter-track bonding.
- Smaller $h$ → higher $E_v$ → better inter-track fusion (but slower build).
- Larger $h$ → lower $E_v$ → weaker inter-track bonding → anisotropic parts.
- Typical value: **0.1 – 0.5 mm**.

#### Model Solution (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | **SLS** | **FDM** | **SLA** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Layer Thickness | 0.05 – 0.15 mm | 0.10 – 0.40 mm | 0.01 – 0.10 mm |
| Support Structures | **Not needed** (powder supports) | Required (for overhangs > 45°) | Required (for overhangs & islands) |
| Surface Finish | Moderate (Ra 8–12 μm) | Poor (Ra 15–30 μm, visible layer lines) | Excellent (Ra 1–5 μm) |
| Material Range | PA12, PA11, TPU, PS, metals, ceramics | ABS, PLA, PETG, Nylon, TPU | Photopolymer resins, ceramics (filled) |
| Part Isotropy | **Near-isotropic** | Highly anisotropic (weak Z-axis) | Near-isotropic |

**(1.4 marks per row × 5 rows = 7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SLS with SLM** — SLS = **sintering** (sub-melting, 90–95% dense, polymer focus); SLM = **selective laser melting** (full melting, 99.5%+ dense, metal focus). Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if this distinction is missed.
> 2. **Wrong units in $E_v$** — Forgetting that $P$ is in W (J/s), the resulting unit is **J/mm³**, not W/mm³. Always state the unit.
> 3. **Stating "$E_v$ has an optimal value" without numerical range** — Always quote the **PA12 window: 0.05 – 0.10 J/mm³** for full marks.
> 4. **Missing the cool-down cycle** in the working principle — it is an essential step (6–12 hours) and worth **0.5–1 mark** in the KTU marking scheme.
> 5. **Saying "FDM has no support needed"** — wrong. FDM *always* needs support for overhangs > 45°. Only SLS avoids supports via the powder bed.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 3: SLS — One-Page Cheat Sheet**

- **Definition:** Powder Bed Fusion process using a **CO₂ laser** (10.6 μm) to sinter powdered material layer-by-layer. (Sintering = sub-melting, diffusion bonding.)
- **Master Equation:**
  $$
  E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]
  $$
- **Materials:** Polymers (PA12, PA11, TPU) are most common. Metals and ceramics possible.
- **Atmosphere:** **Inert N₂** (O₂ < 2%) — prevents oxidation & fire.
- **Pre-heat:** $T_{pre} = T_m - 5 \text{ to } 10 \, °C$ (e.g., **170 °C** for PA12).
- **Build chamber temperature** must be precisely controlled — too low → warping; too high → "caking" of unsintered powder.
- **Typical parameters:** $P$ = 5–50 W, $v$ = 1000–5000 mm/s, $h$ = 0.1–0.5 mm, $t$ = 0.05–0.15 mm.
- **$E_v$ sweet spot for PA12:** **0.05 – 0.10 J/mm³**.
- **Total Energy = $E_v$ × Part Volume** — a key result from the derivation.
- **No support structures** needed — the unsintered powder bed acts as natural support.
- **Advantages:** Complex geometries, no supports, good isotropy, functional end-use parts.
- **Disadvantages:** Porous (90–95% dense for polymers), rough surface (Ra 8–12 μm), long build & cool-down times.
- **Cool-down cycle:** **6–12 hours** (often longer than actual build) — examiners love asking this.
- **ASTM/ISO 52900 process category:** Powder Bed Fusion.
- **Distinguish SLS from SLM/DMLS:** SLS = sintering (polymer focus, sub-melt); SLM/DMLS = full melting (metals).
- **Total build time ≈ laser time + recoating time + heating/cooling time.** Examiners may ask for laser on-time only — read carefully.
- **Post-processing:** Depowdering → bead blasting → optional infiltration (epoxy) → dyeing.
- **Applications:** Aerospace lattices, medical orthotics, automotive jigs, investment casting patterns.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selective Laser Melting (SLM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET525 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING AND 3D PRINTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selective Laser Melting (SLM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** is a powder-bed fusion (PBF) additive manufacturing process in which a high-power, focused laser beam is used to selectively scan and **fully melt** regions of a metallic powder bed layer-by-layer under a strictly controlled inert atmosphere, producing near-fully dense (theoretical density > **99%**) functional metallic parts directly from a 3D CAD model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** SLM is classified under the *Metal Powder Bed Fusion* family. The keyword that examiners hunt for is **"FULL MELTING"** of metal powder. This is the critical discriminator between SLM and DMLS (Direct Metal Laser Sintering) — the latter only sinters the powder partially.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standards / Defining Metrics:**
> - **Laser Power (P):** typically **100 W – 1000 W** (Yb-fiber or Nd:YAG lasers, wavelength ≈ **1070 nm**)
> - **Beam Spot Diameter:** typically **50 µm – 200 µm**
> - **Layer Thickness (t):** typically **20 µm – 100 µm**
> - **Build Chamber Atmosphere:** Argon (Ar) or Nitrogen (N₂), Oxygen < **0.1 % (1000 ppm)**
> - **Bed Preheat Temperature:** up to **200 °C** (to reduce thermal gradients and residual stress)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "Microscopic Welding by a Magic Pen":**
> Imagine you are filling a tray with very fine, metallic flour (metal powder). You take a magic pen that shoots a tiny but extremely hot beam of light. Wherever you "draw" on this flour with the pen, the flour instantly **melts and fuses** into a solid metal line. You then sprinkle another thin layer of metallic flour on top, draw the next cross-section, melt it, and repeat — almost like building a brick wall, but each "brick" is welded atom-by-atom. After thousands of these layers, you dig out your final solid metal object from the powder bed. That magical pen is the **fiber laser**, the flour is the **metal powder**, and the act of drawing cross-section by cross-section is the **SLM process**.

### 1.3 Why SLM Matters in Engineering

SLM enables the fabrication of complex internal channels (e.g., conformal cooling in injection mould inserts), topology-optimized aerospace brackets, and patient-specific orthopaedic implants that are impossible to manufacture by conventional CNC machining or casting.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of the SLM Process

The SLM process can be deconstructed into four tightly coupled physical pillars that examiners frequently test:

**Pillar 1 — Energy Delivery**
- A continuous-wave (CW) or pulsed **Yb-fiber laser** delivers a Gaussian energy distribution.
- Galvanometer-driven mirrors scan the beam at programmed velocities.
- The energy input is governed by **P, v, h, t** (power, scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness).

**Pillar 2 — Powder Bed Consolidation**
- A recoater blade (or roller) spreads a fresh powder layer of thickness **t**.
- Powder morphology (spherical, flowable, size typically **15–45 µm**) is critical to avoid porosity.

**Pillar 3 — Melt Pool Dynamics**
- The laser forms a **melt pool** typically **100–200 µm wide** and **50–100 µm deep**.
- Solidification rates are extremely high (**10³–10⁶ K/s**), producing fine, non-equilibrium microstructures.

**Pillar 4 — Atmosphere & Thermal Management**
- Inert gas flow (Ar / N₂) prevents oxidation and carries away spatter/vapor plumes.
- Substrate preheating and optimized scan strategies (e.g., chessboard, island, contour-then-hatch) reduce residual stress and distortion.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Sequence

1. **CAD Slicing:** The 3D CAD model is sliced into layers of thickness **t**.
2. **Chamber Sealing & Inert Purge:** Oxygen is flushed out to < **0.1 %** to prevent metal ignition and oxidation.
3. **Substrate Lowering:** Build platform descends by **Δz = t**.
4. **Powder Deposition:** Recoater spreads a uniform powder layer.
5. **Laser Scanning:** The fiber laser selectively melts the cross-section defined by the slice.
6. **Solidification:** The melt pool cools rapidly; metallurgical bonds form with the previous layer.
7. **Repeat:** Steps 3–6 are repeated **N** times (where **N = part height / t**).
8. **Cool-down & Depowdering:** After the build, the part cools inside the chamber, then is removed and de-powdered.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear almost every KTU exam cycle.**

| # | Formula | Symbols | Engineering Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $VED = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $P$ = laser power, $v$ = scan speed, $h$ = hatch spacing, $t$ = layer thickness | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** — the single most important process control parameter | $\text{J/mm}^{3}$ |
| 2 | $VED_{\text{opt}} = 60 \text{ to } 120 \; \text{J/mm}^{3}$ | Empirical window for Ti-6Al-4V, 316L SS, Inconel 718 | Below this → lack of fusion porosity; Above this → keyhole porosity and vaporization | $\text{J/mm}^{3}$ |
| 3 | $d_{\text{melt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 P}{\pi \cdot \rho \cdot C_p \cdot v \cdot (T_m - T_0)}}$ | $d_{\text{melt}}$ = melt pool width, $\rho$ = density, $C_p$ = specific heat, $T_m$ = melting point, $T_0$ = initial temp | Approximate analytical melt-pool width from Rosenthal-type heat-transfer model | mm |
| 4 | $\dot{T} = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta T}{d_{\text{melt}}}$ | $\dot{T}$ = cooling rate, $\Delta T$ = superheat | Cooling rate governs grain size and phase selection | K/s |
| 5 | $\sigma_{\text{res}} \approx \alpha \cdot E \cdot \Delta T_{\text{eff}}$ | $\alpha$ = CTE, $E$ = Young's modulus, $\Delta T_{\text{eff}}$ = effective thermal gradient | Estimate of residual stress leading to distortion or cracking | MPa |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{build}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{part}}}{V_{\text{theoretical-powder}}} \times 100\%$ | — | Powder yield efficiency in terms of volume | % |
| 7 | $t_{\text{build}} = \dfrac{h_{\text{part}}}{t} \cdot (t_{\text{recoat}} + t_{\text{scan}})$ | $h_{\text{part}}$ = part height, $t_{\text{recoat}}$ = recoating time, $t_{\text{scan}}$ = scanning time | Total build time estimation | s or h |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pitfall Alert:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) inside these tables, KTU scripts often break. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ instead. Same applies here.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace:** Airbus A350 XWB cabin bracket brackets and rocket engine injectors (SpaceX SuperDraco).
- **Medical:** Custom cranio-facial implants and dental crowns from **Co-Cr** and **Ti-6Al-4V**.
- **Tooling:** Conformal cooling inserts for injection moulds (50 % cycle-time reduction is industry-typical).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Volumetric Energy Density (VED) from First Principles

The VED is **not** an arbitrary engineering metric — it can be derived rigorously from the energy deposited per unit volume of powder.

**Step 1 — Energy Deposited per Unit Length of Scan:**
When the laser of power $P$ moves at scan speed $v$ along a line, the time spent on a unit-length segment of that line is $\dfrac{1}{v}$ (in seconds per mm). The energy delivered to that segment is therefore:

$$E_{\text{per-length}} = P \cdot \frac{1}{v} = \frac{P}{v}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-Sectional Area of the Melted Track:**
The laser melts a track of width approximately equal to the **hatch spacing** $h$ and depth approximately equal to the **layer thickness** $t$. The cross-sectional area of the solidified track is:

$$A = h \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Volume Melted per Unit Length:**
Multiplying the cross-sectional area by a unit length gives the volume processed per unit length:

$$V_{\text{per-length}} = A \cdot 1 = h \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Energy Density Definition:**
By definition, VED is energy per unit volume, so dividing the energy per unit length by the volume per unit length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
VED &= \frac{E_{\text{per-length}}}{V_{\text{per-length}}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\dfrac{P}{v}}{h \cdot t} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{VED = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}}$

This confirms the formula in the cheat sheet. Numerically, if $P = 200\;\text{W}$, $v = 800\;\text{mm/s}$, $h = 0.12\;\text{mm}$ and $t = 0.03\;\text{mm}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
VED &= \frac{200}{800 \times 0.12 \times 0.03} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{200}{2.88} \\[4pt]
&\approx 69.44\;\text{J/mm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

This lies inside the safe processing window of **60 – 120 J/mm³** for 316L stainless steel.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Scan Speed (v*) to Avoid Lack-of-Fusion

Lack-of-fusion porosity occurs when the energy input is too low to fully remelt the substrate beneath. The depth of penetration $D$ of the melt pool can be approximated by a balance between absorbed laser power and the latent heat of fusion of the powder:

$$P_{\text{abs}} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot D \cdot H_f + Q_{\text{losses}}$$

Where $P_{\text{abs}} = \alpha \cdot P$ (with absorptivity $\alpha$ for the metal at the laser's wavelength) and $H_f$ is the latent heat of fusion. Ignoring conduction losses for a first-order estimate and setting $D \geq t$ (so each layer fully fuses to the previous one):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha \cdot P &\geq \rho \cdot h \cdot t \cdot v \cdot H_f \\[4pt]
v^{*} &\leq \frac{\alpha \cdot P}{\rho \cdot h \cdot t \cdot H_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical Meaning:** $v^{*}$ is the **maximum permissible scan speed** before the melt pool becomes too shallow, leaving inter-layer voids.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cooling Rate and its Effect on Microstructure

Using the Rosenthal line-source solution for a moving point heat source, the cooling rate $\dot{T}$ at the trailing edge of the melt pool scales as:

$$\dot{T} \propto \frac{v \cdot (T_m - T_0)}{d_{\text{melt}}}$$

**Implication for grain size:** Using a Hall–Petch-type relationship for cellular/dendritic spacing $\lambda_2$:

$$\lambda_2 = A \cdot \dot{T}^{-n} \quad \text{with} \quad n \approx 0.3 \text{ to } 0.5$$

**Interpretation:** Higher scan speed $v$ → higher cooling rate → finer microstructure → higher yield strength (a hallmark of SLM-processed alloys compared to wrought counterparts).

---

### 3.4 Process–Property–Defect Mapping (Analytical Matrix)

| Process Parameter | Increase → Effect on Density | Increase → Effect on Microstructure | Increase → Effect on Residual Stress |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P$ (Laser Power) | Reduces lack-of-fusion; can cause keyhole porosity if too high | Coarser grains; possible columnar growth | Higher thermal gradient → higher stress |
| $v$ (Scan Speed) | Finer features; risk of lack-of-fusion if too high | Finer grains (high $\dot{T}$) | Lower heat input → lower stress |
| $h$ (Hatch Spacing) | Too wide → inter-track voids; too narrow → over-melting | Affects texture and anisotropy | Minor effect |
| $t$ (Layer Thickness) | Thinner layers → denser parts; thicker → faster build | Smaller columnar grain aspect ratio | Thinner layers → more thermal cycles → potentially higher distortion |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SLM Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CAD Model Input] --> B[Slicer Software]
    B --> C[Layer Data + Scan Path]
    C --> D[Control Computer]
    D --> E[Galvanometer Scanner]
    D --> F[Laser Power Supply]
    D --> G[Recoater Drive]
    D --> H[Build Platform Z-Axis]
    F --> I[Yb-Fiber Laser]
    I --> J[F-Theta Lens]
    J --> E
    E --> K[Melt Pool on Powder Bed]
    G --> L[Powder Feed Chamber]
    H --> M[Substrate Plate]
    L --> N[Inert Gas System]
    N --> O[Build Chamber]
    O --> K
    K --> P[Process Monitoring]
    P --> D
    O --> Q[Filter and Recycle Unit]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Control Computer** orchestrates the laser, scanner, recoater, and Z-axis.
- The **Inert Gas System** continuously flushes the **Build Chamber** to keep O₂ < 0.1 %.
- **Process Monitoring** (melt-pool cameras, pyrometers) feeds back to the controller for closed-loop quality control — a high-end feature in modern SLM systems.

---

### 4.2 SLM Process Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: CAD Slicing] --> S2[Step 2: Chamber Sealing and Inert Purge]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Substrate Preheating]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Powder Recoating]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Laser Melting of Cross-Section]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Layer-wise Solidification]
    S6 --> S7{Layer N Reached?}
    S7 -- No --> S4
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Step 7: Controlled Cooling]
    S8 --> S9[Step 8: Depowdering and Part Recovery]
    S9 --> S10[Step 9: Post-Processing HIP or Heat Treatment]
```

**Interpretation for Examiners:** Always remember that the loop **S4 → S5 → S6** repeats N times. State **N = part height / layer thickness** explicitly for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Selective Laser Melting (SLM). List any two key differences between SLM and DMLS.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 Marks):** SLM is a powder-bed fusion additive manufacturing process in which a high-power fiber laser is used to **fully melt** metallic powder particles layer-by-layer under an inert atmosphere, producing near-fully dense (≥ 99 %) metal parts directly from CAD data.
- **Differences (½ × 2 = 1 Mark):**
  1. **Melting State:** SLM achieves *full melting*; DMLS performs *partial sintering* with a binder phase.
  2. **Material Range:** SLM uses single-element or pre-alloyed powders (316L, Ti6Al4V, Inconel 718, AlSi10Mg); DMLS was originally marketed for multi-material / bronze-steel blends.
  3. **Density:** SLM gives higher densification (> 99 %) compared to DMLS (typically 90 – 95 %).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is Volumetric Energy Density (VED) in SLM? State the standard processing window for a titanium alloy.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 Marks):** VED is the laser energy delivered per unit volume of powder, given by
$$VED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$
where $P$ = laser power, $v$ = scan speed, $h$ = hatch spacing, $t$ = layer thickness. Unit: J/mm³.
- **Ti-6Al-4V Window (1 Mark):** The empirical optimum is **60 – 120 J/mm³** (typically 65 – 95 J/mm³). Below this → lack-of-fusion porosity; above this → keyhole porosity and balling.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** A 14-mark question has **internal choice**. Two alternates are provided. Both sub-parts typically carry **7 + 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of the **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** process. List four key process parameters that govern the final part density. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a Ti-6Al-4V part built in an SLM machine, the following process parameters are used: laser power $P = 280\;\text{W}$, scan speed $v = 1100\;\text{mm/s}$, hatch spacing $h = 0.10\;\text{mm}$ and layer thickness $t = 0.03\;\text{mm}$.
  (i) Calculate the Volumetric Energy Density. **(3 Marks)**
  (ii) Comment on whether the part will be free of porosity. **(2 Marks)**
  (iii) If the layer thickness is increased to $t = 0.06\;\text{mm}$, what happens to the VED? Suggest one corrective action. **(2 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle (7 Marks):**
- **Step 1 – Slicing (1 Mark):** A 3D CAD model is sliced into uniform layers (typically 20 – 50 µm).
- **Step 2 – Inert Atmosphere (1 Mark):** The build chamber is sealed and purged with argon (or nitrogen) until O₂ < 0.1 %.
- **Step 3 – Recoating (1 Mark):** A recoater blade spreads a uniform layer of metal powder over the substrate.
- **Step 4 – Laser Melting (2 Marks):** A focused Yb-fiber laser (λ ≈ 1070 nm), steered by galvanometer mirrors, selectively scans the cross-section, creating a melt pool that fully fuses the powder.
- **Step 5 – Layer-by-Layer Build (1 Mark):** The build platform descends by one layer thickness, a new powder layer is spread, and the process repeats.
- **Step 6 – Cooling & Depowdering (1 Mark):** After the build, the part is cooled under inert gas and then de-powdered.

**Four Key Process Parameters Governing Density:**
1. Laser Power (P)
2. Scan Speed (v)
3. Hatch Spacing (h)
4. Layer Thickness (t)

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**(i) VED Calculation (3 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
VED &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{280}{1100 \times 0.10 \times 0.03} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{280}{3.3} \\[4pt]
&\approx 84.85\;\text{J/mm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Porosity Comment (2 Marks):**
The calculated VED of **≈ 84.85 J/mm³** lies comfortably inside the safe processing window of **60 – 120 J/mm³** for Ti-6Al-4V. Therefore, the part is **likely free of lack-of-fusion porosity**, provided atmospheric control is maintained (O₂ < 0.1 %).

**(iii) Effect of Increasing Layer Thickness (2 Marks):**
New $t = 0.06\;\text{mm}$. New VED:
$$
\begin{aligned}
VED_{\text{new}} &= \frac{280}{1100 \times 0.10 \times 0.06} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{280}{6.6} \\[4pt]
&\approx 42.42\;\text{J/mm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

The VED **drops to 42.42 J/mm³**, which is **below the safe window** → **lack-of-fusion porosity** will result.

**Corrective Action (1 of the following):**
- Reduce the scan speed $v$ to ~ 550 mm/s to restore VED ≈ 85 J/mm³, **or**
- Increase laser power $P$ to ~ 560 W.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the role of the **inert atmosphere** in SLM. What happens if oxygen content exceeds 0.1 %? Name two commonly used inert gases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **lack-of-fusion porosity** and **keyhole porosity** in SLM. For a 316L stainless steel build, the VED was measured as 35 J/mm³. Identify the dominant defect and explain its morphology using a labelled schematic description. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of Inert Atmosphere (7 Marks):**
- **Function 1 – Oxidation Prevention (2 Marks):** At SLM operating temperatures (> 1500 °C in the melt pool), metals like Ti, Al, and Cr would catastrophically oxidize. The inert gas (Ar or N₂) keeps O₂ < 1000 ppm.
- **Function 2 – Spatter Removal (2 Marks):** Gas flow (typically laminar) carries away ejected spatter and metal vapor plumes, preventing redeposition defects and protecting the laser optics.
- **Function 3 – Thermal Regulation (1 Mark):** Inert gas flow provides a uniform convective cooling environment, reducing temperature gradients.
- **Function 4 – Plasma Suppression (1 Mark):** Suppresses laser-induced plasma which can scatter/defocus the beam.
- **Inert Gases (1 Mark):** **Argon (Ar)** — most common; **Nitrogen (N₂)** — used for some steels and Cr-containing alloys.

**Effect of O₂ > 0.1 %:**
- Severe oxidation → loss of mechanical properties.
- Possible **ignition/explosion** with Ti and Al powders (these are pyrophoric).
- **Nitrogen pick-up** if N₂ is incorrectly used with Ti (forms brittle TiN).

---

**(b) Porosity Differentiation (7 Marks):**

**Lack-of-Fusion Porosity (2 Marks):**
- Cause: Insufficient energy input (low VED, typically < 50 J/mm³).
- Morphology: **Irregular, flat-shaped voids** with unmelted powder particles visible inside.
- Location: Typically **inter-layer** or **inter-track** boundaries.

**Keyhole Porosity (2 Marks):**
- Cause: Excessive energy input (very high VED, often > 150 J/mm³) causing vaporization of metal.
- Morphology: **Spherical, smooth-walled voids** formed by trapped gas bubbles from vaporized metal.
- Location: Random within the bulk.

**Case Analysis for 316L with VED = 35 J/mm³ (3 Marks):**
- The VED of **35 J/mm³ is well below** the safe processing window of **60 – 120 J/mm³** for 316L.
- **Dominant Defect: Lack-of-Fusion Porosity.**
- **Morphology:** The voids will be **irregular, elongated, and lie along the inter-layer or inter-track planes**, often containing entrapped, partially-melted powder particles.
- **Labelled Schematic (Textual Form):**
  - Imagine a stack of horizontal stripes (representing layers).
  - Between two adjacent stripes, **flat, lens-shaped voids** form, parallel to the build plane.
  - **Unmelted spherical powder particles** are visible inside these voids under SEM.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Read this carefully before the exam):**
> 1. **"SLM is the same as DMLS"** — Wrong. SLM = *full melting*; DMLS = *partial sintering*. Cost at least 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing "Energy Density" units:** VED uses $\text{J/mm}^{3}$, NOT $\text{J/m}^{3}$ or $\text{W/mm}^{2}$. Examiners are strict on units.
> 3. **Forgetting the inert atmosphere** in block diagrams — O₂ control is a guaranteed 1-mark gift question. Don't skip it.
> 4. **Mis-naming the laser type:** It is a **Yb-fiber laser** (Ytterbium-doped fiber), wavelength ≈ **1070 nm**, NOT a CO₂ laser. CO₂ lasers are used in polymer sintering, not metal SLM.
> 5. **Confusing "lack-of-fusion" with "keyhole" porosity:** Always tie the defect back to the VED value given in the question.
> 6. **In numerical problems, students forget to convert units** (e.g., W to mW, mm/s to mm/min). Always write units in every step.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SLM = Selective Laser Melting** — a *metal powder-bed fusion* process that uses a **Yb-fiber laser (≈ 1070 nm)** to **fully melt** metallic powder.
- **Inert Atmosphere (Ar / N₂)** is mandatory; **O₂ < 0.1 %**.
- The **Master Formula** is $VED = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ (in J/mm³).
- **Safe VED window** for most alloys (Ti6Al4V, 316L, Inconel 718): **60 – 120 J/mm³**.
- **Below window** → **Lack-of-fusion porosity** (irregular, flat, inter-layer).
- **Above window** → **Keyhole porosity** (spherical, vaporization-induced).
- **Layer thickness (t)** = 20 – 100 µm; **Beam spot** = 50 – 200 µm.
- **Cooling rates** are extremely high ($10^{3}$ – $10^{6}$ K/s) → fine, non-equilibrium microstructures.
- **Residual stress** is a major concern; mitigated by **preheating (≤ 200 °C)**, **optimized scan strategies** (chessboard, island), and **post-process HIP** (Hot Isostatic Pressing).
- **SLM ≠ DMLS** — remember the *full-melt vs. partial-sinter* distinction.
- **Build time formula:** $t_{\text{build}} = \dfrac{h_{\text{part}}}{t} \cdot (t_{\text{recoat}} + t_{\text{scan}})$.
- **Applications:** Aerospace brackets, medical implants (cranio-facial, dental), conformal cooling mould inserts.
- **Always cite process parameters** in full when answering descriptive questions: **P, v, h, t, atmosphere, scan strategy**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electron Beam Melting (EBM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET525 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING AND 3D PRINTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electron Beam Melting (EBM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of EBM

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** is a high-vacuum, powder-bed fusion (PBF-EB) additive manufacturing process defined under **ASTM F2792** as a Category 2 (Powder Bed Fusion) process in which a **high-velocity stream of accelerated electrons**, generated by thermionic emission from a tungsten filament cathode and accelerated through a potential difference of typically **30–60 kV**, is used as the localized energy source to selectively melt and consolidate successive layers of metallic powder. The build chamber is held at an elevated, pre-heated temperature (typically **650–750 °C** for Ti-6Al-4V) to minimize residual stresses, and the process is conducted under a controlled vacuum (typically **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ mbar**).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, EBM is classified under **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** with an **electron beam** as the heat source — to be contrasted with **Selective Laser Melting (SLM/DMLS)** which uses a photon-based laser source. The technology was commercialized by **Arcam AB (Sweden)** — now operating under **GE Additive** — and represents one of the two industrial flagship PBF processes for metals.

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — "A Vaccum-Sealed Lightning Sculptor"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you are sculpting a metal object inside a sealed, airless glass chamber. Instead of touching the metal with tools, you fire a precisely-aimed, hair-thin beam of **millions of electrons** (miniature, weightless "bullets" from a tungsten wire) at a bed of fine metallic sand. Where this electron stream strikes, the sand grains instantly heat to above their melting point (~1660 °C for titanium) and fuse together. The gun then moves on, the platform drops a fraction of a hair's width (typically 50–200 µm), a new layer of sand is rolled over the top, and the process repeats — stacking layer upon layer until a fully dense metal part is sculpted from dust.

The "vacuum" is essential because air molecules would scatter the electrons, ruining the beam's focus. The "pre-heated chamber" acts like a warm oven that keeps the part from cracking due to sudden thermal shocks — analogous to how glass is slowly cooled in a lehr to prevent shattering.

## 1.3 Distinguishing Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard EBM process metrics are used throughout the KTU syllabus and are required for numerical problem-solving:

| Parameter | Typical Value (Industrial EBM) | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Accelerating Voltage (V)** | **60** | kV |
| **Beam Current (I)** | 1 – 50 (typical 10–30) | mA |
| **Beam Power (P)** | 50 – 4500 (typical 1000–3000) | W |
| **Beam Spot Diameter (d)** | 100 – 400 | µm |
| **Layer Thickness (t)** | 50 – 200 | µm |
| **Chamber Vacuum** | **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵** | mbar |
| **Build Plate Temperature (T₀)** | **650 – 750** | °C |
| **Scan Velocity (v)** | 100 – 10,000 | mm/s |
| **Hatch Spacing (h)** | 50 – 200 | µm |
| **Chamber Pressure (He partial)** | ~10⁻² (helium backfill for cooling) | mbar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The accelerating voltage of **60 kV** is the *defining signature* of Arcam-style EBM systems. Always quote this value for "standard industrial" EBM unless the question states otherwise. Also note the process is **NOT** the same as Electron Beam Welding (EBW) — the difference is that EBM scans across a powder bed layer-by-layer, while EBW is a joining process for bulk material.

## 1.4 Position in the ASTM F2792 Taxonomy

Under the **ASTM F2792-22** standard (cited in the KTU 2024 PEIET525 syllabus), EBM is classified as:

- **Category:** Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)
- **Sub-category:** PBF-EB (Electron Beam)
- **Synonyms used in industry:** EBM® (Arcam trademark), EBF (Electron Beam Fusion)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for students:** Think **"E-L-P"** — **E**lectron beam, **L**ayer-wise (powder bed), **P**hysical fusion by melting. This separates it from SLM ("S-L-P" — **S**canning **L**aser) and from DED (Directed Energy Deposition, where the energy source and material are *co-axial*, not bed-based).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Physical Operating Principle

The EBM process chain can be decomposed into the following six (6) sequential physical stages. Every KTU 14-mark question on EBM will test at least three of these stages.

1. **Electron Generation (Thermionic Emission Stage)**
   - A **tungsten filament** is resistively heated to approximately **2400–2800 °C** in vacuum.
   - At this temperature, electrons in the conduction band of tungsten acquire enough thermal energy to overcome the **work function** ($\phi \approx 4.5$ eV for W) and escape the metal surface.
   - The current density emitted follows the **Richardson-Dushman Law:**

$$J_{emission} \;=\; A_{R} \, T^{2} \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\phi}{k_{B} T}\right)$$

   - where $A_R \approx 6.0 \times 10^{5} \, \text{A/m}^2\text{K}^2$ is Richardson's constant, $T$ is filament temperature in Kelvin, and $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant.

2. **Electron Acceleration Stage**
   - The liberated electrons pass through a **Wehnelt cup** (control grid) and a **cathode-anode potential difference $V$** of 30–60 kV.
   - The electrons acquire kinetic energy $E_{kin} = eV$, where $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C.

3. **Beam Focusing Stage**
   - **Electromagnetic lenses** (magnetic coils energized by current) focus the diverging electron stream to a spot of diameter 100–400 µm at the powder bed.
   - Because electrons are charged particles (unlike photons), they can be focused by **Lorentz forces** with much higher precision than laser light.

4. **Beam Deflection Stage**
   - **Electromagnetic deflection coils** steer the beam across the build plane in the prescribed 2D slice geometry.
   - Scan speeds of **up to 10,000 mm/s** are achievable — orders of magnitude faster than SLM — because electron beams have **near-zero mass** and no photon-momentum inertia.

5. **Powder Bed Melting Stage**
   - The focused beam strikes the powder bed, converting kinetic energy into heat via atomic collisions (predominantly Coulombic scattering with metal nuclei and electrons).
   - Local temperatures exceed the melting point (e.g., 1660 °C for Ti-6Al-4V), forming a small **melt pool**.
   - A "**multi-spot melting**" strategy is used: a sequence of *contour* (outer boundary) and *hatch* (interior fill) scans per layer.

6. **Layer Deposition Stage**
   - After each layer, the build platform descends by one layer thickness **t** (50–200 µm).
   - A **raking blade/roller** spreads fresh powder from a hopper.
   - The process repeats until the part is complete.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Energy & Process Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **high-yield** — at least one formula here appears in every EBM numerical question. **Master it.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Beam Power** | $P = V \cdot I$ | W (J/s) | Total energy delivered per second |
| 2 | **Power Density** | $P_d = \dfrac{P}{\pi r^2} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^2}$ | W/m² | Energy per unit spot area |
| 3 | **Specific Energy / Energy Density (volumetric)** | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \, h \, t}$ | J/mm³ | Energy delivered per unit melt volume |
| 4 | **Linear Energy Density** | $E_l = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | J/mm | Energy per unit scan length |
| 5 | **Areal Energy Density** | $E_a = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | J/mm² | Energy per unit scanned area |
| 6 | **Beam Diameter Relation** | $d = 2r$ | µm | Spot diameter = 2 × radius |
| 7 | **Melt Pool Width (empirical)** | $w \approx k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{v}}$ | mm | Empirical for track width |
| 8 | **Melt Pool Depth (empirical)** | $h_m \approx \dfrac{P}{\rho \, c_p \, v \cdot d \cdot \Delta T}$ | mm | Heat-balance depth estimate |
| 9 | **Residual Time at Melt** | $t_{res} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | s | How long beam dwells at one spot |
| 10 | **Cooling Rate (approx.)** | $\dot{T} \approx \dfrac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{P}$ | K/s | Rosenthal-line estimate |
| 11 | **Build Rate (volume)** | $\dot{V} = v \cdot h \cdot t \cdot n$ | mm³/s | Volumetric throughput, $n$ = number of beams/scans |
| 12 | **Beam Power from Energy** | $P = \dfrac{E_v \cdot v \cdot h \cdot t}{1}$ | W | Inverse of Eq. 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical board-exam tip:** Equations (3) and (4) are *the* most tested formulas in KTU EBM questions. Memorize the denominator $v \cdot h \cdot t$ — the "triple product" of scan velocity × hatch spacing × layer thickness — because examiners *love* to ask you to compute the missing parameter given the other three.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is EBM critical in industry?** Three sectors dominate:
> 1. **Aerospace & Defense (~70% of EBM part market):** Lightweight Ti-6Al-4V brackets, turbine blades, and structural fittings. GE Aviation's LEAP fuel nozzle ring (a famous example) uses EBM.
> 2. **Medical Implants (~20%):** Titanium acetabular cups, spinal cages — EBM's elevated build plate (700 °C) produces parts with **superior ductility and fatigue life** compared to SLM as-built parts.
> 3. **High-Temperature Alloys (~10%):** Ni-based superalloys (Inconel 718, 625), Co-Cr-Mo for dental and orthopedic applications.

The high vacuum environment of EBM is **non-negotiable for reactive metals** like titanium and zirconium — any oxygen or nitrogen contamination would form brittle oxides/nitrides, ruining mechanical properties. SLM cannot match this level of atmospheric purity even with argon shielding, because argon still contains tens of ppm of contaminants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Beam Power from Voltage and Current

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the power delivered by an EBM electron beam in terms of accelerating voltage $V$ and beam current $I$, and compute the typical power for an Arcam-style system.

**Given:**
- Accelerating voltage $V = 60 \text{ kV} = 6.0 \times 10^4$ V
- Beam current $I = 30$ mA $= 3.0 \times 10^{-2}$ A
- Electron charge $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C

**Step 1 — Energy per electron:**
Each electron, falling through a potential difference $V$, gains kinetic energy:

$$E_{electron} \;=\; eV$$

**Step 2 — Number of electrons per second:**
A current of $I$ amperes corresponds to a flow of:

$$N_{electrons} \;=\; \dfrac{I}{e}$$

electrons per second, since $I = N \cdot e$.

**Step 3 — Total power (energy per second):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{beam} &= E_{electron} \times N_{electrons} \\[6pt]
&= (eV) \cdot \left(\dfrac{I}{e}\right) \\[6pt]
&= V \cdot I
\end{aligned}$$

The $e$ cancels, giving the clean result.

**Step 4 — Numerical substitution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{beam} &= (6.0 \times 10^{4} \text{ V}) \times (3.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ A}) \\[4pt]
&= 1.8 \times 10^{3} \text{ W} \\[4pt]
&= 1.8 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** A typical Arcam EBM system delivers approximately **1.8 kW** of beam power under standard operating conditions, with maximum sustained power up to 4.5 kW. This is *one to two orders of magnitude* greater than typical SLM lasers (200 W to 1 kW).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Density at the Powder Bed

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the power density $P_d$ of a focused electron beam of power $P$ and spot diameter $d$, and compute its value for a 0.2 mm spot diameter at 1.8 kW.

**Step 1 — Spot area:**
For a circular focal spot of diameter $d$, radius is $r = d/2$ and area is:

$$A_{spot} \;=\; \pi r^{2} \;=\; \pi \left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Power density definition:**
Power density is power per unit area:

$$P_{d} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{beam}}{A_{spot}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{d} &= \dfrac{P_{beam}}{\pi d^{2}/4} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{4 \, P_{beam}}{\pi \, d^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation (with $P = 1.8 \times 10^{3}$ W, $d = 0.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m):**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{spot} &= \dfrac{\pi (0.2 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 4.0 \times 10^{-8}}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{2} \\[6pt]
P_{d} &= \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{3}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 5.73 \times 10^{10} \text{ W/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $P_d \approx 5.73 \times 10^{10} \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 57.3 \text{ GW/m}^2$. This enormous power density — comparable to a focused solar laser — is why EBM can melt high-melting-point alloys like tungsten, molybdenum, and Ti alloys at high scan speeds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why EBM is faster than SLM:** Even though SLM uses tightly focused laser spots (~80 µm), the **power density** of EBM is higher because of higher absolute power (kW vs. 100s of W). The result is faster melting, deeper melt pools, and shorter build times per part.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Volumetric Energy Density
