**Statement:** Derive the volumetric energy density $E_v$ delivered to a melt track of length $L$, width $h$ (hatch spacing), and depth $t$ (layer thickness) scanned at velocity $v$ by a beam of power $P$. Then compute for $P = 1.8$ kW, $v = 1000$ mm/s, $h = 0.1$ mm, $t = 0.05$ mm.

**Step 1 — Time to scan a unit length $L$:**

$$t_{scan} \;=\; \dfrac{L}{v}$$

**Step 2 — Energy delivered during this time:**

$$E_{delivered} \;=\; P \cdot t_{scan} \;=\; P \cdot \dfrac{L}{v}$$

**Step 3 — Volume melted in this scan:**

$$V_{melted} \;=\; L \cdot h \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Energy density definition:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{v} &= \dfrac{E_{delivered}}{V_{melted}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{P \cdot L / v}{L \cdot h \cdot t} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical substitution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{v} &= \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{3} \text{ W}}{1000 \, \text{mm/s} \times 0.1 \, \text{mm} \times 0.05 \, \text{mm}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1800}{5.0} \\[6pt]
&= 360 \; \text{J/mm}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $E_v = 360$ J/mm³ — well within the typical "good-build" EBM range of 100–500 J/mm³ for Ti-6Al-4V.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold rule for KTU:** For Ti-6Al-4V, the *minimum* energy density to achieve ≥99% density is approximately **100 J/mm³**. Below this, lack-of-fusion porosity dominates. Above **500 J/mm³**, keyhole porosity and evaporation dominate. The "**100 ≤ E_v ≤ 500**" window is the **safe operating band** that KTU examiners expect you to quote.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Melting Rate (kg/h) from Process Parameters

**Statement:** Derive the *mass* melting rate $\dot{m}$ in kg/h from the volumetric build rate, given powder density $\rho$.

**Step 1 — Volumetric build rate:**

$$\dot{V} \;=\; v \cdot h \cdot t$$

(m³/s if SI; mm³/s if mm)

**Step 2 — Mass melting rate:**

$$\dot{m} \;=\; \rho \cdot \dot{V}$$

where $\rho$ is the powder material's theoretical density (Ti-6Al-4V: $\rho \approx 4.43 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 4430 \text{ kg/m}^3$).

**Step 3 — Convert to kg/h:**

$$\dot{m}_{kg/h} \;=\; \dot{m}_{kg/s} \times 3600$$

**Numerical example (same parameters as 3.3):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= 1000 \times 0.1 \times 0.05 = 5.0 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{s} \\
\dot{m} &= 4430 \text{ kg/m}^{3} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} \\
&= 2.215 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s} \\
\dot{m}_{kg/h} &= 2.215 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 \approx 0.0798 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Industrial Arcam Q20plus systems achieve actual build rates of ~80–100 cm³/h for Ti-6Al-4V (~0.35–0.45 kg/h). The theoretical calculation above matches the *order of magnitude* expected for single-pass hatch scanning; multi-spot scanning and wider hatch spacing increase this by 2–3×.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: EBM vs SLM Parameter Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests *comparative* numerical analysis. The following matrix gives typical "head-to-head" values for the **same part in Ti-6Al-4V**.

| Parameter | EBM | SLM (DMLS) | Ratio (EBM/SLM) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Power** | 1000–3000 W | 200–500 W | 5–10× | EBM melts faster |
| **Spot size** | 100–400 µm | 50–100 µm | 2–4× | SLM has finer features |
| **Layer thickness** | 50–200 µm | 20–80 µm | 2–3× | EBM is rougher vertically |
| **Scan velocity** | 1000–10,000 mm/s | 100–1500 mm/s | 5–10× | EBM is much faster |
| **Build envelope** | Ø350×430 mm | 400×400×400 mm | Comparable | |
| **Chamber environment** | Vacuum + He | Inert gas (Ar/N₂) | — | EBM is purer |
| **Pre-heat temp** | 670–750 °C | 100–200 °C | 3–5× | EBM has lower residual stress |
| **Surface roughness Ra** | 20–50 µm | 10–30 µm | ~2× | EBM needs more post-machining |
| **Build time (typical Ti part)** | ~1× | ~1.5–2× | 0.5–0.7 | EBM is faster |
| **Energy efficiency** | 1–5 % | 10–20 % | 0.1–0.5 | EBM is *less* efficient (vacuum pumping) |
| **Cost per kg part (Ti-6Al-4V)** | $300–600 | $400–800 | ~0.75 | EBM is cheaper per kg |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — EBM System Architecture

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the functional architecture of an EBM machine, showing the energy chain from filament to powder bed and the process-control feedback loops. This is the *Block-Level Functional Architecture* prescribed in the KTU-PREMIER protocol (since EBM is best represented as a system rather than a physical part drawing).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ACPS[("AC Power Supply")] --> HVU[("High Voltage Unit 60 kV")]
    HVU --> WC[("Wehnelt Cup & Anode")]
    HVU --> FL[("Filament Cathode Tungsten")]
    FL -- "Thermionic Emission" --> EBS[("Electron Beam Stream")]
    WC -- "Acceleration" --> EBS
    EBS -- "Magnetic Focusing" --> ML[("Electromagnetic Lenses")]
    ML -- "Beam Shaping" --> FSP[("Focused Spot 200 micrometre")]
    EBS -- "Magnetic Deflection" --> DC[("Deflection Coils XY Scan")]
    DC -- "Steered Path" --> FSP
    SC[("Scan Controller CAD Slice Data")] --> DC
    TC[("Temperature Controller 700 C")] --> BP[("Build Plate")]
    FSP -- "Beam Impact" --> PB[("Powder Bed Ti-6Al-4V")]
    BP -- "Pre-heat" --> PB
    PB -- "Melt Pool Formation" --> SL[("Solidified Layer 50 to 200 micrometre")]
    RB[("Powder Hopper & Raking Blade")] -- "Recoat" --> PB
    BC[("Vacuum Chamber 10 to the power minus 4 mbar")] --> PB
    HE[("Helium Partial Pressure Cooling")] --> BC
    PU[("Vacuum Pump System Turbomolecular + Rotary")] --> BC
    SL -- "Layer Complete" --> LSD[("Lower Build Platform by Delta t")]
    LSD --> RB
    PU -. "Pressure Feedback" .-> BC
    SC -. "Position Feedback" .-> DC
    TC -. "Temp Feedback" .-> BP
```

### Reading the diagram:

- **Solid arrows** indicate the **forward flow** of energy, material, or control signal.
- **Dashed (dotted) arrows** indicate **feedback / sensor signals** for closed-loop control.
- **Rounded rectangles (`()`)** denote physical hardware subsystems.
- **Top-to-bottom flow** represents the energy cascade: electrical power → high voltage → electron beam → focused spot → melt pool → solidified layer.
- The **right-side feedback loop** (Scan Controller → Deflection Coils) closes the part-geometry control.
- The **bottom loop** (Solidified Layer → Lower Build Platform → Raking Blade → Powder Bed) is the *layer-by-layer deposition cycle*.

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — EBM Process States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> VacuumPumpDown
    VacuumPumpDown --> PreheatBuildPlate : Pressure below 10 to the power minus 4 mbar
    PreheatBuildPlate --> SpreadPowderLayer : Plate at 700 C
    SpreadPowderLayer --> PreheatPowder : Beam low power scan
    PreheatPowder --> MeltContour : Beam at full power
    MeltContour --> MeltHatch : Outer boundary done
    MeltHatch --> LayerComplete : Inner fill done
    LayerComplete --> LowerPlatform : Drop by t
    LowerPlatform --> SpreadPowderLayer : Last layer not yet
    LayerComplete --> CoolAndRemove : Last layer done
    CoolAndRemove --> PowderRemoval : Vacuum released
    PowderRemoval --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiner's note:** A *common mistake* students make is omitting the **preheat powder** state. Unlike SLM, EBM performs a **secondary preheat** of the powder layer (using a defocused, low-power beam) *before* the actual melt scan. This is called "**multi-spot melting's first pass**" and it is essential to sinter/sinter-partially the powder so that **smoke and electrostatic repulsion** (called the "**smoke effect**" — caused by charging of powder particles) do not disrupt the beam. **Always include this step in your EBM process descriptions.**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks = 6 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions are the **first 30 minutes** of the KTU End Semester Exam (ESE). They test **definitions, distinctions, and direct recall**.

---

### Question 1. Define Electron Beam Melting (EBM). List any three advantages of EBM over Selective Laser Melting (SLM).** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Electron Beam Melting is a powder-bed fusion additive manufacturing process in which a high-energy electron beam (accelerated through a 60 kV potential) is used to selectively melt metallic powder, layer by layer, in a high-vacuum environment to build a fully dense part.

**Any three advantages (3 × 1/2 mark = 1.5 marks — 0.5 mark each):**
1. **Faster build speed** — Beam power of 1–3 kW (vs. 200–500 W for SLM) yields 3–5× faster builds.
2. **Lower residual stress** — Build plate pre-heated to 700 °C acts like a stress-relief furnace in-situ.
3. **No atmospheric contamination** — Vacuum environment prevents oxygen/nitrogen pickup in reactive metals like titanium.
4. **Cheaper per kg** — Lower energy cost and faster throughput.
5. **Wider material compatibility** — Works with Ti, Co-Cr, Ni-superalloys, and even some ceramics and refractory metals.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write "**EBM uses a laser**" — this is WRONG. EBM uses an **electron beam**, not a laser. **Half a mark will be cut** if you confuse the two processes.

---

### Question 2. Explain the role of vacuum in the EBM process.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The vacuum in the EBM chamber serves **three critical functions** (1 mark each):

1. **Prevents electron scattering:** Electrons in the 30–60 keV range have a mean free path of only a few centimetres in air at atmospheric pressure. Without vacuum, the beam would scatter, lose focus, and fail to deliver a tight 200 µm spot. A vacuum of 10⁻⁴ mbar extends the mean free path to tens of metres.

2. **Prevents atmospheric contamination:** Reactive metals like titanium have an extreme affinity for oxygen. Even a few ppm of O₂ would form TiO₂ inclusions, embrittling the part. Vacuum (with only a partial He backfill for thermal conduction) keeps oxygen and nitrogen below 1 ppm.

3. **Prevents the "smoke effect":** In vacuum, charged powder particles do not bleed off to ground. Without controlled gas, charged fines would levitate and scatter the beam. A small He partial pressure (~10⁻² mbar) provides the conductive medium to dissipate charge — without it, the bed becomes a chaotic plasma.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (1 × 14 marks, with internal choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Part B questions form the **core 90 minutes** of the KTU ESE. The structure here follows the standard KTU pattern: **two sub-parts (a) and (b), each 7 marks**. You will be given an internal choice (either **OR** question).

---

### Choice A: 14-Mark Question

#### Question A. (a) Explain the working principle of Electron Beam Melting (EBM) with a neat block diagram. List the process parameters of EBM.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023] (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (5 marks):**

EBM is a powder bed fusion process where a focused beam of high-energy electrons is used to melt metallic powder. The process works as follows:

1. The **build chamber is evacuated** to 10⁻⁴ mbar, then back-filled with a small He pressure (~10⁻² mbar).
2. A **tungsten filament** is resistively heated to 2500 °C, emitting electrons by thermionic emission.
3. The emitted electrons are **accelerated by a 60 kV potential** difference toward the powder bed.
4. **Electromagnetic lenses focus** the beam to a 100–400 µm spot.
5. **Electromagnetic deflection coils** scan the focused spot across the powder bed in the pattern of the current CAD slice.
6. The beam delivers enormous **power density (~10¹⁰ W/m²)** to the powder, melting it locally to form a melt pool.
7. After a layer is complete, the **build platform descends** by 50–200 µm, a **raking blade spreads a fresh powder layer**, and the cycle repeats.
8. The **build plate is maintained at ~700 °C** throughout the process to minimize residual stress.
9. After the part is complete, the chamber is **cooled under vacuum** (to prevent oxidation) for 5–10 hours before powder removal.

**Block diagram:** (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 above — student should draw equivalent on paper.) (2 marks)

**Process parameters (2 marks — list any four):**
- Beam power ($P$)
- Accelerating voltage ($V$)
- Beam current ($I$)
- Scan velocity ($v$)
- Hatch spacing ($h$)
- Layer thickness ($t$)
- Beam spot diameter ($d$)
- Chamber vacuum level
- Build plate temperature

---

#### Question A. (b) Calculate the volumetric energy density of an EBM process for Ti-6Al-4V with the following parameters. Comment on whether the value is within the acceptable range for fully dense parts.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023] (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Beam power $P = 2.4$ kW $= 2400$ W
- Scan velocity $v = 4000$ mm/s
- Hatch spacing $h = 0.15$ mm
- Layer thickness $t = 0.07$ mm

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the volumetric energy density formula (1 mark):**

$$E_{v} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
v \cdot h \cdot t &= 4000 \times 0.15 \times 0.07 \\
&= 4000 \times 0.0105 \\
&= 42.0 \; \text{mm}^{2}/\text{s} \cdot \text{mm} \;?\\
\end{aligned}$$

Units check: $v$ is mm/s, $h$ and $t$ are mm. So $v \cdot h \cdot t$ has units $\text{mm}^{3}/\text{s}$. Numerically:

$$v \cdot h \cdot t = 4000 \times 0.15 \times 0.07 = 42.0 \; \text{mm}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Divide power by denominator (1 mark):**

$$E_{v} = \dfrac{2400 \; \text{W}}{42.0 \; \text{mm}^{3}/\text{s}} = 57.14 \; \text{J/mm}^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Verification & comment (4 marks):**

Wait — let me recompute carefully:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{v} &= \dfrac{2400 \text{ J/s}}{42.0 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{s}} \\
&= 57.14 \text{ J/mm}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** The computed value of **57.14 J/mm³ is BELOW the typical 100 J/mm³ threshold** for full densification of Ti-6Al-4V. This indicates the part is likely to have **lack-of-fusion porosity** (i.e., voids where powder particles were not fully melted into the layer below). 

**To bring $E_v$ into the acceptable range (100 ≤ E_v ≤ 500 J/mm³)**, the operator can either:
- **Reduce scan velocity** to ~2000 mm/s (gives $E_v$ = 114 J/mm³ — OK),
- **Reduce hatch spacing** to ~0.085 mm (gives $E_v$ = 100 J/mm³ — OK),
- **Reduce layer thickness** to ~0.04 mm (gives $E_v$ = 100 J/mm³ — OK), or
- **Increase beam power** to ~4.2 kW (gives $E_v$ = 100 J/mm³ — OK).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (cut to ZERO):** Many students will write the formula as $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ (forgetting $t$). This is the *areal* energy density, NOT volumetric. You will lose **2 of the 7 marks** for this unit-mixing error. **Always include all three parameters: $v$, $h$, AND $t$.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Second pitfall:** Many students compute but forget to **comment** on whether the value is acceptable. KTU boards deduct **2 marks** for "no engineering interpretation" of a numerical answer. The "**comment on acceptable range**" line in the question is a direct instruction — *do not ignore it*.

---

### Choice B: 14-Mark Alternative

#### Question B. (a) Describe the electron beam generation, focusing, and deflection system in an EBM machine with a neat sketch.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Electron Generation (2 marks):**
A **tungsten filament** (cathode) is heated to 2400–2800 °C by passing a current through it. At this temperature, electrons acquire thermal energy exceeding the work function of tungsten (~4.5 eV) and escape the surface — a phenomenon called **thermionic emission**. The emission current density is given by the **Richardson-Dushman equation:**

$$J \;=\; A_{R} T^{2} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\phi}{k_{B} T}\right)$$

The emitted electrons form a cloud around the filament. A **Wehnelt cup** (a negatively-biased control grid surrounding the filament) shapes and stabilizes this electron cloud.

**2. Acceleration (1 mark):**
A high-voltage potential of **30–60 kV** is applied between the cathode (filament) and the anode (which has a central hole). Electrons are accelerated through this potential, gaining kinetic energy $E = eV$ (e.g., 60 keV for 60 kV accelerating voltage). They exit through the anode hole as a high-velocity, well-collimated beam.

**3. Focusing (2 marks):**
**Electromagnetic lenses** — coils carrying DC current — produce a magnetic field that focuses the diverging electron beam to a tight focal spot on the powder bed. The focal spot diameter is typically 100–400 µm. Magnetic focusing is used (not optical lenses) because electrons are charged particles and respond to Lorentz forces — a unique advantage of EBM over laser-based systems.

**4. Deflection (2 marks):**
**Electromagnetic deflection coils** (one for X-axis, one for Y-axis) steer the focused beam to scan the powder bed in the prescribed pattern. The coils are driven by high-bandwidth signals from the **scan controller** (which reads the sliced CAD data of the part). Scan velocities up to 10,000 mm/s are achievable, and the beam can be turned on/off at MHz rates. The deflection system essentially functions as a "**vector-plotter**" that the scan controller commands in real time.

**Sketch:** (Student should draw the filament, Wehnelt cup, anode, magnetic lens, deflection coils, and powder bed, with the beam path traced through.) Students should label all components and the vacuum chamber outline.

---

#### Question B. (b) An EBM machine processes Ti-6Al-4V at a beam power of 1.5 kW with a beam spot diameter of 0.25 mm. If the scan velocity is 800 mm/s, calculate (i) the power density at the spot, and (ii) the linear energy density. Comment on the result.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Beam power $P = 1.5$ kW $= 1500$ W
- Spot diameter $d = 0.25$ mm $= 0.25 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Scan velocity $v = 800$ mm/s

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Power Density (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Spot area:**

$$A_{spot} = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.25 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{spot} &= \dfrac{3.1416 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-8}}{4} \\
&= \dfrac{1.9635 \times 10^{-7}}{4} \\
&= 4.909 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Power density formula:**

$$P_{d} = \dfrac{P}{A_{spot}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{d} &= \dfrac{1500 \text{ W}}{4.909 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{2}} \\
&\approx 3.056 \times 10^{10} \text{ W/m}^{2} \\
&\approx 30.6 \text{ GW/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (ii) — Linear Energy Density (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Formula:**

$$E_{l} = \dfrac{P}{v}$$

**Step 2 — Substitution:**

$$E_{l} = \dfrac{1500 \text{ W}}{800 \text{ mm/s}} = 1.875 \; \text{J/mm}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Comment:**

A linear energy density of 1.875 J/mm is on the **lower end** of typical EBM values (which range from 1 to 5 J/mm for Ti-6Al-4V). This suggests the part is being processed at **moderately high speed**, with potential for slightly under-melted conditions. To increase $E_l$ (and improve melt-pool stability), the operator could either:
- Increase the beam power (e.g., from 1.5 kW to 2.0 kW, giving $E_l$ = 2.5 J/mm), or
- Reduce the scan velocity (e.g., from 800 mm/s to 600 mm/s, giving $E_l$ = 2.5 J/mm).

The power density of 30.6 GW/m² is consistent with industrial EBM operating regimes (10–100 GW/m²).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall (cut 1 mark):** Students commonly compute the **spot area** as $\pi r^2$ using $r = d$ instead of $r = d/2$. This will **double** the area and **halve** the power density. Always confirm $r = d/2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall (cut 1 mark):** Forgetting to convert mm to m in the spot diameter, leading to a power density off by a factor of 10⁶. **Always work in SI units** (metres, watts) when computing power density.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following checklist is the **last thing** you should read before walking into the KTU ESE. It covers **≥90% of marks** for any EBM question.

### Core Concepts (Definition Stage)
- [ ] **EBM = Powder Bed Fusion (PBF-EB)** using accelerated electrons (30–60 kV) in vacuum (~10⁻⁴ mbar).
- [ ] Commercialized by **Arcam AB (Sweden)**, now part of **GE Additive**.
- [ ] Process standards under **ASTM F2792** as Category 2 PBF.
- [ ] EBM ≠ Electron Beam Welding (EBW). EBM is layer-by-layer powder fusion.

### Process Sequence (Operating Principle Stage)
- [ ] **Vacuum pump-down** → **He backfill** → **Build plate preheat (700 °C)** → **Powder spread** → **Powder preheat (low-power defocused beam)** → **Contour melt** → **Hatch melt** → **Layer complete** → **Lower platform** → repeat.
- [ ] **Build plate preheat = 700 °C** for Ti-6Al-4V is the *defining* EBM signature.
- [ ] Two-stage beam per layer: **preheat pass** (sinter powder) + **melt pass** (full density).

### Hardware Subsystems (System Stage)
- [ ] **Filament (cathode):** Tungsten, 2500–2800 °C, thermionic emission.
- [ ] **Wehnelt cup:** control grid, shapes electron beam.
- [ ] **Anode:** accelerated electrons to 30–60 keV.
- [ ] **Electromagnetic lenses:** focus beam to 100–400 µm spot.
- [ ] **Deflection coils:** X-Y scan at up to 10,000 mm/s.
- [ ] **Vacuum chamber:** 10⁻⁴ mbar; He partial pressure for charge dissipation.
- [ ] **Raking blade / roller:** spreads fresh powder.
- [ ] **Powder hoppers:** Ti-6Al-4V typically 45–105 µm particle size.

### Critical Formulas (Numerical Stage)
- [ ] **Beam Power:** $P = V \cdot I$ (V in volts, I in amps → W)
- [ ] **Power Density:** $P_d = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}$ (W/m²)
- [ ] **Volumetric Energy Density:** $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ (J/mm³)
- [ ] **Linear Energy Density:** $E_l = \dfrac{P}{v}$ (J/mm)
- [ ] **Areal Energy Density:** $E_a = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ (J/mm²)
- [ ] **Residence Time:** $t_{res} = d/v$ (s)
- [ ] **Acceptable Ti-6Al-4V range:** $100 \le E_v \le 500$ J/mm³

### EBM vs SLM Comparison (Comparative Stage — frequent in KTU)
- [ ] EBM = **faster**, **lower stress**, **cheaper per kg**, **worse surface finish**, **faster but rougher**, **vacuum purity**.
- [ ] SLM = **slower**, **higher stress**, **finer features**, **better surface finish**, **inert gas purity**, **lower power**.
- [ ] EBM scan speed: **1000–10,000 mm/s**; SLM: **100–1500 mm/s**.
- [ ] EBM power: **1–3 kW**; SLM: **200–500 W**.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] ❌ Don't confuse EBM (electron beam) with SLM (laser).
- [ ] ❌ Don't omit $t$ (layer thickness) in $E_v$ formula.
- [ ] ❌ Don't forget unit conversion (mm vs m) in $P_d$.
- [ ] ❌ Don't forget to **comment on whether numerical results are in acceptable range**.
- [ ] ❌ Don't claim EBM uses a laser.
- [ ] ❌ Don't claim SLM uses a vacuum (it uses inert gas, not vacuum).
- [ ] ❌ Don't confuse "smoke effect" (powder charging) — it requires He backfill, not pure vacuum.
- [ ] ❌ Don't forget the **preheat pass** before the melt pass in EBM.

### High-Yield Memory Hooks
- [ ] **Mnemonic "P-vht"** for $E_v = P/(v \cdot h \cdot t)$: "**P**ower divided by **v**elocity, **h**atch, **t**hickness."
- [ ] **Mnemonic "60-700-10⁻⁴"** for EBM signature: "**60 kV**, **700 °C** plate, **10⁻⁴ mbar** vacuum."
- [ ] **Mnemonic "T-W-L-A"** for beam chain: "**T**ungsten filament → **W**ehnelt → **L**enses → **A**node-accelerated."

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Advice for KTU 2024:** EBM questions in the KTU board exam almost always include **one numerical** (compute $P_d$, $E_v$, or $E_l$) and **one descriptive** (process, hardware, comparison with SLM). Master the formulas in the cheat sheet and the comparison table — these two together guarantee ≥10 of the 14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stereolithography (SLA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET525 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING AND 3D PRINTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stereolithography (SLA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stereolithography (SLA)** is a **vat photopolymerization** based additive manufacturing process patented by **Charles W. (Chuck) Hull in 1984** (US Patent 4,575,330), in which a focused **ultraviolet (UV) laser beam** is used to selectively cure (harden) a liquid **photopolymer resin** in a layer-by-layer fashion to build a solid three-dimensional object. It is classified under **ISO/ASTM 52900** as the *Vat Photopolymerization* category and is regarded as the **first commercially successful rapid prototyping technology**.

The term originates from Greek roots: *stereo* (solid) + *lithos* (stone) + *graphein* (to write) — literally meaning **"writing solid objects in stone"**, signifying the controlled solidification of matter point-by-point to form a solid 3D part.

### 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Magical Honeycomb Pond":**
> Imagine a shallow pond filled with thick, transparent honey that hardens permanently the moment a focused flashlight beam touches it. To build a toy boat, you do not carve it from a block; instead, you carefully trace the boat's outline with your flashlight at the surface, and only the honey touched by the light solidifies. Once the first outline is drawn, you dip the platform a tiny bit lower (e.g., 0.1 mm) so a fresh layer of liquid honey flows over the solidified one, and you trace the next cross-section. After hundreds of such dips, your toy boat is fully built — **bottom to top, one micro-layer at a time**. This is *exactly* what an SLA printer does: the **honey is photopolymer resin**, the **flashlight is a UV laser**, and the **platform is the build stage**.

### 1.3 Core Defining Characteristics

| Parameter | Specification / Range |
|---|---|
| Process Family | Vat Photopolymerization (ISO/ASTM 52900) |
| Inventor | Charles W. Hull (3D Systems Corp., 1984) |
| Energy Source | UV Laser (He-Cd 325 nm) or UV-LED / DLP projector |
| Material State | Liquid photopolymer resin (acrylic or epoxy based) |
| Typical Layer Thickness | **0.025 mm – 0.3 mm** |
| Achievable Tolerance | ± 0.1 % (min ± 0.1 mm) |
| Surface Finish | Best in AM (Ra ≈ 0.5 – 5 µm after post-cure) |
| Build Speed | **Slow** compared to FDM/FFF |
| Support Structures | Mandatory (scaffold-like for overhangs) |

### 1.4 Historical Significance

> [!NOTE]
> SLA is the **birthplace of the entire 3D printing industry.** Chuck Hull's original 1984 SLA-1 machine took nearly 70 hours to build a simple part. The technology evolved from **laser SLA** → **DLP (Digital Light Processing)** → **CDLP (Continuous Digital Light Processing)** → **mSLA (masked SLA / LCD-based)**. The single most important advancement was the substitution of the slow serial laser scanner with a **digital micromirror device (DMD)**, enabling one-shot curing of an entire layer.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Photopolymerization Chemistry — "Why does liquid resin turn solid?"

The SLA process rests on **photo-initiated free-radical polymerization**. This is a chain reaction triggered by light:

**Step 1 — Photoexcitation:** A UV photon strikes a **photoinitiator molecule** (e.g., BAPO, TPO, Irgacure 819) dissolved in the resin. The photon raises the molecule to an excited triplet state.

**Step 2 — Bond Cleavage (α-cleavage):** The excited photoinitiator splits into **two free radicals** (highly reactive unpaired-electron species).

**Step 3 — Chain Initiation:** A free radical attacks the **C=C double bond** of a (meth)acrylate monomer, opening it and creating a new radical site on the monomer.

**Step 4 — Chain Propagation:** The new radical attacks another monomer, and this repeats thousands of times, creating a long crosslinked polymer chain. The liquid is converted into a rigid, three-dimensional **crosslinked network**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering "Why":** Because polymerization only occurs above a threshold UV energy, partially exposed regions remain liquid and act as **natural support material** — this enables extremely delicate geometries (lattice, micro-channels) that are difficult to achieve in FDM.

### 2.2 The Two Sub-Classes of SLA

| Feature | **Vector-Based Laser SLA (Traditional)** | **Mask-Based VPP (DLP / mSLA / CDLP)** |
|---|---|---|
| Light Source | Galvo-scanned UV laser (He-Cd 325 nm, UV-LED 405 nm) | DMD chip (DLP) or LCD panel (mSLA) |
| Curing Strategy | Vector scanning — point-by-point, line-by-line | Area projection — entire layer cured at once |
| Build Time per Layer | **Depends on area** (slower for large parts) | **Independent of area** (faster for large parts) |
| Resolution Driver | Laser spot diameter (~75 – 250 µm) | Pixel pitch (~35 – 75 µm) |
| Pixel-level X-Y Accuracy | Continuous (analog) | Discrete (digital, voxelated) |
| Throughput | Lower | Higher (especially CDLP) |

### 2.3 The Three Generations of Laser-Scanning SLA

- **Top-Down (Original Hull Design):** Laser traces on the resin surface; platform moves **down** into the vat. Uses smaller resin volume but requires a recoating blade.
- **Bottom-Up (Inverted / Contemporary):** Laser passes through a transparent window at the vat bottom; platform rises. **Now industry standard** because of oxygen-permeable fluoropolymer (Teflon AF) window that inhibits adhesion.
- **Free-Surface (Rare):** Laser cures a meniscus suspended between two plates.

### 2.4 The Five Sub-Systems of an SLA Machine

1. **Vat / Resin Tank:** Holds the liquid photopolymer. Often uses an oxygen-permeable membrane at the bottom to create a **"dead zone"** of uncured resin (essential for bottom-up printers).
2. **Build Platform / Elevator:** A precision Z-stage (resolution ~1 µm) that lifts the part after each layer.
3. **Optical Engine:** UV laser + beam-steering galvanometer mirrors + F-theta focusing lens.
4. **Resin Recoater:** Wiper, blade, or rotating roller that spreads a fresh liquid film.
5. **Control Electronics & Software:** Slicer + path generator (hatch + contour) + exposure calculator.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the Working Curve and Cure Width equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical problem on SLA.**

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Symbol Legend | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{d} = D_{p} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_{0}}{E_{c}}\right)$ | $C_d$ = cure depth (µm); $D_p$ = penetration depth (µm); $E_0$ = incident energy (mJ/cm²); $E_c$ = critical energy (mJ/cm²) | Predicts how deep a single UV exposure polymerizes. **Foundation of SLA process planning.** |
| 2 | $E_{0} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot w \cdot t}$ | $P$ = laser power (mW); $v$ = scan speed (cm/s); $w$ = beam waist (cm); $t$ = dwell time (s) | Computes the energy actually delivered to a unit area of resin. |
| 3 | $W_{c} = W_{0} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{d}}{D_{p}}}$ | $W_c$ = cured line width; $W_0$ = laser spot diameter (1/e²) | Estimates the **breadth** of a single laser track — used for hatch-spacing decisions. |
| 4 | $D_{p} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$ | $D_p$ = penetration depth; $\alpha$ = resin absorption coefficient (mm⁻¹) | Material property; lower $\alpha$ ⇒ deeper curing, less control. |
| 5 | $T_{total} = n \cdot (t_{cure} + t_{recoat}) + t_{post}$ | $n$ = number of layers; $t_{cure}$ = cure time; $t_{recoat}$ = recoat time; $t_{post}$ = post-cure | Total build-time estimation — a frequent KTU question. |
| 6 | $n = \dfrac{H_{part}}{L_{layer}}$ | $H_{part}$ = part height; $L_{layer}$ = layer thickness | Direct layer count from CAD height. |
| 7 | $v_{scan} = \dfrac{P}{E_{0} \cdot w \cdot t}$ | Rearranged Eq. 2 | Used to find optimum laser speed for a chosen $E_0$. |
| 8 | $H_{hatch} \le 0.7 \cdot W_{c}$ | $H_{hatch}$ = hatch spacing | Avoids inter-track voids (weld-line porosity). |
| 9 | $\text{Vol}_{resin} = A_{layer} \cdot n \cdot (1 + \eta)$ | $\eta$ = over-pour factor (~0.05 – 0.20) | Resin consumption planning (costing). |
| 10 | $\text{Acc}_{XY} = f(\text{spot}, \text{galvo error})$ | — | Quantifies the print's in-plane precision. |

> **Mandatory units reminder for KTU papers:** $E_0$ and $E_c$ are in **mJ/cm²**; $D_p$ and $C_d$ in **µm or mm**; $P$ in **mW**; $v$ in **cm/s**; $w$ in **cm**; $t$ in **s**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the SLA Working Curve Equation

The single most important analytical result in SLA is the **working curve** relating cure depth $C_d$ to exposure energy $E_0$. It is derived from **Beer–Lambert's Law of light attenuation** combined with a **threshold polymerization condition**.

**Starting premise — Beer–Lambert Law (Beer, 1852):**
When a UV beam of intensity $I_0$ enters an absorbing liquid medium, the intensity at depth $z$ is:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha z}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **absorption coefficient** of the resin at the laser's wavelength. This is the differential form integrated over the optical path.

**Step 1 — Convert intensity to energy fluence:**
Energy fluence at depth $z$ is $E(z) = E_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha z}$, where $E_0$ is the surface fluence.

**Step 2 — Threshold cure condition:**
Polymerization only occurs where the local fluence exceeds a **critical value** $E_c$, the minimum energy required to drive the chain reaction past gelation. The cure depth $C_d$ is therefore the depth at which $E(z) = E_c$:

$$E_c = E_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha C_{d}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $C_d$:**
Rearrange the exponential equation to isolate $C_d$:

$$e^{-\alpha C_{d}} = \dfrac{E_{c}}{E_{0}}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\alpha C_{d} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_{c}}{E_{0}}\right)$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$:

$$\alpha C_{d} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_{0}}{E_{c}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the definition of penetration depth:**
The **penetration depth** is defined as the depth at which intensity falls to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its surface value. From Beer–Lambert: $D_p = 1/\alpha$, so $\alpha = 1/D_p$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{\,C_{d} = D_{p} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_{0}}{E_{c}}\right)\,}$$

This is the canonical **Jacobs working curve** (P. F. Jacobs, 1992, Rapid Prototyping & Manufacturing — a widely-cited industry formulation; the equation itself, however, is mathematically standard Beer–Lambert and is reproducible from first principles). It is **linear in $E_0$ on a log scale** — the slope is $D_p$, the intercept depends on $E_c$.

### 3.2 Worked KTU-Style Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A photopolymer resin has penetration depth $D_p = 0.20$ mm and critical energy $E_c = 9$ mJ/cm². The laser delivers surface fluence $E_0 = 81$ mJ/cm². **(a)** Calculate the cure depth. **(b)** If a 25 mm tall part is built with $L_{layer} = 0.1$ mm, find the number of layers. **(c)** Compute the total cure time if each layer takes 4 s and recoating takes 1.5 s per layer.

**Part (a) — Cure Depth:**

$$C_{d} = D_{p} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_{0}}{E_{c}}\right) = 0.20 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{81}{9}\right)$$

$$C_{d} = 0.20 \cdot \ln(9) = 0.20 \cdot 2.1972$$

$$\boxed{\,C_{d} = 0.4394 \text{ mm}\,}$$

**Part (b) — Number of Layers:**

$$n = \dfrac{H_{part}}{L_{layer}} = \dfrac{25 \text{ mm}}{0.1 \text{ mm}} = 250 \text{ layers}$$

**Part (c) — Total Build Time (excluding post-cure):**

$$T_{total} = n \cdot (t_{cure} + t_{recoat}) = 250 \cdot (4 + 1.5)$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{total} = 250 \cdot 5.5 = 1375 \text{ s} \approx 22.9 \text{ min}\,}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Cure Width $W_c$

A focused Gaussian laser beam has an intensity profile $I(r) = I_0 \exp(-2r^2/w_0^2)$ where $w_0$ is the **beam radius** at $1/e^2$. The cure width $W_c$ is defined as the lateral extent where local energy equals the threshold $E_c$. Combining the radial Gaussian decay with the Beer–Lambert axial attenuation and integrating over depth $0 \le z \le C_d$ yields the well-known result:

$$W_{c} = W_{0} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{d}}{D_{p}}}$$

where $W_0 = 2 w_0$ is the spot diameter. **Engineering insight:** If $C_d = D_p$ (i.e., the cure depth equals one penetration depth), then $W_c = W_0$ — the cured line is exactly as wide as the laser spot. Deeper curing ⇒ wider cured lines ⇒ **poorer XY resolution**. This is why KTU questions often ask: *Why can't you simply increase $E_0$ to cure thicker layers?* — because resolution degrades.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Energy, Depth, and Time Trade-offs

| Decision Lever | Effect on $C_d$ | Effect on $W_c$ | Effect on $T_{build}$ | Effect on $R_a$ (roughness) |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| ↑ Laser Power $P$ (⇒ ↑ $E_0$) | **↑ Deeper** | **↑ Wider** | ↓ Faster cure per layer | ↓ Smoother (less stair-step) |
| ↑ Scan Speed $v$ (⇒ ↓ $E_0$) | **↓ Shallower** | **↓ Narrower** | ↑ More time | ↑ Rougher |
| ↑ Layer Thickness $L$ | unchanged | unchanged | **↓↓ Much faster** (fewer layers) | ↑ Rougher (staircase) |
| ↑ $D_p$ (lower $\alpha$ resin) | **↑ Deeper** | **↑ Wider** | ↓ Faster | ↓ Smoother |
| ↑ $E_c$ (harder-to-cure resin) | ↓ Shallower | ↓ Narrower | ↑ Slower | unchanged |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Trivia:** When asked *“state the limitations of SLA,”* examiners expect you to mention the **dead zone**, the **photoinitiator toxicity**, the **post-cure shrinkage**, and the **oxygen inhibition** problem — all consequences of the same Beer–Lambert framework.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bottom-Up SLA Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model STL File] --> B[Slicer Software]
    B --> C[Slice to 2D Layers]
    C --> D[Path Generator: Hatch + Contour]
    D --> E[Build Parameter Calculator: E0, v, W0]
    E --> F[UV Laser Source 405 nm or 325 nm]
    F --> G[Beam Expander and Shaper]
    G --> H[Galvanometer X Mirror]
    H --> I[Galvanometer Y Mirror]
    I --> J[F Theta Focusing Lens]
    J --> K[Transparent Window with Dead Zone]
    K --> L[Liquid Photopolymer Resin Vat]
    L --> M[Oxygen Permeable Membrane: Fluoropolymer]
    M --> N[Build Platform Z Stage]
    N --> O[Recoater Wiper or Roller]
    O --> P[Part Curing Process]
    P --> Q[Is Part Complete Decision]
    Q -->|No| N
    Q -->|Yes| R[Remove Part from Platform]
    R --> S[Post Cure: UV Oven 60 to 80 C]
    S --> T[Support Removal and Finishing]
    T --> U[Final 3D Printed Part]
```

**Reading the architecture:**
- The **left column** (A → E) is the *digital pre-processing chain*.
- The **middle column** (F → N) is the *optical + mechanical cure chain*.
- The **right column** (O → U) is the *post-processing chain*.
- The **loop** back from Q to N represents the **layer-by-layer iterative build** — typically 100 to 3000 cycles per part.

### 4.2 Process Flow of a Single Layer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Layer N Count] --> A2[Lower Platform by L_layer]
    A2 --> A3[Recoat: Spread Fresh Resin]
    A3 --> A4[Galvo Scan Hatch Lines at Speed v]
    A4 --> A5[Galvo Scan Contour Outline]
    A5 --> A6[UV Exposure Completes Free Radical Polymerization]
    A6 --> A7[Increment N to N+1]
    A7 --> A8{N Equal n total}
    A8 -->|No| A1
    A8 -->|Yes| A9[Layer Build Complete]
```

### 4.3 SLA Curing Reaction Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[UV Photon h nu] --> PI[Photoinitiator Molecule in Excited State]
    PI --> R1[Free Radical R dot]
    R1 --> M1[Monomer CH2 C double bond CH R]
    M1 --> R2[Chain Radical R CH2 CH dot R]
    R2 --> M2[Next Monomer]
    M2 --> R3[Growing Polymer Chain]
    R3 --> X[Crosslinked 3D Network Solid Part]
    X --> OBS[Observations: Exothermic, Volume Shrinkage 3 to 5 percent]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Map — SLA vs DLP vs mSLA vs 2PP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SLA Family] --> B[Vector Laser]
    A --> C[Area Projection]
    B --> B1[Top Down: Hull Original]
    B --> B2[Bottom Up: Modern Industrial]
    C --> C1[DLP: Digital Micromirror Device]
    C --> C2[mSLA: LCD Mask]
    C --> C3[CDLP: Continuous DLP]
    A --> D[Sub Micron: Two Photon Polymerization 2PP]
    D --> D1[Used for Nanoscale Optics and Microfluidics]
```

> **Diagram interpretation tip:** Examiners often show a labeled SLA cross-section and ask you to identify components. The four items **always** in the answer are: (1) UV laser, (2) galvanometer mirrors, (3) build platform, and (4) resin vat. Adding (5) recoater and (6) F-theta lens earns a full mark.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]**
> **Q1.** Define **Stereolithography (SLA)**. Name the inventor, the patent year, and the ISO/ASTM category in which it is classified.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Stereolithography (SLA) is a **vat photopolymerization** based additive manufacturing process in which a focused **ultraviolet (UV) laser beam** selectively cures a liquid photopolymer resin layer by layer to build a solid 3D object. **(1 mark)**
> It was invented by **Charles W. (Chuck) Hull** in **1984** (US Patent 4,575,330) and commercialized by **3D Systems Corporation**. **(1 mark)**
> It is classified under **ISO/ASTM 52900 (52900:2015)** in the *Vat Photopolymerization* family. **(1 mark)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between **laser-based SLA** and **DLP (Digital Light Processing)**. Mention the curing strategy and resolution driver for each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Laser SLA** uses a **galvanometer-scanned UV laser** to cure one spot at a time; its resolution is governed by the **laser spot diameter (~75 – 250 µm)**. **(1.5 marks)**
> - **DLP** uses a **digital micromirror device (DMD)** to project an entire 2D mask of the layer, curing it in one shot; its resolution is governed by the **DMD pixel pitch (~5 – 50 µm)**. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B]** — *Module 3 Reference Question*
>
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of Stereolithography (SLA)**. Differentiate between **top-down** and **bottom-up** configurations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **working curve equation** $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0/E_c)$ starting from **Beer–Lambert's law**. A resin has $D_p = 0.15$ mm and $E_c = 10$ mJ/cm². Compute the cure depth when the laser delivers $E_0 = 50$ mJ/cm². **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle and Configurations (7 marks):**
The SLA process is governed by **photo-initiated free-radical polymerization**. A UV laser is steered by two galvanometer mirrors to trace the 2D cross-section of the part on the surface of (or through the bottom of) a vat of liquid photopolymer resin. Where the UV energy exceeds the critical threshold $E_c$, the resin solidifies into a hard, crosslinked polymer. After one layer cures, the platform moves by the layer thickness $L$, a recoater spreads a fresh film, and the next layer is traced. The process iterates 100 – 3000 times until the full 3D part is built. **(3 marks)**
- **Top-Down (Original Hull design):** The laser cures the **top surface** of the resin; the platform is **lowered** into the vat after each layer. Pros: smaller resin volume; Cons: requires a recoating blade, oxygen inhibition on the open surface. **(2 marks)**
- **Bottom-Up (Inverted / Contemporary):** The laser passes **upward through a transparent window** at the bottom of the vat; the platform **rises** after each layer. Pros: oxygen-permeable fluoropolymer membrane creates a "dead zone" of uncured resin, eliminating peel forces; better surface finish. Cons: requires a transparent, oxygen-permeable window. **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):**
**Derivation (5 marks)** — As shown in §3.1 of these notes:
Starting from Beer–Lambert: $I(z) = I_0 e^{-\alpha z}$, hence $E(z) = E_0 e^{-\alpha z}$. At the cure boundary, $E(z = C_d) = E_c$:
$$E_c = E_0 e^{-\alpha C_d} \implies e^{-\alpha C_d} = E_c/E_0$$
$$-\alpha C_d = \ln(E_c/E_0) \implies C_d = \frac{1}{\alpha} \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right) = D_p \ln(E_0/E_c)$$

**Numerical (2 marks):** With $D_p = 0.15$ mm, $E_c = 10$ mJ/cm², $E_0 = 50$ mJ/cm²:
$$C_d = 0.15 \cdot \ln(50/10) = 0.15 \cdot \ln(5) = 0.15 \cdot 1.6094$$
$$\boxed{\,C_d = 0.2414 \text{ mm}\,}$$

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** List and explain the **five major sub-systems of an SLA machine**. Describe the role of the **oxygen-permeable window** in bottom-up SLA. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and applications** of SLA. Compare the surface finish and accuracy of SLA with FDM. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) — Sub-systems (7 marks):**
1. **Vat / Resin Tank (1 mark):** Holds the photopolymer. Bottom-up vats use a transparent window with a fluoropolymer membrane. The fluoropolymer's **oxygen permeability** allows dissolved O₂ to diffuse through and form a ~10 µm **"dead zone"** of un-polymerized resin right above the window. This dead zone prevents the cured part from sticking to the window, enabling a **peel-free, low-force build** — critical for the precision SLA is famous for. **(3 marks total for explanation of sub-systems + dead zone)**
2. **Build Platform (1 mark):** Precision Z-stage, typically 1 – 5 µm resolution.
3. **Optical Engine (1 mark):** UV laser + beam expander + X-Y galvo scanners + F-theta lens.
4. **Recoater (1 mark):** Blade, wiper, or rotating roller to spread fresh resin.
5. **Control & Software (1 mark):** Slicer, hatch generator, exposure calculator.

**Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations, Applications & Comparison (7 marks):**

**Advantages (2 marks):** Best surface finish in AM (Ra 0.5 – 5 µm); highest dimensional accuracy (±0.1 %); capable of very fine features (< 100 µm); excellent for intricate lattice and micro-channel parts; wide range of engineering resins (transparent, tough, heat-resistant, castable, dental).

**Limitations (2 marks):** Limited mechanical/thermal properties of photopolymers (brittle, low HDT); requires support structures; post-curing needed (extra 30 – 60 min); expensive resins and machines; UV-sensitive materials degrade; oxygen inhibition complicates surface chemistry; limited multi-material capability.

**Applications (1 mark):** Dental crowns and aligners; jewelry patterns for investment casting; hearing-aid shells; medical anatomical models; microfluidics; optics (lenses, light guides); prototype visual models.

**SLA vs FDM Comparison (2 marks):** SLA delivers **superior surface finish (Ra ≈ 1 µm vs 10 – 20 µm for FDM)** and **tighter tolerances (±0.1 % vs ±0.5 %)** because of the much thinner layers and continuous photopolymerization (no nozzle-bead artifacts). FDM offers **stronger functional parts** (ABS, nylon, PC) and **larger build volumes** at **lower cost**. Choice depends on whether the goal is **aesthetic/precision prototype** (SLA) or **functional/structural part** (FDM).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numericals — $E_0$, $E_c$ are in **mJ/cm²**; mixing with J/cm² or mJ/mm² costs the unit mark.
> 2. **Skipping the derivation step of inverting $E_c/E_0$ to $E_0/E_c$** — many students lose 1 of the 5 derivation marks here.
> 3. **Calling SLA an "FDM-type" process** — it is a *vat photopolymerization*, NOT a material extrusion. This is a **favourite trick question** to test your grasp of ISO/ASTM 52900 taxonomy.
> 4. **Confusing top-down with bottom-up** — examiners often swap the laser and platform positions; the *correct* association is: top-down = laser on top + platform lowers; bottom-up = laser below + platform rises.
> 5. **Omitting the dead-zone concept** in the bottom-up explanation — a complete answer must mention **oxygen-permeable membrane** + **uncured dead zone** + **peel-force elimination**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **SLA = Vat Photopolymerization** (ISO/ASTM 52900), invented by **Chuck Hull in 1984**, first commercial 3D printing process.
- [x] **Photo-initiated free-radical polymerization** of (meth)acrylate resins cured by a **UV laser (He-Cd 325 nm or UV-LED 405 nm)**.
- [x] **Two architectures:** Top-Down (legacy, platform lowers) and **Bottom-Up (modern standard, platform rises, oxygen-permeable dead-zone window).**
- [x] **Five sub-systems:** Vat, Build Platform, Optical Engine (laser + galvos), Recoater, Control Electronics.
- [x] **Master equation (Working Curve):** $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0/E_c)$ — derived from **Beer–Lambert Law**.
- [x] **Cure width:** $W_c = W_0 \sqrt{C_d / D_p}$ — deeper cure ⇒ wider line ⇒ coarser resolution.
- [x] **Layer thickness range:** 0.025 – 0.3 mm; typical 0.05 – 0.1 mm; smaller layer = smoother part + longer build.
- [x] **Process steps:** CAD → STL → Slicing → Build prep → Layer-wise cure → **Post-cure (UV oven, 60 – 80 °C, 30 – 60 min)** → Support removal.
- [x] **SLA vs DLP:** laser = vector scanning, resolution = spot size; DLP = mask projection, resolution = pixel pitch; DLP is faster for large area.
- [x] **DLP evolution:** DLP → CDLP (continuous, ~100× faster) → mSLA (LCD, low cost).
- [x] **Advantages:** best surface finish, highest accuracy, intricate geometry, fine features.
- [x] **Limitations:** limited material properties (brittle), post-cure needed, expensive, supports required, oxygen inhibition.
- [x] **Applications:** dental, jewelry (investment casting patterns), hearing aids, microfluidics, anatomical models, optics, prototypes.
- [x] **KTU high-yield numbers to memorize:** $D_p \in [0.1, 0.3]$ mm; $E_c \in [5, 15]$ mJ/cm²; $W_0 \in [75, 250]$ µm; post-cure shrink 3 – 5 %.
- [x] **Time formula:** $T_{total} = n \cdot (t_{cure} + t_{recoat}) + t_{post}$, with $n = H_{part}/L_{layer}$.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Engineering Net Shaping (LENS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET525 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING AND 3D PRINTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laser Engineering Net Shaping (LENS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **LENS (Laser Engineering Net Shaping)** is a **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** based Additive Manufacturing process in which a high-power laser beam is used to create a moving molten pool on a substrate, into which metal powder is injected through coaxial or multi-jet nozzles to build up a fully dense, three-dimensional metallic part layer-by-layer.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

Laser Engineering Net Shaping (LENS) is a solid freeform fabrication process classified under **ASTM F2792-12a** as a **Powder Bed Fusion / Directed Energy Deposition (DED-PB / DED-G)** variant that uses a focused, high-power laser (typically a pulsed or continuous wave **Nd:YAG laser** operating at **1064 nm** wavelength) to melt metallic powder delivered through a deposition head. The deposition head is mounted on a multi-axis (typically 4 or 5-axis) gantry/CNC platform, allowing the part geometry to be built directly from a 3D CAD model by slicing it into horizontal layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Origin & Development:** LENS technology was originally developed by **Sandia National Laboratories (USA)** in the late 1990s and later commercialized by **Optomec Inc.** as the **LENS® 450, 750, and 850M** series. KTU examiners frequently ask about the origin — remember "Sandia → Optomec".

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **LENS** like a **3D pen welding with metal ink**:

- Imagine a regular **fountain pen** that draws with blue ink. Now replace the ink with **fine metal powder**, and instead of just letting the ink flow, use a **torch (laser)** to **melt** the powder exactly where you want it to stick. Then, move the "pen" up slightly and draw the next line on top. After thousands of such lines stacked on top of each other, you get a solid metal sculpture.

- In a more industrial sense, LENS is like a **giant, precise version of a glue gun**, but instead of glue, it sprays molten metal, and instead of squeezing the trigger with your hand, a **laser beam** does the melting on the fly.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exam:** "**L**aser **E**ngineering = L**E**ns focuses light. **N**et **S**haping = **N**o support structures needed, build the **N**et shape directly." → **LENS = Focused Light + Net Shape**

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Constants (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Laser Wavelength (Nd:YAG) | **1064** | nm |
| Typical Laser Power Range | **500 – 4000** | W |
| Powder Feed Rate | **0.1 – 30** | g/min |
| Build Envelope (LENS 850-M) | **900 × 1500 × 900** | mm |
| Layer Thickness | **0.25 – 1.0** | mm |
| Spot Diameter | **0.5 – 4.0** | mm |
| Deposition Atmosphere | **Argon (Ar)** | Inert |
| Oxygen Level (controlled) | **< 10 ppm** | ppm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constant:** The oxygen concentration inside the build chamber **must be maintained below 10 ppm** to prevent oxidation of the molten pool — this is a favourite 3-mark question in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Operation — Structured Logic Steps

The LENS process can be decomposed into the following sequential physical and thermal stages:

1. **CAD Model Preparation**
   - The desired part is modeled in CAD software (e.g., SolidWorks, CATIA) and exported as an **STL file**.
   - The STL file is sliced into horizontal layers of uniform thickness (**h_layer**) using dedicated slicing software.

2. **Powder Feeding & Delivery**
   - Metallic powder (typically **15–150 µm** particle size) is stored in a hopper and metered by a **powder feeder** (screw-type or rotating disc).
   - **Inert carrier gas (Argon)** transports the powder through flexible tubing to the **deposition head**.
   - Multiple powder streams converge at the laser focal point — typically 3 or 4 nozzles arranged coaxially around the laser beam path.

3. **Laser–Powder Interaction (Melt Pool Formation)**
   - A focused **Nd:YAG laser beam** strikes the substrate or previously deposited layer.
   - The laser energy is absorbed by the metal surface and powder particles (coupled via **absorption coefficient α**).
   - A **molten pool** (diameter ≈ 1–3 mm) is formed and maintained on the substrate.

4. **Solidification**
   - As the laser head moves away (scan speed **v**), the melt pool cools rapidly.
   - Cooling rates can reach **10³ to 10⁵ K/s**, producing a fine-grained microstructure.
   - Directional heat extraction → **columnar grains** aligned with the build direction.

5. **Layer-by-Layer Build-up**
   - The deposition head (or substrate) is raised by one layer thickness after each scan.
   - The next layer is melted on top of the previous, creating **metallurgical bonding** between layers.

6. **Multi-Axis Path Planning**
   - 4/5-axis systems allow the nozzle to remain **normal to the deposition surface**, enabling overhangs and curved geometries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the laser coaxial with the powder nozzles?**
> The coaxial arrangement ensures that the powder stream and laser beam intersect at the **same focal point regardless of the direction of motion**. This is what gives LENS its hallmark **5-axis deposition capability** without dead zones.

## 2.2 Critical Process Parameters

The quality of a LENS-built part is governed by four cardinal parameters. KTU examiners expect a tabular mapping of these parameters to their effect on the part:

| Parameter | Symbol | Effect on Part |
|---|---|---|
| **Laser Power** | P (W) | ↑ P → deeper melt pool, higher deposition, risk of substrate distortion |
| **Scan Speed** | v (mm/s) | ↑ v → thinner tracks, finer features, risk of lack-of-fusion |
| **Powder Feed Rate** | ṁ (g/min) | ↑ ṁ → thicker deposits, risk of unmelted powder |
| **Hatch Spacing** | Δh (mm) | ↑ Δh → rough surface, porosity; ↓ Δh → overlap, finer surface |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot d}$ | **Laser Energy Density** (P = power, v = scan speed, d = beam diameter) | J/mm² |
| 2 | $Q_{melt} = \rho \cdot V \cdot L_f$ | **Energy to melt powder** (ρ = density, V = volume, $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion) | J |
| 3 | $R_{dep} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot A_{track}}$ | **Deposition Rate** (ṁ = powder feed rate, A_track = cross-section) | mm/s |
| 4 | $h_{layer} = k \cdot \dfrac{P}{v \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot (T_m - T_0)}$ | **Approximate Layer Height** (k = efficiency, $c_p$ = specific heat) | mm |
| 5 | $T_{gradient} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta x}$ | **Thermal Gradient** governing residual stress | K/mm |
| 6 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{h_{layer}}{v_{solid}}$ | **Local Solidification Time** | s |
| 7 | $\eta_{abs} = 1 - R(\lambda)$ | **Laser Absorptivity** (R = reflectivity at wavelength λ) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Table-Breaking Note:** All absolute values / magnitudes above are written using **plain numeric expression** (e.g., $h_{layer}$ and $v_{solid}$), NOT the vertical pipe `|` character — this prevents markdown table-parsing failure in the rendered PDF/HTML.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Repair of **Ti-6Al-4V** turbine blades, deposition of nickel-superalloy claddings on compressor discs.
- **Automotive**: Rapid prototyping of high-strength steel dies.
- **Medical**: Custom **Co-Cr** knee and hip implants with graded porosity.
- **Defense**: On-site repair of ship propeller blades using portable LENS units.
- **Tooling**: Adding wear-resistant carbide coatings to HSS tool surfaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Density Required for a Track

**Objective:** Derive the laser **energy density** $E$ that determines whether a deposited track is fully dense, partially dense, or burnt.

> **Given / Standard Symbols:**
> - $P$ = laser power (W)
> - $v$ = scan speed (mm/s)
> - $d$ = laser spot diameter (mm)
> - $t_d$ = interaction time (s) = $d / v$
> - $A$ = spot area (mm²) = $\pi d^2 / 4$

### Step 1 — Time of Laser–Material Interaction

The laser beam stays in contact with any given point on the substrate for a duration equal to the spot diameter divided by the scan speed:

$$
t_d = \frac{d}{v}
$$

**Logic:** A slower scan or a wider beam gives more time for the metal to absorb energy.

### Step 2 — Total Energy Delivered per Spot

The total energy delivered while the beam dwells on one spot is simply power × time:

$$
E_{spot} = P \cdot t_d = P \cdot \frac{d}{v}
$$

**Logic:** This is the standard physics formula $E = P \times t$ in disguise.

### Step 3 — Energy Density (Energy per Unit Area)

Dividing by the spot area $A = \pi d^2 / 4$:

$$
E = \frac{E_{spot}}{A} = \frac{P \cdot (d/v)}{\pi d^2 / 4}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
E = \frac{4 P}{\pi d \cdot v}
$$

**Logic:** Since $A \propto d^2$ but $E_{spot} \propto d$, the energy density scales as $1/d$ — meaning a **smaller spot concentrates more energy** per unit area.

### Step 4 — Engineering Approximation (Most Common Form)

For exam purposes, KTU uses a simplified linear form:

$$
E = \frac{P}{v \cdot d} \quad \text{[J/mm}^2\text{]}
$$

**Logic:** The constant $4/\pi \approx 1.27$ is dropped for design convenience. Both forms are accepted; quote the simplified form unless the question specifies exactness.

### Step 5 — Quality Window

For most metals (e.g., SS316, Ti-6Al-4V), the empirical quality window is:

$$
50 \;\text{J/mm}^2 \;\le E \le 200 \;\text{J/mm}^2
$$

- $E < 50$ → **Lack of fusion** porosity
- $E > 200$ → **Keyhole** porosity and vaporization

## 3.2 Derivation: Deposition Rate in LENS

### Step 1 — Powder Mass Flow

Let $\dot{m}$ be the powder mass flow rate (g/s) entering the melt pool.

### Step 2 — Conversion to Volumetric Flow

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho}
$$

where $\rho$ is the density of the solid metal (e.g., for Ti-6Al-4V, $\rho = 4.43$ g/cm³).

### Step 3 — Cross-Section of a Single Track

A single bead has an approximate rectangular cross-section:

$$
A_{track} = w \cdot h = d \cdot h_{layer}
$$

where $w \approx d$ (the bead width approximates the spot diameter).

### Step 4 — Linear Deposition Rate

$$
R_{dep} = \frac{\dot{V}}{A_{track}} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot d \cdot h_{layer}} \quad \text{[mm/s]}
$$

**Logic:** This is the speed at which the head can lay down a continuous, void-free track — a critical specification for production planning.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter → Effect → Defect Mapping

| Parameter | Low Value Effect | High Value Effect | Common Defect if Mis-set |
|---|---|---|---|
| Laser Power P | Cold melt pool, no fusion | Excessive dilution, keyhole | **Lack of fusion / Keyhole** |
| Scan Speed v | Overheating, sag | Starved melt pool, voids | **Sagging / Porosity** |
| Powder Feed ṁ | Incomplete filling, porosity | Overflowing puddle, balling | **Balling / Surface roughness** |
| Oxygen ppm | Oxidation, brittle parts | n/a | **Inclusions, embrittlement** |
| Layer thickness h | Excessive remelting | Stepped surface, delamination | **Delamination** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** A common mistake students make is writing *"increase power to fix porosity"* without specifying *which kind of porosity*. Always state: **lack-of-fusion porosity** is fixed by ↑ P or ↓ v, while **keyhole porosity** is fixed by ↓ P or ↑ v.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LENS System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional architecture** of a LENS system, mapping the interactions between sub-systems. (Per the protocol, this is rendered as a block-level architecture since physical 3D-machine drawing is impractical in Mermaid.)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CAD Model STL] --> B[Slicer Software]
    B --> C[Layer Slice Data]
    C --> D[Path Planner]
    D --> E[CNC Controller]
    
    E --> F[5 Axis Gantry Motion]
    F --> G[Deposition Head Positioning]
    
    P1[Powder Hopper 1] --> Q1[Powder Feeder Metal A]
    P2[Powder Hopper 2] --> Q2[Powder Feeder Metal B]
    
    Q1 --> R[Coaxial Nozzle Assembly]
    Q2 --> R
    
    L1[Nd YAG Laser 1064 nm] --> M[Beam Delivery Optics]
    M --> R
    
    G --> R
    R --> S[Melt Pool on Substrate]
    S --> T[Solidified Layer]
    T --> U[Next Layer Z Increment]
    U --> S
    
    IG[Argon Gas Cylinder] --> R
    IG --> CH[Sealed Chamber less than 10 ppm O2]
    
    CH --> S
    
    T --> X[Final 3D Metallic Part]
    T --> Y[Support Removal and Post Processing]
    Y --> X
```

**Block-Level Reading Order for the Diagram:**

1. **A → B → C → D → E** = the **digital thread** (CAD → Slice → Path → CNC).
2. **L1 → M → R** = the **laser delivery chain** (source → optics → nozzle).
3. **P1 → Q1 → R** and **P2 → Q2 → R** = the **dual powder feeders** (enables multi-material / graded parts).
4. **IG → CH → R** = the **inert atmosphere envelope** (critical for non-reactive processing).
5. **R → S → T → U → S** = the **closed-loop deposition cycle** (melt → solidify → increment → re-melt).
6. **T → Y → X** = the **post-processing exit path** to the finished part.

## 4.2 Process Parameter Interaction Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Laser Power P] --> X[Energy Density E]
    B[Scan Speed v] --> X
    C[Spot Diameter d] --> X
    
    X --> D[Melt Pool Temperature]
    X --> E[Melt Pool Depth]
    X --> F[Melt Pool Width]
    
    G[Powder Feed Rate m_dot] --> H[Track Height h]
    X --> H
    
    H --> I[Surface Roughness Ra]
    H --> J[Inter Layer Bonding]
    
    I --> K[Final Part Quality]
    J --> K
    
    D --> L[Residual Stress and Distortion]
    E --> L
    F --> L
```

**Reading Order:**

- The **first cluster (A, B, C → X)** establishes that **Energy Density is the unifying input**.
- The **second cluster (X → D, E, F)** shows how energy density controls the **geometry of the melt pool**.
- The **third cluster (G + X → H)** integrates the powder stream into the deposited **track height**.
- The **fourth cluster (H → I, J → K)** translates the track properties into **surface finish and bonding quality**.
- The **bottom row (D, E, F → L)** isolates the **thermal-mechanical effects** that drive distortion.

## 4.3 Defect-Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LENS Part Inspection] --> B{Visual Porosity?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Powder Unmelted?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Lack of Fusion]
    D --> D1[Fix: Increase P or Decrease v]
    C -- No --> E{Crater Like Cavity?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Keyhole Porosity]
    F --> F1[Fix: Decrease P or Increase v]
    E -- No --> G[Gas Porosity]
    G --> G1[Fix: Improve Ar Purging]
    
    B -- No --> H{Surface Rough?}
    H -- Yes --> I[High Layer Thickness]
    I --> I1[Fix: Reduce h_layer or Reduce Hatch]
    H -- No --> J{Distortion?}
    J -- Yes --> K[High Residual Stress]
    K --> K1[Fix: Preheat Substrate or Use Scan Strategy]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not confuse LENS with SLM (Selective Laser Melting).** SLM uses a powder bed, LENS uses **powder injection through a nozzle**. Examiners deduct marks for this interchange.
> 2. **Mention the oxygen limit (< 10 ppm)** whenever you describe the LENS atmosphere — losing 1 mark for omission is common.
> 3. **Always state the laser type (Nd:YAG, 1064 nm)** in a "describe LENS" answer. Writing just "laser" loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Avoid the phrase "similar to welding"** — LENS is not welding. Use "**laser-assisted powder deposition with metallurgical bonding**".

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any three distinguishing features of the LENS process compared to powder-bed fusion processes like SLM.*

**Model Answer (3 points):**

1. **Powder Delivery Method:** LENS uses **coaxial powder injection** through a nozzle directly into the melt pool, whereas SLM uses a **pre-laid powder bed** and a roller/recater. (*1 mark*)
2. **Build Volume / Part Size:** LENS can build **larger parts** (up to ~1 m) because there is no confined powder bed; SLM is typically limited to ~400 mm³. (*1 mark*)
3. **Multi-Material & Repair Capability:** LENS supports **multiple powder feeders** for **functionally graded materials** and is widely used for **in-situ repair** of high-value components (e.g., turbine blades). SLM is largely single-material. (*1 mark*)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Why is an Argon atmosphere maintained in the LENS build chamber? What is the typical oxygen threshold?*

**Model Answer (3 points):**

1. **Purpose:** The Argon atmosphere **prevents oxidation** of the molten metal pool and the heated powder stream. Oxidation would otherwise form brittle oxide inclusions, reducing fatigue life and ductility. (*2 marks*)
2. **Threshold:** Oxygen concentration is maintained **below 10 ppm** (parts per million) for reactive metals like titanium and aluminum alloys. (*1 mark*)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** With the help of a schematic, explain the **working principle of the LENS process**. Discuss the role of each major sub-system.  *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A LENS machine is to deposit a single track on a Ti-6Al-4V substrate using the following parameters:
- Laser Power, $P = 1500$ W
- Scan Speed, $v = 10$ mm/s
- Spot diameter, $d = 3$ mm
- Powder feed rate, $\dot{m} = 6$ g/min
- Density of Ti-6Al-4V, $\rho = 4.43$ g/cm³

Calculate:
   (i) The **laser energy density** $E$ in J/mm². *(2 marks)*
   (ii) The **linear deposition rate** $R_{dep}$ in mm/s, assuming a layer thickness of 0.5 mm. *(2 marks)*
   (iii) Comment on whether the energy density falls in the **defect-free window** for Ti-6Al-4V (50–200 J/mm²). *(3 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Working Principle of LENS (7 marks)**

The **Laser Engineering Net Shaping (LENS)** process builds metal parts by **depositing and melting powder layer-by-layer** under the focused energy of a high-power laser. The schematic (refer to the Block Architecture in Section 4) shows the following sub-systems:

1. **Laser Source — Nd:YAG, 1064 nm (1 mark):** Provides the high-power coherent beam (typically 500 W to 4 kW) that forms the melt pool.
2. **Beam Delivery Optics (1 mark):** Mirrors and a focusing lens direct and focus the laser beam into the deposition head.
3. **Powder Feeders & Carrier Gas (1 mark):** A screw or disc feeder meters powder from a hopper; Argon gas carries the powder through tubing to the coaxial nozzle.
4. **Coaxial Nozzle Assembly (1 mark):** Multiple nozzles (typically 3 or 4) surround the laser axis symmetrically. This **coaxial arrangement** allows the deposition head to move in any direction in 3D space while maintaining a constant focal point — the basis of 5-axis capability.
5. **Multi-Axis CNC Platform (1 mark):** A 4- or 5-axis gantry/robotic arm moves the deposition head along the toolpath generated from the sliced CAD model.
6. **Inert Atmosphere Chamber (1 mark):** A sealed enclosure purged with Argon maintains oxygen concentration **< 10 ppm**, preventing oxidation of reactive metals like titanium.
7. **Closed-Loop Deposition Cycle (1 mark):** The head deposits one layer (thickness 0.25–1 mm), moves up by one layer thickness, and the next layer is melted on top — creating a **metallurgically bonded** solid 3D part.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Energy Density (2 marks):**

Using the simplified formula $E = P / (v \cdot d)$:

$$
E = \frac{1500 \text{ W}}{10 \text{ mm/s} \times 3 \text{ mm}} = \frac{1500}{30} = 50 \text{ J/mm}^2
$$

**(ii) Linear Deposition Rate (2 marks):**

First, convert powder feed rate to g/s:
$$
\dot{m} = \frac{6 \text{ g/min}}{60 \text{ s/min}} = 0.1 \text{ g/s}
$$

Convert density to g/mm³:
$$
\rho = 4.43 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 4.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/mm}^3
$$

Track cross-section: $A_{track} = d \times h_{layer} = 3 \text{ mm} \times 0.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \text{ mm}^2$

$$
R_{dep} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot A_{track}} = \frac{0.1}{4.43 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5} = \frac{0.1}{6.645 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 15.05 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**(iii) Quality Comment (3 marks):**

The calculated energy density is **exactly 50 J/mm²**, which lies **at the lower bound** of the defect-free window (50–200 J/mm²) for Ti-6Al-4V.

- The track is **just barely fused** — there is risk of **lack-of-fusion porosity** at the substrate interface.
- **Recommendation:** Slightly **increase P to ~1800 W** (yielding E ≈ 60 J/mm²) or **decrease v to 8 mm/s** (yielding E ≈ 62.5 J/mm²) to operate safely in the middle of the window.
- Operating at the lower edge makes the part **highly sensitive to small fluctuations** in powder flow or laser output.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Compare the **LENS process** with **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** under the following heads: (i) Powder delivery mechanism, (ii) Build volume, (iii) Surface finish, (iv) Multi-material capability, (v) Typical applications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A LENS-deposited SS316L part shows **porosity on X-ray CT inspection**. Diagnose the possible defect mode and prescribe a **corrective action plan** for each of the following parameter changes: (i) Laser power reduced by 30%, (ii) Scan speed increased by 50%, (iii) Oxygen level drifted to 200 ppm. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) LENS vs SLM Comparison (7 marks)**

| Head | LENS | SLM |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Powder Delivery | **Coaxial nozzle injection** into a moving melt pool | **Pre-laid powder bed** spread by roller/recater |
| (ii) Build Volume | **Larger** (~900 × 1500 × 900 mm possible) | **Smaller** (~400 × 400 × 400 mm typical) |
| (iii) Surface Finish | **Rougher** (Ra 10–25 µm), requires machining | **Smoother** (Ra 5–15 µm) |
| (iv) Multi-Material | **Yes** — multiple feeders, FGM possible | **Limited** — single powder bed at a time |
| (v) Applications | Repair of high-value parts, large aerospace structures, cladding | Intricate functional prototypes, dental/medical implants, lattice structures |

*Each row carries ~1.4 marks. Distribute 7 marks across the five points.*

**(b) Defect Diagnosis & Corrective Actions (7 marks)**

**Defect Diagnosis:** Porosity in SS316L under LENS typically arises from one of three root causes — **lack of fusion, gas entrapment, or oxidation**.

| Parameter Change | Defect Mode | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| (i) **P reduced by 30%** → E falls below 50 J/mm² | **Lack-of-fusion porosity** (un-melted powder particles, irregular voids) | **Increase P back to nominal**, or **decrease v** to restore E in the 50–200 J/mm² window |
| (ii) **v increased by 50%** → E drops sharply (E ∝ 1/v) | **Starved melt pool** → irregular voids along track edges | **Reduce v** to original value; alternatively raise **P** to compensate |
| (iii) **O₂ drift to 200 ppm** | **Oxide inclusions + gas porosity** (Argon purge failure) | **Re-purge chamber with Argon**, verify O₂ < 10 ppm, check seals/leaks; consider powder pre-drying |

*Each diagnosis-action pair carries ~2.3 marks.*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **LENS = Directed Energy Deposition** (DED), **not** Powder Bed Fusion (PBF). *(Class difference)*
- **Laser type:** Nd:YAG, **1064 nm** wavelength, power range **500 W – 4 kW**.
- **Powder delivery:** **Coaxial nozzle** (multi-jet, 3 or 4 streams).
- **Atmosphere:** **Argon-purged chamber**, oxygen **< 10 ppm**.
- **Energy density formula:** $E = P / (v \cdot d)$ with units **J/mm²**.
- **Defect-free window for Ti-6Al-4V:** **50 ≤ E ≤ 200 J/mm²**.
- **Below window → lack-of-fusion porosity**; **Above window → keyhole porosity**.
- **Layer thickness:** 0.25 – 1.0 mm; **Build envelope:** up to 1 m in industrial LENS units.
- **Origin:** **Sandia National Labs** → commercialized by **Optomec (LENS 450/750/850-M)**.
- **Key advantages:** (1) Multi-material / FGM, (2) Large part capability, (3) In-situ repair, (4) Directional deposition, (5) High deposition rate.
- **Key disadvantages:** (1) Rough surface finish, (2) Residual stress/distortion, (3) Limited feature resolution, (4) Requires inert chamber, (5) Powder wastage.
- **Deposition rate formula:** $R_{dep} = \dot{m} / (\rho \cdot d \cdot h_{layer})$.
- **5-axis capability** comes from the **coaxial nozzle design** — the focal point stays at the nozzle tip regardless of head orientation.
- **Cooling rates** in LENS: **10³ to 10⁵ K/s** → **fine, columnar grain** microstructure.
- **Most exam-favoured comparisons:** LENS vs **SLM**, LENS vs **DMD (Direct Metal Deposition)**, LENS vs **EBM**.
- **Valuation mantra:** "Specify the laser, specify the atmosphere, specify the powder size, and always close with the application."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hands-on Sessions using FDM and Resin 3D Printers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET525 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING AND 3D PRINTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hands-on Sessions using FDM and Resin 3D Printers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Hands-on Sessions in Additive Manufacturing

**Hands-on Sessions** in the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for *Additive Manufacturing and 3D Printing* refer to the structured laboratory practice modules where students physically operate **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** printers and **Resin-based (Vat Photopolymerization / SLA / DLP)** printers to fabricate actual three-dimensional parts from digital CAD models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Strict Terminology):**
> A hands-on session is defined as the supervised, end-to-end execution of the **Digital Thread Workflow** — from *CAD model preparation → STL/OBJ export → Slicing → Machine Setup → Print Execution → Post-Processing → Quality Inspection* — using both an **Extrusion-based (FDM)** system and a **Vat Photopolymerization (Resin)** system, with documented parameter logging as per laboratory rubrics.

## 1.2 The Two Printer Families Used in the Session

The KTU curriculum exposes students to the **two most pedagogically distinct** AM technologies, namely:

1. **FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling)** — a *Material Extrusion* process where a thermoplastic filament is melted and deposited layer by layer.
2. **Resin 3D Printing** — a *Vat Photopolymerization* process where a liquid photopolymer resin is selectively cured by **UV light (typically 405 nm wavelength)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **FEP vs FEP-Free Resin Tanks:** Modern resin printers (e.g., Mars, Saturn series) use an **FEP (Fluorinated Ethylene Propylene)** film at the bottom of the vat, which is optically transparent, chemically resistant, and acts as a non-stick release layer.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a 3D printer as a **"smart cake icing machine"**:

- The **FDM printer** is like a **hot glue gun mounted on a moving robotic arm**. It melts a plastic stick (filament) and squeezes out thin lines (like icing a cake), one layer at a time, to build a 3D object from the bottom up.
- The **Resin printer** is like a **digital projector that hardens liquid plastic with light**. The liquid resin is like a tray of honey; wherever a beam of UV light touches it, it instantly becomes solid. The projector draws one thin slice (cross-section) at a time, and the object slowly rises out of the honey.

| Aspect | FDM (Hot Glue Analogy) | Resin (Honey + Light Analogy) |
|---|---|---|
| Raw Material | Solid plastic filament on a spool | Liquid photopolymer resin in a vat |
| Hardening Mechanism | Cooling of melted plastic | UV-light triggered chemical cross-linking |
| Layer Thickness | 0.1 – 0.4 mm (typical) | 0.01 – 0.1 mm (much finer) |
| Surface Finish | Visible layer lines | Smooth, almost injection-molded quality |
| Strength | Anisotropic (weak Z-axis) | Brittle but isotropic |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Highlighted in the Lab Rubric

The following metrics are the **key performance indicators (KPIs)** recorded in every hands-on session per the KTU 2024 lab manual:

- **Print Accuracy (dimensional tolerance):** typically **±0.1 mm** for FDM and **±0.05 mm** for Resin.
- **Layer Height:** adjustable from **0.02 mm to 0.3 mm** depending on the printer class.
- **Infill Density:** typically **15%–100%** for FDM.
- **Post-Cure Time:** **2–10 minutes** under 405 nm UV for resin prints.
- **Washing Time:** **3–5 minutes in IPA (Isopropyl Alcohol ≥ 90% concentration)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** KTU evaluators consistently reward students who explicitly mention **build orientation**, **support placement strategy**, and **post-processing protocol** in their lab records.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The FDM Hands-on Workflow — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown
