### **Worked Example 3 — Material Cost Calculation**

**Problem:**
> A medical model has a CAD volume of **45 cm³**. The photopolymer used is VisiJet M3 Crystal, with density **$1.12 \, g/cm^3$** and a cost of **₹18,000 per kg**. The support material accounts for **25% of the build material volume** (most of which is recycled, so only 15% of support material is consumed). Calculate the cost of the build material for one part.

**Given:**
- Part volume $V_{\text{part}} = 45$ cm³
- Density $\rho = 1.12$ g/cm³
- Cost per kg = ₹18,000
- Support material = 25% of build volume
- Support consumption = 15% of that (i.e., 85% recycled)

**Step 1 — Calculate support material volume:**

$$V_{\text{support}} = 0.25 \times V_{\text{part}} = 0.25 \times 45 = 11.25 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Calculate consumed support material:**

$$V_{\text{support,consumed}} = 0.15 \times 11.25 = 1.6875 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Calculate consumed build material volume:**

Since the part is 100% build material:

$$V_{\text{build,consumed}} = V_{\text{part}} = 45 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Total material volume consumed:**

$$V_{\text{total,consumed}} = 45 + 1.6875 = 46.6875 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mass:**

$$m_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{total,consumed}} \times \rho = 46.6875 \times 1.12$$

$$m_{\text{total}} = 52.29 \text{ g} = 0.05229 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate cost:**

$$C = 0.05229 \times 18{,}000$$

$$\boxed{C \approx \text{₹} 941.22}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** MJM material is expensive (~₹15,000–₹25,000/kg). The economics of MJM is heavily influenced by **support material recycling efficiency**. Modern 3D Systems ProJet printers claim up to **80–90% support material recyclability**.

---

### **Worked Example 4 — Crosslink Density & Heat Distortion Temperature**

**Problem:**
> A cured MJM photopolymer has a density $\rho_p = 1.18 \, g/cm^3$ and a molecular weight between crosslinks $M_c = 850 \, g/mol$. Calculate the crosslink density and comment on the expected heat distortion temperature (HDT).

**Step 1 — Apply the crosslink formula:**

$$\nu_e = \frac{\rho_p}{2 M_c} = \frac{1.18}{2 \times 850}$$

$$\nu_e = \frac{1.18}{1700} = 6.94 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{mol/cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{\nu_e \approx 6.94 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{mol/cm}^3}$$

> **Explanation:** A higher $\nu_e$ means a more rigid, glassy, brittle polymer network with higher HDT and chemical resistance but lower impact strength. Standard MJM resins typically have $\nu_e$ in the range $5 \times 10^{-4}$ to $15 \times 10^{-4} \, mol/cm^3$, corresponding to HDT values of **50 °C to 120 °C**.

---

### **Worked Example 5 — Surface Roughness Estimation**

**Problem:**
> A dental mould is printed at layer thickness 0.016 mm using MJM. Estimate the average surface roughness $R_a$ using the empirical relationship $R_a \approx 0.5 \times t_L$.

**Step 1 — Apply the empirical formula:**

$$R_a \approx 0.5 \times t_L = 0.5 \times 0.016 \text{ mm}$$

$$R_a = 0.008 \text{ mm} = 8 \, \mu m$$

$$\boxed{R_a \approx 8 \, \mu m}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This roughness is suitable for **investment casting patterns** (which are subsequently melted out and replaced by molten metal). For optical applications, post-polishing is required to bring $R_a$ down to **< 0.5 µm**.

---

### **Worked Example 6 — Volumetric Throughput of Print Head Array**

**Problem:**
> A MJM print head has 1500 active nozzles. Each nozzle fires at 25 kHz and ejects 30 pL droplets. Calculate the volumetric throughput in mm³/s.

**Step 1 — Calculate droplets per second:**

$$f_{\text{total}} = n_{\text{nozzles}} \times f_{\text{nozzle}} = 1500 \times 25{,}000$$

$$f_{\text{total}} = 3.75 \times 10^7 \text{ droplets/s}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by droplet volume:**

$$Q_{\text{vol}} = 3.75 \times 10^7 \times 30 \times 10^{-12} \, m^3/s$$

$$Q_{\text{vol}} = 1.125 \times 10^{-3} \, m^3/s$$

Wait — we need to convert to mm³/s. $1 \, m^3 = 10^9 \, mm^3$:

$$Q_{\text{vol}} = 1.125 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^9 \, mm^3/s$$

$$Q_{\text{vol}} = 1.125 \times 10^6 \, mm^3/s$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{vol}} \approx 1.125 \times 10^6 \, mm^3/s \approx 1125 \, cm^3/s}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is the *peak* throughput. In practice, only ~30–40% of nozzles fire per layer (since most pixels are "white space" — empty regions). The *effective* throughput is therefore closer to **350–500 cm³/s of deposited material**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Decision Chart

| **Parameter** | **MJM (3D Systems)** | **PolyJet (Stratasys)** | **SLA** | **FDM** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Layer thickness | 16–32 µm | 14–28 µm | 25–100 µm | 100–300 µm |
| Surface roughness $R_a$ | 4–8 µm | 4–6 µm | 10–25 µm | 100–250 µm |
| Multi-material capability | YES (limited) | **YES (excellent)** | NO | NO |
| Build speed | Medium | Fast | Slow | Slow |
| Material cost | High | High | High | Low |
| Wall thickness min | 0.3 mm | 0.2 mm | 0.4 mm | 0.8 mm |
| Accuracy | ±0.025 mm | ±0.02 mm | ±0.05 mm | ±0.2 mm |
| Casting pattern quality | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Poor |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Material Jetting (MJM) System

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the *internal functional architecture* of a Material Jetting (MultiJet Modeling) printer, illustrating the major sub-systems, their roles, and the directional flow of material, energy, and data.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CAD[STL or 3MF CAD File Input] --> SLICER[Slicer Software 3D Sprint or GrabCAD]
    SLICER --> BITMAP[Per Layer Bitmap Print Map]
    BITMAP --> CTRL[Motion Controller FPGA Board]
    CTRL --> PIEZO[Piezoelectric Print Head Array]
    CTRL --> UVLAMP[UV Curing Lamp Array]
    CTRL --> ZSTAGE[Z Axis Platform Stepper]
    RESIN[Build Material Reservoir VisiJet Resin] --> PIEZO
    SUPPORT[Support Material Reservoir Wax or Gel] --> PIEZO
    PIEZO --> DROPLET[Micro Droplet Ejection pL scale]
    DROPLET --> DEPOS[Deposition on Build Platform or Previous Layer]
    DEPOS --> UVCURE[Instant UV Photopolymerization 365 to 405 nm]
    UVLAMP --> UVCURE
    UVCURE --> SLICED[Newly Cured Solid Layer]
    ZSTAGE --> DEPOS
    SLICED --> REPEAT{Layer Count Target Reached?}
    REPEAT --> NO1[NO Return to BitMap Stage]
    NO1 --> BITMAP
    REPEAT --> YES1[YES Build Complete]
    YES1 --> POST[Post Processing Support Removal Water Jet or Heat]
    POST --> UVFINAL[Optional UV Post Cure Chamber 10 to 60 min]
    UVFINAL --> INSPECT[Dimensional and Visual Inspection]
    INSPECT --> PART[Finished MJM Part]
```

---

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Build Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1 CAD Import] --> B[Step 2 Slicing and Bitmap Generation]
    B --> C[Step 3 Platform Level and Z Position]
    C --> D[Step 4 Jet Build Material Droplets]
    D --> E[Step 5 Jet Support Material Droplets]
    E --> F[Step 6 UV Cure Layer]
    F --> G[Step 7 Wiper Pass for Flatness]
    G --> H{All Layers Done?}
    H --> NO[No Lower Platform by tL]
    NO --> D
    H --> YES[Yes Raise Platform Out of Resin]
    YES --> I[Step 8 Drain Excess Resin]
    I --> J[Step 9 Support Removal]
    J --> K[Step 10 UV Post Cure]
    K --> L[Step 11 Inspection and Delivery]
```

---

## 4.3 Comparison Block — MJM vs PolyJet vs SLA

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AMCategory[Additive Manufacturing Material Jetting and Vat]
        MJM[MJM 3D Systems MultiJet Modeling]
        POLY[PolyJet Stratasys]
        SLA2[SLA Vat Photopolymerization]
    end
    MJM --> M1[Acrylic based photopolymers]
    MJM --> M2[UV cure 365 to 405 nm]
    MJM --> M3[16 to 32 micron layers]
    POLY --> P1[Acrylic and elastomer photopolymers]
    POLY --> P2[UV cure 365 to 405 nm]
    POLY --> P3[14 to 28 micron layers]
    SLA2 --> S1[Epoxy or acrylate resins]
    SLA2 --> S2[UV laser or projector]
    SLA2 --> S3[25 to 100 micron layers]
```

---

## 4.4 Material-to-Process Suitability Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Select MJM Process] --> Q1{Is the part for visual demonstration?}
    Q1 --> YES1[Use Acrylic Photopolymer e.g. VisiJet Crystal]
    Q1 --> NO1[Continue]
    NO1 --> Q2{Does the part require flexibility?}
    Q2 --> YES2[Use Elastomer Photopolymer e.g. VisiJet Flex]
    Q2 --> NO2[Continue]
    NO2 --> Q3{Is the part a casting pattern?}
    Q3 --> YES3[Use Castable Wax Resin e.g. VisiJet FTX]
    Q3 --> NO3[Continue]
    NO3 --> Q4{Is the part for medical use?}
    Q4 --> YES4[Use Biocompatible Resin e.g. VisiJet M3 Bio]
    Q4 --> NO4[Use Standard Rigid Photopolymer]
```

---

## 4.5 Build Volume Schematic (Axonometric Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BuildVolume[Typical MJM Build Volume 298 x 185 x 203 mm]
        direction TB
        X[Width X axis 298 mm]
        Y[Depth Y axis 185 mm]
        Z[Height Z axis 203 mm]
    end
    X --> PLATFORM[Aluminium Build Platform]
    Y --> PLATFORM
    Z --> PLATFORM
    PLATFORM --> PART[3D Printed Part Max]
```

> **Note on Mermaid compliance:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1` is replaced by descriptive uppercase labels like `CAD`, `SLICER`, etc., prefixed with letters to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts like `end`, `graph`, `subgraph`). No markdown formatting appears inside any quoted label.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *Define Material Jetting in the context of additive manufacturing. Mention any two key differences between Material Jetting and Vat Photopolymerization.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Material Jetting is an additive manufacturing process in which droplets of a liquid build material (typically a photopolymer resin) are selectively jetted from one or more inkjet-style print heads and simultaneously cured using ultraviolet (UV) light, layer by layer, to form a solid 3D object. A support material is jetted simultaneously to support overhangs.
>
> **Differences (1.5 marks — any two of the following):**
> 1. In **Material Jetting**, the liquid resin is *jetted only at required locations*; in **Vat Photopolymerization**, the *entire vat* of liquid is filled and only specific regions are selectively cured.
> 2. Material Jetting uses *droplet deposition* (inkjet-like); Vat Photopolymerization uses *laser scanning (SLA)* or *whole-layer projection (DLP)*.
> 3. Material Jetting can deposit *multiple materials in a single build*; Vat Photopolymerization is generally limited to a single resin per build.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *List any four materials that can be used in the Material Jetting (MultiJet Modeling) process. State one application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **Acrylic photopolymer (VisiJet Crystal):** Application — visual prototypes, transparent models, lens prototypes.
> 2. **Castable wax resin (VisiJet FTX):** Application — investment casting patterns for jewellery and turbine blades.
> 3. **Elastomer photopolymer (VisiJet Flex / TangoPlus):** Application — gaskets, soft-touch grips, wearable prototypes.
> 4. **Biocompatible photopolymer (VisiJet M3 Bio / MED610):** Application — surgical guides, dental models, anatomical replicas.
>
> *(Any 4 correct material–application pairs = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> *With neat diagrams, explain the working principle of the **Material Jetting (MultiJet Modeling)** process. Discuss the materials used, process parameters, benefits, limitations, and major applications.*

**Model Answer (14 marks):**

**(a) Working Principle (7 marks):**

Material Jetting (MJM) is a droplet-based additive manufacturing process in which thousands of micro-droplets of a liquid photopolymer resin are ejected from a piezoelectric print head array onto a build platform. Each droplet is on the order of **5–80 picolitres** in volume. The print head scans across the build platform in the X–Y plane, jetting droplets according to a **sliced bitmap image** generated from the CAD model. As soon as a layer is deposited, high-power **UV lamps (365–405 nm)** cure the photopolymer instantly through free-radical polymerization. The build platform then descends by one layer thickness (typically **16–32 µm**), and the process repeats.

A second print head simultaneously deposits a **support material** (wax or gel) underneath overhangs and inside complex cavities. After the build is complete, the support is removed by **water-jetting, melting, or hand rinsing**.

> *Insert a labelled block diagram showing: STL input → Slicer → Print head array → Droplet deposition → UV lamp → Cured layer → Z-step → Repeated layers → Support material → Final part.*

Key sub-processes:
- **Piezoelectric drop-on-demand (DOD) jetting** — droplets ejected only where needed.
- **UV photopolymerization** — acrylate/methacrylate monomers crosslink to form a solid.
- **Simultaneous multi-material deposition** — build and support materials jetted in a single pass.
- **Z-axis incremental layering** — platform descends by $t_L$ after each layer.

**(b) Materials, Benefits, Limitations & Applications (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Details |
|---|---|
| **Materials** | Acrylic photopolymers (rigid); elastomer photopolymers (flexible); castable wax resins; high-temperature photopolymers; biocompatible photopolymers. |
| **Key Process Parameters** | Layer thickness (16–32 µm), droplet volume (5–80 pL), nozzle firing frequency (up to 30 kHz), UV intensity, build chamber temperature (30–35 °C). |
| **Benefits** | High dimensional accuracy (±25 µm); excellent surface finish ($R_a$ ≈ 4–8 µm); multi-material and multi-colour capability; fast lead time; clean burnout for investment casting. |
| **Limitations** | Limited to photopolymers (not true engineering thermoplastics); high material cost; parts can be brittle and UV-sensitive over time; support removal is labour-intensive; build volume is moderate. |
| **Applications** | Investment casting patterns (jewellery, turbine blades); dental and medical models; soft-touch prototypes; transparent parts for flow visualization; short-run production of optical components. |

**Conclusion:** MJM offers the highest surface quality and resolution among commercial AM processes and excels in applications demanding multi-material realism and ultra-fine detail.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
> *Compare and contrast **Material Jetting (MultiJet Modeling)** with **Vat Photopolymerization (SLA)**. Discuss the droplet physics involved in MJM and derive the expression for the Weber number. List the factors affecting surface finish in MJM parts.*

**Model Answer (14 marks):**

**(a) Comparison with SLA (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Material Jetting (MJM) | Vat Photopolymerization (SLA) |
|---|---|---|
| Material state | Liquid droplets jetted on demand | Liquid vat of resin |
| Deposition method | Inkjet-style droplets | Laser scanning (SLA) or whole-layer projection (DLP) |
| Resin containment | Jet only at required locations | Entire vat filled |
| Multi-material | YES (multi-cartridge) | NO (single resin) |
| Build speed | Faster (parallel jetting) | Slower (point-by-point laser cure) |
| Surface roughness | 4–8 µm | 10–25 µm |
| Material cost | High | High |
| Support material | Wax/gel, jetted simultaneously | Same resin, requires breakaway or manual removal |
| Accuracy | ±25 µm | ±50 µm |
| Wall thickness minimum | 0.2–0.3 mm | 0.4–0.5 mm |

**Droplet Physics in MJM (3.5 marks):**
The Weber number expresses the ratio of inertial forces to surface tension forces acting on a droplet during ejection and impact:

$$We = \frac{\rho v^2 D_0}{\sigma}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of photopolymer (kg/m³)
- $v$ = droplet velocity (m/s)
- $D_0$ = droplet diameter (m)
- $\sigma$ = surface tension (N/m)

For stable jetting without splashing, $We$ should ideally lie in the range **4 to 50** depending on substrate and viscosity. MJM systems are tuned to keep $We$ near the centre of this range to ensure clean spreading without satellite droplet formation.

**Factors Affecting Surface Finish in MJM (3.5 marks):**
1. **Layer thickness $t_L$** — finer layers produce smoother surfaces ($R_a \approx 0.5 t_L$).
2. **Droplet size and placement accuracy** — smaller, well-placed droplets reduce stair-stepping.
3. **Photopolymer viscosity** — low viscosity improves spreading and reduces voids.
4. **UV exposure dose** — overcure or undercure both degrade surface integrity.
5. **Build orientation** — angled surfaces show stair-stepping; flat horizontal surfaces are smoothest.
6. **Post-curing uniformity** — uneven UV post-cure can cause surface warpage or colour shifts.
7. **Wiper/roller pass accuracy** — poor planarization leaves ridges between layers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Conflating MJM (3D Systems) with PolyJet (Stratasys). They are **NOT identical** — they use different print heads, resins, and support materials. Examiners deduct up to 1 mark for interchanging names.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Writing "MJM uses a vat of resin" — this is **WRONG**. MJM does *not* use a vat. Deduct 1–2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Omitting the **support material** in the process description — examiners specifically look for the dual-material (build + support) feature. At least 1 mark is lost if support material is not mentioned.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Using **FDM terminology** like "filament" or "extruder" — this shows conceptual confusion. MJM uses **liquid droplets** and **inkjet print heads**, not thermoplastic extrusion.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Forgetting to convert units in numerical problems. KTU examiners expect you to show unit conversions clearly (e.g., µm → mm → m).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Material Jetting (MJM)**

- **Process category:** Material Jetting (ISO/ASTM 52900); sub-type MultiJet Modeling (3D Systems).
- **Core mechanism:** Piezoelectric drop-on-demand (DOD) jetting of photopolymer droplets + instantaneous UV curing (365–405 nm).
- **Layer thickness:** 16–32 µm (much finer than FDM at 100–300 µm).
- **Droplet size:** 5–80 picolitres; diameter 20–50 µm.
- **Materials:** Acrylic photopolymers, elastomers, castable wax, biocompatible resins, high-temperature resins.
- **Support material:** Wax-based or gel-based; jetted simultaneously; removed by water-jet, heat, or hand.
- **Build envelope (typical ProJet 3600):** 298 × 185 × 203 mm.
- **Dimensional accuracy:** ±0.025 mm.
- **Surface finish:** $R_a$ ≈ 4–8 µm.
- **Key advantage:** Multi-material and full-colour capability.
- **Key disadvantage:** Limited to photopolymers; high running cost; part brittleness and UV-degradation over time.
- **Major applications:** Jewellery investment casting patterns, dental and surgical models, transparent prototypes, soft-touch consumer product prototypes, optical components, anatomical teaching models.
- **NOT the same as:** Vat Photopolymerization (SLA/DLP) — no vat, no laser, no full-layer projection.
- **NOT the same as:** PolyJet (Stratasys) — different vendor, different resin system, different support.
- **NOT the same as:** Binder Jetting — MJM uses photopolymer that *cures*; BJ uses powder that is *glued*.
- **Key formulas to memorize:** $We = \frac{\rho v^2 D_0}{\sigma}$, $D_{\text{spread}} = D_0 \sqrt{\frac{We+3}{3}}$, $T_{\text{build}} = \frac{H}{t_L} \times T_{\text{layer}}$, $\nu_e = \frac{\rho_p}{2 M_c}$, $R_a \approx 0.5 t_L$.
- **KTU-favourite line of questioning:** "Compare MJM with SLA" OR "Explain the MJM process with a block diagram and list four applications" OR "A part of height H mm is built at layer thickness $t_L$ mm — calculate build time."
- **One-liner for viva:** *"MJM is the inkjet-printer-of-3D-printing, jetting photopolymer droplets that are instantly UV-cured to produce ultra-high-resolution parts with multi-material capability."*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications and Case Studies of Additive Manufacturing: Biomedical, Manufacturing, Aerospace, Automotive, Food and Electronics Domains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET525 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING AND 3D PRINTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applications and Case Studies of Additive Manufacturing: Biomedical, Manufacturing, Aerospace, Automotive, Food and Electronics Domains # MODULE 4: APPLICATIONS AND CASE STUDIES OF ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Additive Manufacturing (AM) Applications** refer to the domain-specific deployment of layer-by-layer fabrication technologies to manufacture functional end-use parts, prototypes, tooling, and biomedically-relevant structures across six high-impact industrial sectors: **Biomedical, General Manufacturing, Aerospace, Automotive, Food Processing, and Electronics**. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically categorizes AM applications based on: - **Functional requirement** (structural, biocompatible, edible, conductive, aerodynamic) - **Process compatibility** (FDM, SLA, SLS, DMLS, SLM, PolyJet, Binder Jetting) - **Material domain** (polymers, metals, ceramics, biomaterials, food pastes, conductive inks) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Domain-Specific Digital Craftsman" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a master craftsman who owns six specialized workshops, each equipped with different tools for different jobs. In one workshop, he sculpts bone scaffolds for hospitals; in another, he forges lightweight turbine blades for jet engines; in another, he pipes chocolate into intricate dessert shapes. Additive Manufacturing is that craftsman — the same fundamental "layer-by-layer" principle adapts its tools (process) and raw materials (feedstock) to serve radically different industries. ### 1.3 The Six Application Domains — Quick Overview | Domain | Primary AM Process | Typical Material | Key Functional Need | |---|---|---|---| | **Biomedical** | SLA, SLS, DMLS, Bioplotting | Ti6Al4V, PEEK, PLA, Hydrogels | Biocompatibility, Patient-specific geometry | | **General Manufacturing** | FDM, SLS, MJ | ABS, Nylon, PETG | Rapid prototyping, low-volume tooling | | **Aerospace** | SLM, DMLS, EBM | Inconel 718, TiAl6V4, AlSi10Mg | High strength-to-weight ratio, topology optimization | | **Automotive** | FDM, SLS, MJ | Nylon (PA12), Carbon-fiber composites | Lightweighting, custom jigs & fixtures | | **Food** | Binder Jetting, FDM (modified), Extrusion | Chocolate, Sugar paste, Dough | Edibility, complex geometry, customization | | **Electronics** | Material Extrusion, AJP, Hybrid | Conductive inks, Photopolymers, Cu | Embedded circuits, antennas, dielectric structures | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Term:** The phrase *"end-use functional parts vs. prototyping"* is a frequent 14-mark exam differentiator. Prototyping (visual mock-ups) is dominated by FDM/SLA, while **end-use functional parts** in aerospace/medical require SLM/DMLS/SLS with qualified materials. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Domain-Wise Strategic Rationale ("Why AM Here?") ### 2.1.1 Biomedical Domain - **Why AM?** Patient-specific geometry (one-of-a-kind skulls, dental crowns, hearing-aid shells) is virtually impossible with traditional CNC/molding. - **Core drivers:** Customization, biocompatibility, controlled porosity (for bone ingrowth). - **Key Metrics:** - Pore size: **200–500 µm** (for osteoblast cell migration) - Surface roughness Ra: **< 32 µm** (osseointegration threshold) - Material biocompatibility index: **ISO 10993** compliance - Patient scan resolution: **CT slice ≤ 0.5 mm** ### 2.1.2 General Manufacturing Domain - **Why AM?** Short lead time for jigs, fixtures, and low-volume production runs. - **Core drivers:** Tooling cost elimination, design iteration speed, supply-chain simplification. - **Key Metrics:** - Lead time reduction: **60–90%** vs. traditional tooling - Part consolidation: **5–20 components → 1 part** - First-article cost saving: **30–70%** (low volume) ### 2.1.3 Aerospace Domain - **Why AM?** Topology optimization can reduce part mass by **30–60%** while maintaining strength — directly reducing fuel burn. - **Core drivers:** Weight reduction, part consolidation, buy-to-fly ratio improvement. - **Key Metrics:** - Buy-to-fly ratio (AM): **1:1 to 5:1** vs. traditional **10:1 to 20:1** - Part weight reduction: **30–60%** - Material utilization: **≥ 95%** (near-net shape) - Qualification standard: **AMS 7000 series, ASTM F3301** ### 2.1.4 Automotive Domain - **Why AM?** Rapid prototyping of body panels, dashboards, and end-use parts for low-volume EVs/hyper-cars. - **Core drivers:** Mass production of customized parts, ergonomic grips, lightweight lattice structures. - **Key Metrics:** - Cycle time (DLS): **< 30 minutes/part** - Customized grips/seats lead time: **days vs. months** ### 2.1.5 Food Domain - **Why AM?** Personalized nutrition, complex sugar/chocolate geometries, novel food textures. - **Core drivers:** Customization, food safety, novel aesthetics. - **Key Metrics:** - Print temperature: **30–45 °C** (chocolate), **60–80 °C** (dough) - Layer height: **0.4–1.0 mm** - Post-processing: **cooling tunnel, no chemical curing** ### 2.1.6 Electronics Domain - **Why AM?** Embedded antennas, 3D-printed circuits, dielectric waveguides, conformal sensors. - **Core drivers:** Geometric freedom, multi-material printing, RF performance. - **Key Metrics:** - Conductor resolution: **~50 µm line width** (AJP) - Substrate dielectric constant: **2.5–3.5** (typical photopolymer) - Operating frequency: **up to 110 GHz** (3D-printed waveguides) ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Domain | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $R_{a} = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} \vert z(x) \vert \, dx$ | Surface roughness; $L$=evaluation length, $z(x)$=height deviation | Biomedical | µm | | 2 | $\text{BTF} = \dfrac{m_{\text{raw stock}}}{m_{\text{final part}}}$ | Buy-to-Fly ratio | Aerospace | dimensionless | | 3 | $P_{r} = \dfrac{m_{\text{conventiona}} - The student should remember that **Buy-to-Fly ratio (BTF)** is the most frequent 14-mark exam metric for aerospace questions in KTU 2024. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility Summary - **Biomedical:** Reduces surgery time (pre-planned implants fit on first attempt). - **Manufacturing:** Enables *agile product development* with on-demand spares. - **Aerospace:** GE LEAP fuel nozzle — **20 parts consolidated into 1**, **25% lighter**. - **Automotive:** Bugatti's 3D-printed brake caliper (Ti6Al4V, SLM) — world's largest functional titanium AM auto part. - **Food:** 3D Systems' Chef Jet — sugar & chocolate printing for confectionery. - **Electronics:** Optomec Aerosol Jet Printing (AJP) of 5G antennas on conformal surfaces. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Buy-to-Fly (BTF) Ratio Improvement in Aerospace The Buy-to-Fly ratio is the canonical KTU 2024 metric for justifying AM in aerospace. Let us derive its improvement quantitatively. ### Step 1 — Define BTF $$ \text{BTF} = \frac{m_{\text{raw stock}}}{m_{\text{final part}}} $$ *This is the mass ratio of starting material to finished part. A BTF of 1 is ideal (no waste).* ### Step 2 — Conventional Machining Case For a turbine blade machined from a forged billet: $$ m_{\text{billet}} = 45.0 \text{ kg}, \quad m_{\text{final blade}} = 2.0 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \Rightarrow \text{BTF}_{\text{conv}} = \frac{45.0}{2.0} = 22.5 $$ *Logic:* **22.5 kg of titanium is machined away** to leave a 2 kg blade. ### Step 3 — AM (SLM) Case The same blade printed in Inconel 718 with optimized lattice: $$ m_{\text{powder used}} = 2.4 \text{ kg}, \quad m_{\text{final blade (lattice)}} = 1.3 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \Rightarrow \text{BTF}_{\text{AM}} = \frac{2.4}{1.3} \approx 1.85 $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Material Saving $$ \Delta m = 45.0 - 2.4 = 42.6 \text{ kg} \text{ saved} $$ $$ \text{Saving \%} = \frac{42.6}{45.0} \times 100\% = 94.67\% $$ ### Step 5 — Compute Cost Saving (with assumed costs) Let $C_{\text{Ti billet}} = \$ 250/\text{kg}$ and $C_{\text{AM powder}} = \$ 400/\text{kg}$: $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{conv}} &= 45.0 \times 250 = \$ 11{,}250 \\ C_{\text{AM}} &= 2.4 \times 400 = \$ 960 \\ \text{Cost saving} &= 11{,}250 - 960 = \$ 10{,}290 \\ \text{Saving \%} &= \frac{10{,}290}{11{,}250} \times 100\% \approx 91.5\% \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Final Mass Reduction from Topology Optimization $$ P_{r} = \frac{m_{\text{conv}} - m_{\text{AM}}}{m_{\text{conv}}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.0 - 1.3}{2.0} \times 100\% = 35\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** BTF improvement + topology optimization jointly yield **~91.5% cost saving** AND **35% weight reduction**. This is the standard KTU answer framework for "Why AM in aerospace?" — always quote **two metrics** (cost + weight). --- ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Domain × Process × Material | Domain | AM Process | Material | Strength (MPa) | Density (g/cm³) | Surface Finish Ra (µm) | KTU Hot Question | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Biomedical (implant) | SLM / DMLS | Ti6Al4V | **895–1050** | 4.43 | 5–20 | "Justify SLM for orthopedic implant" | | Biomedical (scaffold) | Bioplotting | Hydrogel + PCL | 0.5–5 (scaffold) | 1.1 | n/a | "Explain pore-size requirement" | | Aerospace (bracket) | SLM | Ti6Al4V | 950 | 4.43 | 6–15 | "Compute BTF improvement" | | Aerospace (nozzle) | DMLS | Inconel 718 | 1100 | 8.19 | 8–12 | "GE LEAP nozzle case" | | Automotive (grip) | MJ (PolyJet) | Photopolymer | 50–70 | 1.18 | 1–2 | "Multi-material grip design" | | Food (chocolate) | Extrusion | Cocoa butter | n/a (texture) | 1.32 | 50–200 | "Food-safe process selection" | | Electronics (antenna) | AJP | Silver ink | conductivity $6 \times 10^{7}$ S/m | n/a | n/a | "Why AJP for 5G?" | --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Layer-Resolution Trade-off (Universal) For FDM/SLA-based processes in any domain, the build time trade-off is: $$ t_{\text{layer}} = \frac{h}{v_{\text{dep}}} $$ where $h$ is layer height and $v_{\text{dep}}$ is deposition speed (m/s). Total build time: $$ T_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{layers}} \cdot t_{\text{layer}} = \frac{H}{h} \cdot \frac{h}{v_{\text{dep}}} = \frac{H}{v_{\text{dep}}} $$ > Wait — let us re-derive properly (correcting the layer-time expression): $$ t_{\text{layer}} = \frac{L_{\text{path}}}{v_{\text{dep}}} $$ where $L_{\text{path}}$ is total path length per layer. $$ T_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{layers}} \cdot t_{\text{layer}} = \frac{H}{h} \cdot \frac{L_{\text{path, avg}}}{v_{\text{dep}}} $$ *Logic:* **Smaller layer height $h$** → more layers → longer build time, but better surface finish. KTU frequently tests this as a 3-mark "trade-off" question. --- ## 3.4 Case Study: GE LEAP Fuel Nozzle (Aerospace) | Step | Action | Quantitative Result | |---|---|---| | 1 | Identify part to consolidate | Fuel nozzle = **18 parts** traditionally | | 2 | Apply DMLS with CoCr alloy | One-piece print | | 3 | Consolidate to 1 part | **18 → 1 = 94% part reduction** | | 4 | Measure weight | **25% lighter** | | 5 | Measure durability | **5× more durable** (life-cycle) | | 6 | Annual production volume | **~30,000 nozzles/year** | $$ \text{Part Reduction \%} = \frac{18 - 1}{18} \times 100\% = 94.4\% $$ $$ \text{Weight Reduction \%} = \frac{m_{\text{conv}} - m_{\text{AM}}}{m_{\text{conv}}} \times 100\% = 25\% $$ # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **List any three major industrial domains where Additive Manufacturing has significant applications. For each, name one specific AM process and one suitable material.** **Model Answer:** | Domain | AM Process | Material | |---|---|---| | Biomedical (orthopedic implants) | **SLM / DMLS** | **Ti6Al4V** | | Aerospace (turbine brackets) | **DMLS** | **Inconel 718** | | Food (confectionery) | **Material Extrusion** | **Chocolate / Sugar paste** | *(Any 3 from biomedical, aerospace, automotive, manufacturing, food, electronics accepted with matching process+material.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **What is the Buy-to-Fly (BTF) ratio? Why is it an important metric for justifying AM in the aerospace industry?** **Model Answer:** The **Buy-to-Fly (BTF) ratio** is defined as the ratio of the mass of raw stock material to the mass of the final functional part: $$ \text{BTF} = \frac{m_{\text{raw stock}}}{m_{\text{final part}}} $$ **Significance in aerospace:** - **Traditional machining** of a titanium turbine blade from a billet can yield BTF ≈ **15:1 to 25:1**, meaning **>90% material is wasted as chips**. - **AM (SLM/DMLS)** reduces BTF to near **1:1 – 5:1** because powder is deposited only where needed. - Lower BTF → **less material cost, less machining time, less scrap, lower buy-to-fly inventory**, directly translating to cost savings and weight reduction — both critical in aerospace economics. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** **(a)** Explain in detail the applications of Additive Manufacturing in the **biomedical domain**. Discuss the relevant materials, processes, and give **two specific case studies** with justification. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A titanium orthopedic implant is traditionally machined from a 35 kg Ti6Al4V billet to produce a final part weighing 1.8 kg. Using AM (SLM), only 2.2 kg of powder is consumed to produce the same implant weighing 1.5 kg after topology optimization. Compute: (i) The BTF ratio for both processes (ii) The mass saving percentage (iii) The weight reduction percentage (iv) Comment on the suitability of AM for this application. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Biomedical Applications of AM (7 marks):** 1. **Patient-Specific Implants:** - **Cranial implants:** Custom skull plates from CT scan data, printed in Ti6Al4V via SLM. Case: *Oxford Performance Materials' PEEK skull implant (2013, USA)*. - **Dental crowns & bridges:** CoCr alloy printed via DMLS, eliminating manual wax-up. 2. **Surgical Guides & Models:** - **SLA-printed anatomical models** for pre-surgical planning reduce operation time by ~25–40%. - **PolyJet surgical guides** with multi-material (rigid + soft tissue simulants) for rehearsal. 3. **Tissue Engineering Scaffolds:** - **Bioplotting / Extrusion** of hydrogels (alginate, gelatin) and PCL into porous scaffolds with pore size **200–500 µm** for osteoblast migration. - *Case:* Wake Forest Institute — printed bladders and skin grafts transplanted into patients. 4. **Prosthetics & Orthotics:** - **SLS Nylon (PA12)** for custom prosthetic sockets — lighter and more comfortable than traditional laminated sockets. 5. **Hearing Aids & Insoles:** - SLA hearing-aid shells produced in batches of thousands; each custom-molded to patient's ear canal. **Key Materials:** Ti6Al4V, CoCr, PEEK, PLA, PCL, hydrogels, photopolymers. **Key Processes:** SLM, DMLS, SLA, SLS, MJ, Bioplotting. --- **(b) Quantitative Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $m_{\text{billet}} = 35$ kg, $m_{\text{conv part}} = 1.8$ kg - $m_{\text{AM powder}} = 2.2$ kg, $m_{\text{AM part}} = 1.5$ kg **(i) BTF Ratios:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{BTF}_{\text{conv}} &= \frac{35.0}{1.8} = 19.44 \\ \text{BTF}_{\text{AM}} &= \frac{2.2}{1.5} = 1.47 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Mass Saving Percentage:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta m_{\text{raw}} &= 35.0 - 2.2 = 32.8 \text{ kg} \\ \text{Mass saving \%} &= \frac{32.8}{35.0} \times 100\% = 93.71\% \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Weight Reduction of Final Part:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{r} &= \frac{m_{\text{conv part}} - m_{\text{AM part}}}{m_{\text{conv part}}} \times 100\% \\ P_{r} &= \frac{1.8 - 1.5}{1.8} \times 100\% = 16.67\% \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Suitability Comment:** AM is **highly suitable** for this orthopedic implant because: - BTF drops from **19.44 to 1.47** (≈ 92% reduction in raw material use). - Final implant is **~17% lighter**, beneficial for patient comfort and load-bearing biomechanics. - **Patient-specific geometry** from CT scan enables a perfect anatomical fit, impossible with mass-machined implants. - Combined benefits: material economy, customization, weight reduction, and reduced surgery time. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Discuss the role of Additive Manufacturing in the **aerospace and automotive domains**. Compare the design freedoms, weight benefits, and qualification challenges in both domains. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of the **GE LEAP fuel nozzle** case study, compute: (i) The percentage part consolidation (ii) The percentage weight reduction (assume conventional part = 500 g, AM part = 375 g) (iii) The complexity factor (CF) (iv) Explain why topology optimization is a key enabler for AM in aerospace. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution:** **(a) AM in Aerospace vs. Automotive (7 marks):** | Aspect | Aerospace | Automotive | |---|---|---| | **Design freedom** | Topology-optimized lattice structures (mandatory) | Styling prototypes, ergonomic grips | | **Weight benefit** | **30–60%** reduction (fuel economy) | **10–25%** reduction (range for EVs) | | **Material** | Inconel 718, Ti6Al4V, AlSi10Mg | PA12, CF-Nylon, photopolymers | | **Process** | SLM, DMLS, EBM | SLS, FDM, MJ | | **Qualification** | **AMS 7000 / ASTM F3301** — strict, multi-year | ISO/TS 16949 — less stringent | | **Volume** | Low (hundreds–thousands/year) | Higher (especially hyper-cars) | | **Key case** | GE LEAP nozzle | Bugatti Ti brake caliper | **Similarity:** Both demand lightweighting and part consolidation; both use topology optimization. **Difference:** Aerospace requires higher safety margins, traceability, and certification. --- **(b) GE LEAP Case Study Computation (7 marks):** **Given:** - Conventional nozzle = **18 parts**, weight = **500 g** - AM nozzle = **1 part**, weight = **375 g** **(i) Part Consolidation Percentage:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Consolidation \%} &= \frac{N_{\text{orig}} - N_{\text{consol}}}{N_{\text{orig}}} \times 100\% \\ &= \frac{18 - 1}{18} \times 100\% \\ &= \frac{17}{18} \times 100\% \\ &\approx 94.44\% \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Weight Reduction Percentage:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{r} &= \frac{500 - 375}{500} \times 100\% \\ &= \frac{125}{500} \times 100\% \\ &= 25\% \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Complexity Factor:** $$ \begin{aligned} CF &= N_{\text{orig}} - N_{\text{consol}} \\ &= 18 - 1 \\ &= 17 \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Topology Optimization as Key Enabler (full 7-mark treatment depends on rubric):** Topology optimization is a **computational design method** that removes material from low-stress regions of a part, distributing material only along load paths. It is a **key enabler for AM in aerospace** because: 1. **AM uniquely can produce** the resulting organic, lattice, organic-shaped geometries — conventional methods (casting, forging, machining) cannot. 2. **Weight savings of 30–60%** are achievable without compromising structural integrity, directly translating to fuel savings. 3. **Part consolidation** — multiple sub-components (brackets, ribs) become a single optimized part, eliminating fasteners, joints, and assembly errors. 4. **Performance improvement** — optimized flow paths in nozzles (like LEAP) improve aerodynamic and combustion efficiency by **15%+**. 5. **Buy-to-Fly ratio** approaches 1:1, dramatically reducing material cost for expensive aerospace alloys. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Confusing BTF with material utilization %:** BTF = $m_{\text{raw}}/m_{\text{final}}$; utilization = $m_{\text{final}}/m_{\text{raw}} \times 100\%$. They are reciprocals. Do NOT interchange them in 7-mark solutions. > 2. **Wrong unit for porosity:** Pore size is in **µm** (micrometers), not mm. Writing "200–500 mm" loses 1 mark. > 3. **Forgetting process-material pairing:** A 14-mark aerospace question ALWAYS expects you to name both the **process** (e.g., DMLS) and **material** (e.g., Inconel 718) for full credit. > 4. **Writing "FDM" for metal aerospace parts:** FDM is for polymers. Aerospace metal parts use **SLM / DMLS / EBM**. This single error can cost 2 marks. > 5. **Food AM with non-food-safe materials:** Never use ABS/PLA in food context unless certified food-safe. Use chocolate, sugar, dough, or food-grade polymers. > 6. **Missing ISO/AMS standards:** Always cite **ISO 10993 (biomedical biocompatibility)** and **AMS 7000 / ASTM F3301 (aerospace qualification)** for full 14-mark credit. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### ✅ Rapid Revision Checklist - [ ] **Six major AM domains** = Biomedical, Manufacturing, Aerospace, Automotive, Food, Electronics. - [ ] **Biomedical primary process** = SLM (Ti6Al4V) for implants; SLA for surgical guides; Bioplotting for scaffolds. - [ ] **Pore size for bone scaffolds** = **200–500 µm**; biocompatibility standard = **ISO 10993**. - [ ] **Aerospace flagship process** = SLM / DMLS with **Inconel 718** or **Ti6Al4V**; **AMS 7000** qualification. - [ ] **GE LEAP nozzle** = **18 parts → 1 part** (94.4% consolidation), **25% lighter**, **5× more durable**. - [ ] **BTF ratio**: Conventional ≈ 10–25; AM ≈ 1–5. Lower is better. - [ ] **Material utilization** of AM ≥ **95%** (for powder-bed processes). - [ ] **Automotive flagship case** = Bugatti 3D-printed Ti6Al4V brake caliper (largest functional Ti AM auto part). - [ ] **Food AM processes** = Extrusion (chocolate, dough), Binder Jetting (sugar). Print temperature **30–80 °C**; **NO toxic curing**. - [ ] **Electronics flagship process** = **AJP (Aerosol Jet Printing)** for conformal 5G antennas; conductor resolution ~**50 µm**; substrate dielectric εᵣ ≈ 2.5–3.5. - [ ] **Weight reduction** of AM aerospace parts: **30–60%**; automotive: **10–25%**. - [ ] **Topology optimization** = the design technique that *enables* AM's geometric advantage; cannot be realized by traditional methods. - [ ] **Part consolidation** (CF) is a 14-mark KTU favorite — always quantify it as percentage. - [ ] **Material-Process-Purpose triad** must be remembered for every domain — examiners expect all three in 7-mark answers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** In every 14-mark question, structure your answer as **(1) Process selection, (2) Material selection, (3) Quantitative metric, (4) Case study/example, (5) Justification**. This 5-point structure mirrors the board examiner's rubric and guarantees ≥ 12/14 marks if content is correct.
